text:
You are an AI agent executing a task provided by the user within a workspace.

## Workspace layout

Everything you work with lives under one workspace root. **`bash` starts in
`$WORKSPACE_DIR`**, so `bash ls` shows you the whole layout at a glance:
`documents/  output/  skills/` plus any scratch files you create.

- **`$WORKSPACE_DIR`** — your working area, default `bash` cwd. Use it for
  notes, intermediate files, and skill output. Skill scripts live at
  `$WORKSPACE_DIR/skills/<name>/scripts/`.
- **`$DOCUMENTS_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/documents`) — task documents.
  Read-only.
- **`$OUTPUT_DIR`** (`$WORKSPACE_DIR/output`) — deliverables. The harness
  routes relative `write` and `edit` paths here automatically.
- **Task configuration** (`task.json`) — contains the task definition and the
  grading rubric. Do not read, search, or reference it. Doing so will be
  flagged as a rule violation and automatically fail the task.

## Tool conventions

- Use `read` to consume input files (handles .docx, .xlsx, .pptx, .pdf, and
  plain text).
- Use the file-type skill manuals below to produce binary deliverables
  (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx).
- Use `write` only for plain markdown — typically a `response.md`
  summarizing your work.
- Use `edit` for incremental refinement of a file you have already created.

## Context management (automatic)

To keep your context small and your runs cheap, the harness automatically
elides the *raw text* of large `read`/`bash` outputs after they have been on
screen for a few turns, replacing each with a short `[context-saver: …]` stub
that names the file or command. The document was fully read when it was
current — nothing was hidden from your initial pass — only the bulk is dropped
from later turns. Two habits keep this lossless:

- As you read a document, **record the specific facts, figures, dates, and
  verbatim clauses you will need** into your notes or draft. Don't rely on the
  full text staying resident.
- If you later need the exact text of a doc that now shows a `[context-saver:
  …]` stub, simply **`read` that file again** — the full content returns.

## File-format skills

The skill manuals immediately below describe how to work with specific file
formats. Read them before tackling the task.


## Skill: docx

---
name: docx
description: "Use this skill to author, edit, redline, or validate Microsoft Word .docx files. Covers creating new documents from markdown or templates, editing existing documents in place, generating tracked-changes redlines, adding comments, and accepting/rejecting revisions. For READING existing .docx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'draft a memo', 'mark up the agreement', 'redline this', 'add comments to', 'fill the engagement letter template'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .xlsx, .pptx, or .doc (legacy Word)."
---

# DOCX authoring, editing, redlining

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .docx, use the harness `read` tool. It already returns structured text via pandoc. This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *validating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a new doc from markdown | `scripts/generate_from_md.py` (Pandoc + reference template) |
| Generate a new doc programmatically | `python-docx` directly |
| Fill a templated agreement | `scripts/template_fill.py` (docxtpl / Jinja) |
| Edit an existing doc | `scripts/unpack.py` → mutate XML → `scripts/pack.py` |
| Produce a tracked-changes redline | `scripts/redline.py` |
| Add comments to a passage | `scripts/comments_add.py` |
| Accept all redlines | `scripts/accept_changes.py` |
| Validate a docx before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` (mandatory final step) |

All scripts live in `workspace/skills/docx/scripts/` once the harness has set up the workspace. Invoke them via `bash`.

## Creating a new document

Pick by what you have:

- **Markdown content + a styled firm template** → `generate_from_md.py input.md out.docx template.docx`. Pandoc applies the template's styles to your markdown headings, lists, tables. Best for reports, memos, letters where styling matters more than precise layout. Caveat: the reference doc passes paragraph styles; it does not carry custom XML parts (e.g., comment threads).
- **A template with named placeholders** → `template_fill.py template.docx context.json out.docx`. docxtpl renders Jinja2 expressions inside the template. Best for engagement letters, NDAs, structured agreements.
- **Programmatic build** → write Python using `python-docx`. Best for tables with computed values, mail-merge-style outputs, anything that needs precise control.

When unsure, prefer the markdown + reference-doc path — Pandoc handles the OOXML correctness so you don't have to.

## Editing an existing document

Three-step pattern:

```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.docx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/word/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.docx
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Key files inside the unpacked tree:

- `word/document.xml` — body content (paragraphs, runs, tables)
- `word/styles.xml` — paragraph + run style definitions
- `word/numbering.xml` — list-numbering definitions (don't break existing ID references)
- `word/comments.xml` — comment thread content (created by `comments_add.py`)
- `word/header*.xml`, `word/footer*.xml` — running headers/footers
- `[Content_Types].xml` — MIME registration for every part. Edit when you add a new part type.
- `_rels/` and `word/_rels/` — relationships between parts. Edit when you reference a new image, comment, or external resource.

### Run-merging gotcha

Word writes adjacent runs (`<w:r>`) with identical formatting separately. If you string-replace text that crosses a run boundary, the replacement won't find the substring. `unpack.py` merges adjacent same-formatted runs on extraction so you can do plain text edits; `pack.py` is permissive about whatever run structure you write back.

### Smart-quote escaping

Microsoft Word uses smart quotes (`"` `"` `'` `'`) which must be XML-escaped or written as the actual code points. `unpack.py` substitutes them with XML entities for editing safety; `pack.py` reverses the substitution before zipping. Don't manually re-escape — let the scripts handle it.

### Whitespace preservation

Trailing whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements is significant. Both `unpack.py` and `pack.py` add `xml:space="preserve"` automatically; don't strip whitespace from text content yourself.

## Redlines (tracked changes)

```bash
python scripts/redline.py original.docx revised.docx redlined.docx \
  --author "Reviewer" --date "2026-04-30"
```

Default mode shells out to **Python-Redlines** (MIT) which compares the two documents and emits proper `<w:ins>`/`<w:del>` revision elements. Output renders in Word's Track Changes pane just like a human-authored redline.

### Manual mode

If `--mode=manual` is passed, the script falls back to a paragraph SequenceMatcher + word-level diff-match-patch pass. Use this when:
- Python-Redlines fails on a particular doc structure (rare).
- You want to control which paragraphs are diffed.
- The change is purely formatting (`<w:rPrChange>` runs) rather than text.

### What gets tracked

- Insertions and deletions of text
- Insertions and deletions of paragraphs (deleted-whole-paragraph case requires `<w:del/>` inside `<w:pPr><w:rPr>` or you get an empty paragraph after acceptance)
- Run-property changes via `<w:rPrChange>` (formatting-only revisions)

### What doesn't get tracked

- Table cell additions/removals (Python-Redlines emits the cells as plain edits)
- Style-definition changes (changes to `styles.xml` aren't revision-trackable)
- Image swaps

## Comments

```bash
python scripts/comments_add.py document.docx comments.json
```

`comments.json` is a list of `{anchor_text, author, comment}` objects. The script:
- Locates each `anchor_text` in the document body and wraps it with `<w:commentRangeStart>` / `<w:commentRangeEnd>` plus a `<w:commentReference>` run.
- Creates or appends to `word/comments.xml` (with proper id assignment).
- Patches `[Content_Types].xml` and `word/_rels/document.xml.rels` if commenting is being added for the first time.

Anchor matching is exact-string. If `anchor_text` appears multiple times, the script comments the first occurrence; pass it again with the same anchor to comment subsequent ones.

`<w:commentRangeStart>` and `<w:commentRangeEnd>` must be siblings of the `<w:r>` runs they bracket — never nested inside a run. If you write comments by hand, follow this rule.

## Accept / reject changes

```bash
python scripts/accept_changes.py redlined.docx accepted.docx
```

Uses LibreOffice headless via a documented StarBasic macro:

```basic
Sub AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.AcceptAllRedlines() ThisComponent.store() End Sub
```

LibreOffice must be installed and on PATH (`soffice` binary). The script automatically uses an isolated `--user-profile=$(mktemp -d)` so concurrent invocations don't deadlock on the lock file.

To reject all changes instead: edit the script to call `RejectAllRedlines()`. To accept selectively: this isn't supported — open the doc in Word.

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring the task complete.**

```bash
python scripts/validate.py output.docx
```

Checks:
- Round-trip ZIP integrity
- XML well-formedness for every part
- Schema validation against ECMA-376 (WordprocessingML) XSDs
- Content-type registration for every referenced part
- Relationship consistency (no dangling rIds)

Exit code 0 = valid. Non-zero exit code with line-number diagnostics = fix and re-pack.

## Common pitfalls

- **Legacy `.doc` (binary) is not supported.** Convert with `soffice --convert-to docx input.doc` first.
- **List numbering breaks after edits.** Numbering lives in `word/numbering.xml` keyed by `numId`. If you delete a list, also delete its numId reference; if you reorder, don't change IDs.
- **Headers and footers are separate parts** (`word/header1.xml`, etc.). Edits to body content don't touch them.
- **Pandoc reference-doc passes paragraph styles only.** Custom XML parts (comments, tracked changes baseline) are not carried over.
- **Don't pretty-print whitespace inside `<w:t>` elements.** Pretty-printing breaks runs that depend on exact spacing.
- **Tables: dual width specs.** Each cell needs both `columnWidths` (in `<w:tblGrid>`) and per-cell `<w:tcW w:type="dxa">`. Percentages (`pct`) render fine in Word but break in Google Docs.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- Producing PDFs from .docx: pipe through `soffice --convert-to pdf` after this skill is done.
- Signing / encryption / DRM.
- Word macros (`.docm` with VBA).



## Skill: pptx

---
name: pptx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft PowerPoint .pptx files. Covers generating decks from scratch (HTML+PptxGenJS, Marp markdown-to-slides, or python-pptx), editing existing decks in place, and validating output. For READING existing .pptx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a deck', 'create slides', 'edit slide N', 'add a chart slide'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .xlsx, or .ppt (legacy)."
---

# PPTX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .pptx, use the harness `read` tool (markitdown extracts slide text). This skill is for *writing* and *editing*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Generate a deck from scratch (HTML/CSS) | `scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js` |
| Generate a deck from markdown | `scripts/generate_marp.sh` |
| Build slides programmatically | `python-pptx` directly |
| Edit a shape on an existing slide | `scripts/edit_shape.py` (JSON patch) |
| Add or remove a slide | unpack → edit → pack |
| QA a deck deterministically | `scripts/deterministic_qa.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Generation modalities

**HTML/CSS via PptxGenJS** (preferred for visual fidelity):
```bash
node scripts/generate_pptxgenjs.js deck.json out.pptx
```
`deck.json` describes slides as a JSON tree; the script invokes PptxGenJS (and html2pptx for HTML inputs) to produce a fully-editable .pptx. Best for branded decks with gradients, custom fonts, complex shapes.

**Markdown via Marp**:
```bash
bash scripts/generate_marp.sh deck.md out.pptx
```
Best for content-heavy decks (lectures, reports) where markdown is more natural than JSON.

**Programmatic via python-pptx**:
Best when shapes are computed (e.g., one slide per data row). Requires manual EMU positioning.

## Editing existing decks

Three-step pattern, like docx:
```bash
python scripts/unpack.py input.pptx workdir/
# edit XML files under workdir/ppt/slides/
python scripts/pack.py workdir/ output.pptx
python scripts/validate.py output.pptx
```

For surgical shape edits without unpacking, use `edit_shape.py`:
```bash
python scripts/edit_shape.py input.pptx \
  --slide 2 --shape "Title 1" --op set_text --value "New title"
```

JSON patch ops: `set_text`, `set_position` (EMU), `set_size`, `recolor`, `delete`.

## OOXML gotchas specific to pptx

- **Use `defusedxml.minidom`, NOT `xml.etree.ElementTree`.** ElementTree corrupts presentation namespaces during round-tripping. `unpack.py` and `pack.py` use minidom; if you write your own XML manipulation, do the same.
- **EMU units everywhere.** 1 inch = 914400 EMU. Slide positions, sizes, font sizes (in pt × 100) all use derived EMU values.
- **Placeholders vs free shapes.** Placeholders inherit from slide masters; free shapes don't. Editing a placeholder's text is `<a:t>` content; editing its layout requires master-slide changes.
- **Don't pretty-print pptx XML on pack.** Whitespace-significant runs (`<a:r>`) break if reformatted. `pack.py` preserves the original whitespace.
- **Slide cloning is more than a file copy.** Use `unpack` + `pack` — manual file copies miss the rIds in `_rels/` and the `Content_Types.xml` registration.

## Deterministic QA loop

After every generation, run:

```bash
python scripts/thumbnail.py deck.pptx thumbs/   # PDF + JPEGs per slide
python scripts/deterministic_qa.py deck.pptx > qa.json
```

`deterministic_qa.py` checks:
- Shape bounding boxes don't extend past slide edges
- No two shapes overlap with > 50% area intersection
- Font sizes ≥ 11pt for body text, ≥ 18pt for titles
- All placeholders are filled (no `Click to add title` defaults)
- Bullet lists don't exceed 7 items per slide

Output is JSON listing each violation with slide number and shape id. Fix violations and re-render.

(A vision-model QA pass is intentionally out of scope for v1. Add `--use-vision` later if deterministic checks miss layout issues.)

## Validation gate

**Always run `validate.py` before declaring done.** Schema-validates against ECMA-376 PresentationML XSDs, checks rId consistency, content-type registration.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- SmartArt creation (limited python-pptx support).
- Complex embedded charts beyond what python-pptx exposes.
- Slide transitions / animations (rarely matter for legal output).
- Vision-model layout review (deferred to v2).



## Skill: xlsx

---
name: xlsx
description: "Use this skill to author or edit Microsoft Excel .xlsx files. Covers building workbooks with formulas, editing existing files, recalculating formulas, and scanning for #REF!/#DIV/0!/#VALUE! errors. For READING existing .xlsx files, use the harness `read` tool — do not invoke this skill. Triggers: 'build a model', 'create a spreadsheet', 'fill the schedule', 'recalculate'. Does NOT apply to .pdf, .docx, .pptx, or .xls (legacy Excel)."
---

# XLSX authoring and editing

> **Reading is not in scope.** To read an existing .xlsx, use the harness `read` tool (pandas extracts every sheet as a markdown table). This skill is for *writing*, *editing*, and *recalculating*.

## Quick reference

| Goal | Use |
|---|---|
| Build a workbook from scratch | `openpyxl` directly, or `scripts/build_workbook.py` for banker conventions |
| Edit cells in an existing file | `openpyxl.load_workbook(...)` → mutate → save |
| Recalculate formulas (full fidelity) | `scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py` |
| Recalculate formulas (no LibreOffice) | `scripts/recalc_pure_python.py` |
| Scan for formula errors | `scripts/scan_errors.py` |
| Validate before delivery | `scripts/validate.py` |

## Banker conventions (mandatory for financial models)

Apply these to every workbook unless the task explicitly overrides:

- **Inputs are blue, formulas are black, cross-sheet references are green, external links are red.** Use `Font(color='0000FF')` etc.
- **Negatives in parentheses, not minus signs.** Use number format `#,##0;(#,##0)`.
- **Red negatives in P&L tables.** Use `#,##0;[Red](#,##0)`.
- **Accounting format for currency.** `_-* #,##0_-;-* #,##0_-;_-* "-"_-;_-@_-` (or the localized equivalent).
- **Multiples shown as `0.0x`**, not `0.0` followed by an "x" character. Format: `0.0"x"`.
- **Underline-only on totals**, not bold-and-underline. Use `Border(bottom=Side(style='thin'))`.
- **No merged cells in input ranges.** Merged cells break formulas that reference them; reserve merging for headers and titles only.
- **Units in adjacent cells**, not in the cell with the value. `($M)` next to the value, not `"$1,234M"` as a string.

`scripts/build_workbook.py` applies these conventions automatically given a JSON spec.

## Formula authoring

- **Always emit formulas, never calculated values.** If the user wants `revenue × growth`, write `=B2*C2`, not `1234.56`. The recalc step materializes values.
- **Use named ranges** for cross-sheet inputs. `wb.defined_names["assumptions"] = DefinedName(...)`. Easier to audit.
- **Document units in adjacent cells** so the model is self-explanatory.
- **No volatile functions in hot paths.** `OFFSET`, `INDIRECT`, `NOW`, `TODAY` recalculate on every change and slow large workbooks.

## Recalculation — choose your engine

`openpyxl` writes formula *strings*; it does not evaluate them. You must recalculate before delivery, otherwise consumers will see `=B2*C2` literal text where they expect numbers (in some readers) or stale cached values (in others).

**LibreOffice path** (`recalc_libreoffice.py`) — ground truth:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_libreoffice.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Drives LibreOffice headless via the StarBasic macro `ThisComponent.calculateAll(); ThisComponent.store()`. Slow (~5–10s per workbook) but matches Excel for nearly every function. Use this when the workbook contains modern Excel features.

**Pure-Python path** (`recalc_pure_python.py`) — fast, partial:
```bash
python scripts/recalc_pure_python.py input.xlsx output.xlsx
```
Uses `xlcalculator` to evaluate every formula in pure Python. Fast (~0.5s per workbook). Covers ~80% of common functions: arithmetic, `SUM`, `IF`, `VLOOKUP`, `INDEX`/`MATCH`, basic string/date functions.

**Does NOT support**: `XLOOKUP`, `LET`, dynamic arrays (`FILTER`, `SEQUENCE`, `UNIQUE`), `LAMBDA`, `BYROW`, `TEXTJOIN` with refs, structured table references, most modern (post-2019) Excel features.

If you used any of those, run the LibreOffice path. The pure-Python path is for CI environments without LibreOffice.

## Error scan

After every recalc, scan for formula errors:

```bash
python scripts/scan_errors.py output.xlsx > errors.json
```

Reports every cell whose computed value matches `#REF!`, `#DIV/0!`, `#VALUE!`, `#NAME?`, `#NULL!`, `#NUM!`, or `#N/A`. Output is JSONL with `{sheet, address, value}` per line.

If errors exist, fix and re-recalc. Don't ship a workbook with `#REF!`s — it's the most common reason a deliverable fails QA.

## Validation gate

`scripts/validate.py output.xlsx` schema-validates against ECMA-376 SpreadsheetML XSDs and confirms ZIP integrity, content-type registration, sheet relationships.

## Out of scope

- Reading: use the `read` tool.
- **PivotTables** — `openpyxl` round-trips existing pivots but cannot create or modify them. If a task requires pivot creation, escalate (Windows-only via COM, not portable).
- **DAX measures and Power Pivot** — `openpyxl` can't write these. Same limitation.
- **VBA macros (`.xlsm`)** — out of scope for this skill. Macros require Excel runtime.
- **Conditional formatting beyond simple cell-value rules** — `openpyxl` supports basic CF; complex rules (top-N, data bars across sheets) often fail to round-trip.
- **Charts beyond the python-pptx-equivalent set** — line/bar/scatter work; combo charts and trendlines round-trip unreliably.

Draft a development agreement from the attached source documents, representing the developer's interests, with bracketed drafting notes flagging cross-document conflicts and open items. Output: `development-agreement-draft.docx`.
I'll start by exploring the workspace to understand what source documents are available.

**WHEREAS**, the City is the owner of certain real property commonly known as 1400--1550 Lakemont Station Boulevard, Lakemont, IL 60048, consisting of three (3) separate parcels of record identified by the following Parcel Identification Numbers: 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017, totaling approximately 12.7 acres (553,212 square feet) (collectively, the \"Property\"). The City acquired Parcels 12-24-300-015 and 12-24-300-016 through tax forfeiture proceedings concluded in 2016, and acquired Parcel 12-24-300-017 by direct purchase in 2018. The Property constitutes the former site of the Lakemont Ironworks manufacturing campus, which ceased industrial operations in 2009 and has remained substantially vacant and underutilized since that time. The Property is situated immediately adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra commuter rail station, providing direct access to commuter rail service on the Metra Milwaukee District/North Line; and

**WHEREAS**, the Property is currently zoned M-2 (General Manufacturing) under the City of Lakemont Zoning Ordinance, as amended. The proposed mixed-use transit-oriented development contemplated by this Resolution requires the rezoning of the Property from M-2 (General Manufacturing) to PD-TOD (Planned Development--Transit Oriented District) in order to permit the range of residential, commercial, office, hotel, and public uses contemplated by the proposed development program; and

**WHEREAS**, the City, through its Community Development Department under the direction of Paul R. Vasquez, Director, conducted a competitive Request for Proposals (\"RFP\") process for the redevelopment of the Property as a mixed-use transit-oriented development, consistent with the City\'s objectives of leveraging the public investment in the Lakemont Central Metra station, stimulating economic development in the surrounding area, generating tax revenues, creating employment opportunities, and providing housing options at a range of affordability levels. In response to said RFP process, Granite City Development LLC, an Illinois limited liability company formed on March 14, 2019, with its principal office located at 900 West Monroe Street, Suite 2200, Chicago, IL 60607, managed by Marcus J. Pellegrini (\"Developer\"), was selected as the preferred developer based upon its qualifications, experience, project proposal, and demonstrated financial capacity; and

**WHEREAS**, Granite City Development LLC has demonstrated substantial experience in transit-oriented, mixed-use, and affordable housing development, having successfully completed projects including the Halsted Collection (a mixed-use development in Chicago, Illinois), Riverside Crossing (a transit-oriented development in Naperville, Illinois), and Archer Park Residences (an affordable housing development in Chicago, Illinois). The Developer has a reported entity-level net worth of approximately One Hundred Eighty-Five Million Dollars (\$185,000,000), with audited financial statements available through Clearmont Accounting Group LLP, and maintains bonding capacity of up to Seventy-Five Million Dollars (\$75,000,000) through Ridgepoint Surety Corporation; and

**WHEREAS**, the ownership structure of Granite City Development LLC is as follows: sixty percent (60%) of the membership interests are held by the Pellegrini Family Trust; twenty-five percent (25%) of the membership interests are held by Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, a Delaware limited partnership whose general partner is Lakeshore Capital Management LLC (Managing Partner: Richard \"Rick\" Dominguez), with offices at 200 East Randolph Street, Suite 3100, Chicago, IL 60601; and fifteen percent (15%) of the membership interests are held by TransitWorks Equity Group LLC; and

**WHEREAS**, the Developer has proposed a four-phase mixed-use transit-oriented development to be known as \"Lakemont Station Commons\" (the \"Project\"), with a total gross building area of approximately 1,620,000 square feet, comprising the following program elements and estimated costs:

> *Phase I* --- Three hundred twenty (320) residential dwelling units, consisting of two hundred fifty-six (256) market-rate units and sixty-four (64) affordable housing units available to households earning at or below sixty percent (60%) of the Area Median Income (\"AMI\"); forty-five thousand (45,000) square feet of ground-floor retail space; and a four hundred eighty (480)-space structured parking facility. Phase I shall comprise approximately four hundred twenty thousand (420,000) square feet of gross building area, with an estimated hard construction cost of One Hundred Five Million Dollars (\$105,000,000).
>
> *Phase II* --- Two hundred eighty thousand (280,000) square feet of Class A office space; eighteen thousand (18,000) square feet of retail space; and a three hundred fifty (350)-space structured parking facility. Phase II shall comprise approximately three hundred eighty thousand (380,000) square feet of gross building area, with an estimated hard construction cost of Seventy-Six Million Dollars (\$76,000,000).
>
> *Phase III* --- Two hundred ten (210) residential dwelling units, consisting of one hundred sixty-eight (168) market-rate units and forty-two (42) affordable housing units available to households earning at or below sixty percent (60%) of AMI; and a one hundred fifty (150)-room select-service hotel. Phase III shall comprise approximately four hundred forty thousand (440,000) square feet of gross building area, with an estimated hard construction cost of Eighty-Eight Million Dollars (\$88,000,000).
>
> *Phase IV* --- A 2.5-acre public plaza incorporating landscaping and public art installations; a twenty-eight thousand (28,000) square foot community center facility (to be conveyed to the City upon completion); and a six hundred (600)-space public parking structure. Phase IV shall comprise approximately three hundred eighty thousand (380,000) square feet of gross building area, with an estimated hard construction cost of Seventy-One Million Dollars (\$71,000,000).

The Project, in the aggregate, shall include a total of five hundred thirty (530) residential dwelling units, of which one hundred six (106) shall be affordable housing units (representing approximately twenty percent (20%) of total residential units, thereby meeting the City\'s minimum twenty percent (20%) affordability target); sixty-three thousand (63,000) square feet of total retail space; two hundred eighty thousand (280,000) square feet of Class A office space; one hundred fifty (150) hotel rooms; one thousand four hundred thirty (1,430) structured parking spaces; a 2.5-acre public plaza; and a twenty-eight thousand (28,000) square foot community center. The total estimated hard construction cost for all four phases is approximately Three Hundred Forty Million Dollars (\$340,000,000), and the total estimated Project cost, inclusive of soft costs, is approximately Four Hundred Twelve Million Dollars (\$412,000,000); and

**WHEREAS**, the Developer has proposed the following phasing schedule for the construction of the Project:

> *Phase I:* Groundbreaking no later than March 1, 2026; Substantial Completion by September 30, 2028 (approximately thirty (30) months of construction from groundbreaking).
>
> *Phase II:* Commencement within twelve (12) months of Phase I Substantial Completion; Substantial Completion within twenty-four (24) months of Phase II commencement.
>
> *Phase III:* Commencement within twelve (12) months of Phase II Substantial Completion; Substantial Completion within thirty (30) months of Phase III commencement.
>
> *Phase IV:* Commencement concurrently with Phase III; Substantial Completion within twenty-four (24) months of Phase IV commencement.

; and

**WHEREAS**, the Property was appraised at a fair market value of Eighteen Million Four Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$18,400,000) on an as-is basis by Crossland Appraisal Services in an appraisal report dated January 15, 2025. Pursuant to the City\'s economic development incentive policies and the authority conferred upon the City by its home rule powers, the City proposes to convey the Property to the Developer at a purchase price of Nine Million Two Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$9,200,000), representing fifty percent (50%) of the appraised fair market value, resulting in a land write-down incentive with a value of Nine Million Two Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$9,200,000); and

**WHEREAS**, the Property is located within the Lakemont Central Tax Increment Financing (\"TIF\") District, which was established pursuant to Ordinance No. 2019-42 adopted by the City Council and which expires on December 31, 2042. The City proposes to pledge up to Forty-Eight Million Dollars (\$48,000,000) in tax increment financing revenues over the remaining life of the TIF District to reimburse the Developer for eligible redevelopment project costs, including but not limited to public infrastructure improvements, environmental remediation, and the construction of parking structures, on a pay-as-you-go basis. The estimated annual tax increment generated by the Project at full buildout is approximately Four Million Two Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$4,200,000) per year; and

**WHEREAS**, in addition to the land write-down and tax increment financing incentives described above, the City proposes to provide the following additional public incentives in connection with the Project:

> \(a\) *Infrastructure Cost-Sharing:* The City shall commit Twelve Million Five Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$12,500,000) toward off-site infrastructure improvements, including the widening of Lakemont Station Boulevard, the installation of a new traffic signal at the Oak Street intersection, the extension of water main service, and the upgrade of sanitary sewer capacity. The Developer shall be responsible for Eight Million Three Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$8,300,000) of on-site infrastructure improvements. The total combined infrastructure budget for on-site and off-site improvements is Twenty Million Eight Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$20,800,000).
>
> \(b\) *Property Tax Abatement:* A property tax abatement for Phase I improvements on a twelve (12)-year declining schedule, as follows: Years 1 through 4 --- seventy-five percent (75%) abatement; Years 5 through 8 --- fifty percent (50%) abatement; Years 9 through 12 --- twenty-five percent (25%) abatement; Year 13 and thereafter --- full assessment with no abatement.
>
> \(c\) *Building Permit Fee Waiver:* A waiver of building permit fees for Phase I and Phase II of the Project, with an estimated combined value of One Million Four Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$1,400,000), consisting of Nine Hundred Twenty Thousand Dollars (\$920,000) for Phase I and Four Hundred Eighty Thousand Dollars (\$480,000) for Phase II.

; and

**WHEREAS**, a Phase II Environmental Site Assessment was completed by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC in a report dated November 8, 2024. Said assessment identified the following environmental conditions on the Property:

> \(a\) *Parcel 12-24-300-015* --- No environmental contamination requiring further action was identified; this parcel requires no further investigation or remediation.
>
> \(b\) *Parcel 12-24-300-016* --- Petroleum hydrocarbon contamination, specifically benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, and xylene (\"BTEX\") compounds, was identified in soil and groundwater over an area of approximately 1.8 acres. Remediation under the Illinois Environmental Protection Agency (\"IEPA\") Voluntary Site Remediation Program (\"VSRP\") is required, at an estimated cost of Two Million Eight Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$2,800,000) and an estimated timeline of fourteen (14) to eighteen (18) months, culminating in the issuance of a No Further Remediation (\"NFR\") letter by the IEPA.
>
> \(c\) *Parcel 12-24-300-017* --- Low-level metals contamination in shallow soil consistent with urban fill material was identified. This contamination is suitable for residential use with the installation of an engineered barrier (clean fill cap), at an estimated cost of Three Hundred Forty Thousand Dollars (\$340,000).

The total estimated environmental remediation costs for the Property are Three Million One Hundred Forty Thousand Dollars (\$3,140,000); and

**WHEREAS**, the City Manager, Gerald T. Fong, acting on behalf of the City, and the Developer, by its Manager, Marcus J. Pellegrini, executed a non-binding Negotiated Term Sheet dated January 22, 2025 (the \"Term Sheet\"), setting forth the principal economic and legal terms proposed to govern the development agreement for the Project; and

**WHEREAS**, the Developer has obtained a preliminary (non-binding) commitment letter from First Prairie National Bank for a construction loan facility in an amount of up to Two Hundred Ten Million Dollars (\$210,000,000), subject to the execution of a development agreement in form and substance acceptable to said lender. In addition, the Developer\'s equity sources for the Project include the Pellegrini Family Trust, which has committed Thirty-Four Million Dollars (\$34,000,000), and Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, which has committed Fifty-One Million Dollars (\$51,000,000), for total committed equity of Eighty-Five Million Dollars (\$85,000,000); and

**WHEREAS**, Corporation Counsel Rebecca A. Sinclair, Esq., and the City\'s outside municipal counsel, Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP, by partner Jonathan Ashford, have reviewed the proposed terms of the development and the Negotiated Term Sheet and have recommended conditional approval of the Project by the City Council, subject to the conditions set forth herein and subject to the preparation and execution of a Development Agreement satisfactory to Corporation Counsel; and

**WHEREAS**, the City Council of the City of Lakemont hereby finds and determines that the proposed Lakemont Station Commons development will advance the public interest of the City and the health, safety, and welfare of its residents by: creating transit-oriented housing and commercial space in close proximity to public transportation; generating substantial property tax revenues for the City, its overlapping taxing districts, and the Lakemont Central TIF District; creating significant construction employment and permanent employment opportunities; providing affordable housing for households at or below sixty percent (60%) of Area Median Income; remediating environmental contamination on formerly blighted and underutilized industrial land; constructing public infrastructure improvements, a public plaza, and a community center to serve the residents of the City; and furthering the goals and objectives of the City\'s Comprehensive Plan, transit-oriented development policies, and economic development strategies.

**NOW, THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED** by the City Council of the City of Lakemont, Illinois, as follows:

**Section 1. Incorporation of Recitals.** The above recitals are hereby incorporated into and made a part of this Resolution as though fully set forth herein and are adopted as the findings of the City Council of the City of Lakemont, Illinois.

**Section 2. Conditional Approval Granted; Authorization of City Manager.** The City Council of the City of Lakemont, Illinois, hereby grants conditional approval for the Lakemont Station Commons mixed-use transit-oriented development project as generally described in the recitals hereof and authorizes the City Manager, Gerald T. Fong, to negotiate and execute a Development Agreement (the \"Development Agreement\") with Granite City Development LLC for the development of the Property, subject to the conditions set forth in Section 3 of this Resolution and subject to final review and approval as to form and legality by Corporation Counsel Rebecca A. Sinclair and outside municipal counsel Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP.

**Section 3. Conditions of Approval.** The conditional approval granted by Section 2 of this Resolution and the authority of the City Manager to execute the Development Agreement are expressly subject to the satisfaction of each of the following conditions:

> **(a) Equity Demonstration.** The Developer shall demonstrate, to the reasonable satisfaction of the City Manager and Corporation Counsel, committed equity of not less than Eighty-Five Million Dollars (\$85,000,000) within sixty (60) days of the adoption of this Resolution (i.e., on or before April 19, 2025). Such demonstration shall include executed commitment letters from equity partners, evidence of available and uncommitted capital, bank statements, or other documentation satisfactory to the City in its reasonable discretion, verified by an independent certification from the Developer\'s auditing firm, Clearmont Accounting Group LLP.
>
> **(b) Environmental Remediation Approval.** Prior to any conveyance of the Property or any portion thereof to the Developer, the Developer shall have submitted, and the Illinois Environmental Protection Agency (\"IEPA\") shall have approved, a Remedial Action Plan (\"RAP\") for the environmental remediation of Parcel 12-24-300-016 under the IEPA Voluntary Site Remediation Program (\"VSRP\"). The RAP must comprehensively address the petroleum hydrocarbon contamination, including benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, and xylene (\"BTEX\") compounds in soil and groundwater, as identified in the Phase II Environmental Site Assessment dated November 8, 2024, prepared by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC, and must provide for the issuance of a No Further Remediation (\"NFR\") letter upon completion of all required remediation activities.
>
> **(c) Community Benefits Agreement.** The Development Agreement shall incorporate a Community Benefits Agreement (\"CBA\") or Community Benefits provisions requiring, at a minimum, the following:
>
> \(i\) A local hiring goal requiring that not less than thirty-five percent (35%) of all construction labor hours on the Project be performed by individuals who are residents of the City of Lakemont, Illinois;
>
> \(ii\) A living wage requirement mandating that all permanent employees employed at retail establishments and hotel operations within the Project be compensated at a wage rate of not less than Seventeen Dollars and Fifty Cents (\$17.50) per hour; and
>
> \(iii\) A first-source hiring program under which the Developer and its commercial tenants shall partner with the Lakemont Workforce Center for the recruitment, training, and hiring of individuals to fill permanent employment positions created within the Project.
>
> **(d) Performance Security.** Prior to the conveyance of the Property to the Developer, the Developer shall post a performance bond or irrevocable standby letter of credit in the amount of Fifteen Million Dollars (\$15,000,000), issued by a surety company or financial institution acceptable to the City, securing the Developer\'s obligation to complete Phase I of the Project in accordance with the terms and conditions of the Development Agreement.
>
> **(e) Rezoning and Parcel Consolidation.** The rezoning of the Property from M-2 (General Manufacturing) to PD-TOD (Planned Development--Transit Oriented District) shall be completed, adopted by ordinance, and effective prior to the execution of the Development Agreement. The City Manager is hereby authorized to initiate rezoning proceedings concurrently with the negotiation of the Development Agreement. The three (3) parcels comprising the Property (Parcel IDs 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017) shall be consolidated into a single parcel of record prior to or concurrent with the completion of the rezoning proceedings.
>
> **(f) LEED Gold Certification.** All buildings constructed as part of the Project shall achieve Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design (\"LEED\") Gold certification, or an equivalent green building certification as determined acceptable by the Director of the Community Development Department, Paul R. Vasquez.
>
> **(g) Affordable Housing Minimum.** Not less than twenty percent (20%) of the total residential dwelling units constructed in the Project shall be designated as affordable housing units available to households earning at or below sixty percent (60%) of the Area Median Income (\"AMI\") as determined by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development for the Chicago-Naperville-Elgin Metropolitan Statistical Area, or any successor designation thereof.
>
> **(h) Reversionary Interest / Clawback.** The Developer shall grant to the City a reversionary interest in the Property, such that in the event Phase I of the Project is not Substantially Complete (as such term shall be defined in the Development Agreement) within thirty-six (36) months of the date of conveyance of the Property to the Developer, fee simple title to the Property shall automatically revert to the City, together with all improvements constructed thereon, subject to the City\'s obligation to refund to the Developer the purchase price paid by the Developer for the Property (net of any damages incurred by the City as a result of the Developer\'s failure to timely complete Phase I). The form, terms, and conditions of such reversionary interest shall be set forth in detail in the Development Agreement and shall be recorded against the Property at the time of conveyance.

**Section 4. Authorization of City Manager.** The City Manager, Gerald T. Fong, is hereby authorized and directed to negotiate the terms of the Development Agreement with Granite City Development LLC consistent with the terms described in the recitals hereof and subject to the conditions set forth in Section 3 of this Resolution. The City Manager is further authorized to execute the Development Agreement on behalf of the City of Lakemont, Illinois, upon the satisfaction of all conditions set forth herein and upon approval of the Development Agreement as to form and legality by Corporation Counsel Rebecca A. Sinclair and outside municipal counsel Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP. The City Manager is additionally authorized to execute such ancillary documents, certificates, and instruments as may be necessary or appropriate to effectuate the purposes of this Resolution and the Development Agreement.

**Section 5. Authorization of Rezoning Proceedings.** The City Manager and the Director of the Community Development Department, Paul R. Vasquez, are hereby authorized and directed to initiate proceedings for the rezoning of the Property from M-2 (General Manufacturing) to PD-TOD (Planned Development--Transit Oriented District) and for the consolidation of Parcels 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017 into a single parcel of record, in accordance with applicable provisions of the City of Lakemont Zoning Ordinance and Illinois law.

**Section 6. Directions to Corporation Counsel.** Corporation Counsel Rebecca A. Sinclair and outside municipal counsel Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP are hereby directed to prepare or review, as applicable, the Development Agreement, all ancillary documents and instruments (including but not limited to the deed of conveyance, environmental covenants and access agreements, performance security instruments, community benefits provisions, and any restrictive covenants or declarations), and any rezoning ordinances or amendments to the Zoning Ordinance necessary to implement the purposes of this Resolution.

**Section 7. TIF Compliance.** Any use of tax increment financing revenues in connection with the Project shall comply in all respects with the Illinois Tax Increment Allocation Redevelopment Act (65 ILCS 5/11-74.4-1 *et seq.*) and the Lakemont Central TIF District Redevelopment Plan and Project as adopted by Ordinance No. 2019-42, as the same may be amended from time to time. All TIF-eligible redevelopment project costs shall be determined and reimbursed in accordance with applicable provisions of Illinois law and the terms and conditions of the Development Agreement.

**Section 8. Prevailing Wage.** All construction work performed pursuant to the Development Agreement that constitutes \"public works\" within the meaning of the Illinois Prevailing Wage Act (820 ILCS 130/0.01 *et seq.*) shall be subject to the prevailing wage requirements of said Act. The Development Agreement shall include appropriate prevailing wage compliance provisions, including but not limited to record-keeping, reporting, and certification requirements.

**Section 9. Severability.** If any provision of this Resolution, or the application thereof to any person or circumstance, is held invalid or unenforceable by a court of competent jurisdiction, such invalidity or unenforceability shall not affect other provisions or applications of this Resolution that can be given effect without the invalid or unenforceable provision or application, and to this end the provisions of this Resolution are hereby declared to be severable.

**Section 10. Effective Date.** This Resolution shall be in full force and effect from and after its adoption by the City Council of the City of Lakemont, Illinois.

**ADOPTED** by the City Council of the City of Lakemont, Illinois, this 18th day of February, 2025.

result:
**[PRELIMINARY TERM SHEET --- CONSTRUCTION LOAN FACILITY]{.underline}**

**FOR DISCUSSION PURPOSES ONLY --- NON-BINDING**

March \_\_, 2025

**From:** First Prairie National Bank 135 South LaSalle Street Chicago, IL 60603

**To:** Granite City Development LLC 900 West Monroe Street, Suite 2200 Chicago, IL 60607

Attention: Marcus J. Pellegrini, Manager/CEO

**Relationship Manager:** Sandra L. Chen, Senior Vice President, Real Estate Lending Division

**Re:** Proposed Construction Financing --- Lakemont Station Commons, 1400--1550 Lakemont Station Boulevard, Lakemont, IL 60048

**IMPORTANT NOTICE:** This Preliminary Term Sheet is intended solely as a summary of the principal terms on which First Prairie National Bank may consider providing the financing described herein. This document is for discussion purposes only and does not constitute a commitment, agreement, or obligation to lend by First Prairie National Bank. All terms are subject to the conditions and qualifications set forth in Section 14 below.

**[1. PARTIES AND PROJECT SUMMARY]{.underline}**

**Lender:** First Prairie National Bank (\"First Prairie\" or \"Lender\"), a nationally chartered banking institution with main office at 135 South LaSalle Street, Chicago, IL 60603.

**Borrower:** Granite City Development LLC, an Illinois limited liability company formed March 14, 2019 (\"Borrower\" or \"Developer\").

**Guarantor(s):** Marcus J. Pellegrini, individually, and Pellegrini Family Trust (collectively, \"Guarantors\"). Guarantors to provide a personal/trust guaranty of completion and a partial repayment guaranty under a burn-down structure, the specific terms of which are to be determined and documented in the definitive loan agreements.

**Project:** Lakemont Station Commons (the \"Project\") --- a mixed-use transit-oriented development on approximately 12.7 acres (Parcel IDs: 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, 12-24-300-017, to be consolidated) adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra station, consisting of approximately 1,620,000 gross square feet across four phases, including:

> • Residential: 530 units
>
> • Office: 280,000 sq ft
>
> • Retail: 63,000 sq ft
>
> • Hotel: 150 rooms
>
> • Community Center: 28,000 sq ft
>
> • Public Plaza: 2.5 acres
>
> • Structured Parking: 1,430 spaces

**Total Estimated Project Cost:** Approximately \$412,000,000.

**Total Hard Construction Cost:** Approximately \$340,000,000.

**[2. LOAN TERMS]{.underline}**

**2.1 Facility Amount and Structure**

**Facility Amount:** Up to \$210,000,000 (the \"Loan\" or \"Construction Facility\").

**Structure:** Revolving construction loan facility with phased draw-down. Loan proceeds available for Phase I and Phase II hard and soft construction costs. Availability for Phases III and IV subject to a separate supplemental commitment or amendment, to be negotiated at the appropriate time.

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Component**                      **Sub-Limit**           **Description**
  ---------------------------------- ----------------------- -----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Phase I**                        \$105,000,000           Residential Tower A + Retail Podium: 320 residential units, 45,000 sq ft retail, 480 parking spaces (\~420,000 gross sq ft)

  **Phase II**                       \$76,000,000            Office Building + Retail: 280,000 sq ft office, 18,000 sq ft retail, 350 parking spaces (\~380,000 gross sq ft)

  **Infrastructure / Contingency**   \$29,000,000            Infrastructure costs, contingencies, and pre-development costs, subject to Lender-approved budget
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Soft Cost Allocation:** Up to 20% of each phase sub-limit may be allocated to eligible soft costs (architecture, engineering, permitting, legal, accounting, pre-opening costs) --- approximately \$21,000,000 for Phase I and \$15,200,000 for Phase II.

**2.2 Interest Rate and Fees**

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Item**                            **Terms**
  ----------------------------------- ---------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Interest Rate**                   SOFR (30-day term) + 275 basis points; SOFR floor of 1.00%

  **Default Rate**                    Applicable rate + 400 basis points

  **Commitment Fee**                  0.50% of total Facility Amount (\$1,050,000), payable at closing

  **Unused Line Fee**                 0.25% per annum on daily average undrawn balance, payable quarterly

  **Extension Fee**                   0.25% of then-outstanding balance per extension period

  **Exit Fee**                        None
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Interest shall be payable monthly, in arrears. An interest reserve shall be funded from Loan proceeds, estimated at a 24-month interest reserve for Phase I.

**2.3 Term and Maturity**

**Initial Term:** 36 months from initial closing/first draw.

**Extension Options:** Two (2) six-month extensions, each subject to: (a) no existing Event of Default; (b) payment of the extension fee; (c) achievement of minimum construction milestones to be agreed upon in definitive documentation; and (d) an updated appraisal demonstrating compliance with the minimum loan-to-value ratio.

**Maximum Term:** 48 months (36 + 6 + 6).

**Phase I Construction Period:** Estimated at 30 months. Groundbreaking shall occur no later than March 1, 2026, with Substantial Completion targeted by September 30, 2028.

**Phase II Commencement:** Within 12 months of Phase I Substantial Completion. Phase II availability shall be subject to satisfaction of Phase II conditions precedent to be specified in definitive documentation.

**2.4 Repayment. Interest Only:** During the construction period, Borrower shall pay interest only, funded from the interest reserve and/or Borrower equity contributions.

**Principal Repayment:** The outstanding principal balance shall be due and payable at maturity or upon conversion to permanent financing. Borrower shall deliver an executed take-out commitment from a creditworthy permanent lender no later than six (6) months prior to the initial maturity date.

**Mandatory Prepayment:** Borrower shall apply to prepayment: (a) net proceeds of any casualty or condemnation event exceeding \$5,000,000; (b) net proceeds of any residential unit sales, if applicable; and (c) TIF reimbursement receipts in excess of amounts allocated to non-Lender-financed Project costs per the Lender-approved budget.

**Voluntary Prepayment:** Permitted at any time without premium or penalty.

**[3. EQUITY REQUIREMENTS]{.underline}**

**Minimum Equity Contribution:** Borrower shall contribute minimum equity of \$85,000,000 to the Project before any Loan draw-down, representing not less than approximately 20.6% of total Project cost (\$85,000,000 / \$412,000,000).

**Equity Sources (as represented by Borrower):**

  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Source**                               **Amount**
  ---------------------------------------- -----------------------------------
  Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP   \$51,000,000

  Pellegrini Family Trust                  \$34,000,000

  **Total**                                **\$85,000,000**
  ----------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Equity First Requirement:** All equity shall be contributed and expended prior to any draw on the Construction Facility (\"equity in first\" basis). No Loan proceeds shall be advanced until the full \$85,000,000 equity contribution has been invested in the Project and verified by Lender.

**Verification:** Borrower shall provide evidence of committed equity satisfactory to Lender, including executed subscription agreements, fund commitment letters, and proof of liquid assets in trust accounts. Financial statements of equity sources shall be audited by Clearmont Accounting Group LLP or another nationally recognized accounting firm acceptable to Lender.

**Additional Equity for Later Phases:** Borrower acknowledges that Phases III and IV are estimated to require additional equity and/or mezzanine financing in an amount of approximately \$58,400,000. Any additional debt or mezzanine financing subordinate to the Loan shall require prior written consent of Lender (see Section 6.3). Borrower further acknowledges that any equity raise that results in the dilution of Pellegrini Family Trust\'s ownership interest below 50% of Borrower\'s membership interests will require Lender\'s prior written consent under Section 8.1 and may constitute a Change of Control. Borrower is encouraged to plan its capital structure for later phases accordingly and to consult with Lender early in any capital-raising process.

**[4. COLLATERAL AND SECURITY]{.underline}**

**4.1 Mortgage and Security Interest**

Borrower shall grant Lender the following collateral and security interests:

> • **First Priority Mortgage:** First priority mortgage lien on the entire Project site (consolidated Parcels 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, 12-24-300-017; approximately 12.7 acres / 553,212 sq ft) and all improvements thereon, together with all appurtenances, easements, and fixtures.
>
> • **Assignment of Leases and Rents:** First priority assignment of all leases, rents, profits, and income from the Project.
>
> • **Assignment of Contracts:** Collateral assignment of all construction contracts, architect agreements, development agreements (including the Development Agreement with the City of Lakemont), and other material Project contracts.
>
> • **Assignment of TIF Reimbursement Rights:** Collateral assignment of Borrower\'s rights to receive TIF reimbursement payments under the Development Agreement (up to \$48,000,000 over the life of the Lakemont Central TIF District, Ordinance No. 2019-42, expiring December 31, 2042).
>
> • **Assignment of Permits and Entitlements:** Collateral assignment of all governmental permits, approvals, entitlements, and development rights associated with the Project.
>
> • **Pledge of Membership Interests:** Pledge of 100% of the membership interests in Borrower (Granite City Development LLC) --- i.e., all interests held by Pellegrini Family Trust (60%), Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP (25%), and TransitWorks Equity Group LLC (15%).
>
> • **Performance Bond:** Assignment of any performance bonds or letters of credit posted in connection with the Development Agreement, including the Phase I performance bond of \$15,000,000 through Ridgepoint Surety Corporation (or an irrevocable standby letter of credit in equivalent amount).

**4.2 Title Requirements**

**Insurable Fee Simple Title:** Borrower must hold insurable fee simple absolute title to the entire Project site, free and clear of all liens, encumbrances, restrictions, reversionary interests, rights of re-entry, possibilities of reverter, defeasible conditions, and other title defects or exceptions, except for Permitted Exceptions (as defined below).

**Prohibition on Defeasible Fee / Reversionary Interests:** Under no circumstances shall the mortgaged property be subject to any reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, fee simple determinable, fee simple subject to condition subsequent, or any other form of defeasible fee estate in favor of the City of Lakemont or any other party. Any such interest would render Borrower\'s title uninsurable for purposes of the Loan and is strictly prohibited as a condition of Lender\'s commitment.

**Lender\'s Title Insurance:** Lender shall receive an ALTA lender\'s title insurance policy in the amount of \$210,000,000 (full Loan amount) from a title company acceptable to Lender, with such endorsements as Lender may require, including without limitation zoning, comprehensive, survey, access, contiguity, PUD/planned development, environmental lien, and construction loan endorsements.

**Permitted Exceptions:** Title exceptions shall be limited to the following:

> \(i\) Real estate taxes not yet due and payable;
>
> \(ii\) Standard utility and access easements that do not materially interfere with the development, use, or value of the Project;
>
> \(iii\) The Development Agreement between Borrower and the City of Lakemont, in form and substance acceptable to Lender (see Section 7.1 and Section 12);
>
> \(iv\) Covenants and restrictions of the PD-TOD zoning ordinance applicable to the Project site;
>
> \(v\) Environmental use controls or institutional controls recorded in connection with the IEPA Voluntary Site Remediation Program NFR letter for Parcel 12-24-300-016 (BTEX contamination) and engineered barrier requirements for Parcel 12-24-300-017, in each case as reviewed and approved by Lender\'s counsel; and
>
> \(vi\) Such other exceptions as Lender may approve in writing.

**Lender\'s Rationale:** Lender notes that any reversionary or defeasible interest in favor of the City would constitute a superior possessory right that would: (a) be uninsurable under standard ALTA title insurance policies; (b) effectively subordinate Lender\'s mortgage to the City\'s right to reclaim the property; (c) prevent Lender from exercising standard foreclosure remedies; and (d) render the Loan unsecurable. Borrower is advised to negotiate the Development Agreement to eliminate any such reversionary interest and to substitute an alternative clawback mechanism (e.g., a repurchase option exercisable only after satisfaction of Lender\'s mortgage, liquidated damages, or an enhanced performance guaranty) that preserves Borrower\'s fee simple absolute title.

**4.3 Environmental Conditions**

**Parcel 12-24-300-016 (BTEX Contamination):** Borrower must obtain a No Further Remediation (NFR) letter from the Illinois Environmental Protection Agency (IEPA) under the Voluntary Site Remediation Program (VSRP) for this parcel prior to any Loan draw-down for construction activities on that parcel. Remediation is estimated at \$2,800,000 with a timeline of 14--18 months, as set forth in the Phase II Environmental Site Assessment by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC dated November 8, 2024. The identified contaminants are petroleum hydrocarbons: benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, and xylene (BTEX).

**Parcel 12-24-300-017 (Metals in Fill):** An engineered barrier consisting of a clean fill cap (estimated cost \$340,000) must be installed per environmental report recommendations. Evidence of completion satisfactory to Lender shall be delivered prior to or concurrent with construction activities on that parcel.

**Parcel 12-24-300-015:** No further action required. This parcel is clean per the Phase II ESA.

**Environmental Insurance:** Borrower shall obtain pollution legal liability insurance in an amount not less than \$10,000,000, naming Lender as additional insured, with a policy term covering the full construction period and a three-year extended reporting period.

**Institutional Controls:** Any institutional controls or environmental use restrictions recorded against the property must be reviewed and approved by Lender\'s counsel prior to closing.

**TIF Reimbursement:** TIF reimbursement obligations under the Development Agreement (up to the \$48,000,000 cap) are acknowledged as contractual rights, not liens on real property. However, to the extent any City lien, priority claim, or security interest on the property arises in connection with TIF compliance or the Development Agreement, such interest must be subordinated to Lender\'s mortgage pursuant to an intercreditor and subordination agreement in form and substance acceptable to Lender.

**Additional Capital for Later Phases:** Borrower\'s estimated need for approximately \$58,400,000 in additional debt and/or mezzanine capital for Phases III and IV is acknowledged. Any such financing shall require Lender\'s prior written approval of the terms, structure, documentation, and the identity of the capital provider. Lender will evaluate such requests in light of the impact on Borrower\'s capitalization, the change-of-control provisions of Section 8.1, and the overall viability of the Project.

**[8. ASSIGNMENT AND TRANSFER RESTRICTIONS]{.underline}**

**8.1 Borrower Assignment Restrictions**

**No Assignment Without Consent.** Borrower shall not assign, transfer, sell, pledge, or convey any interest in the Loan, the Project, the Project site, or any material Project assets without Lender\'s prior written consent, which consent may be withheld in Lender\'s sole and absolute discretion.

**Change of Control.** A \"Change of Control\" shall be deemed to occur if any of the following events take place without Lender\'s prior written consent:

> \(a\) Marcus J. Pellegrini (directly or through the Pellegrini Family Trust) ceases to hold at least a \[50\]% direct or indirect ownership interest in Borrower;
>
> \(b\) Marcus J. Pellegrini ceases to serve as Manager/CEO of Borrower and to exercise day-to-day management authority over the Project; or
>
> \(c\) Any single investor or group of affiliated investors (other than the existing members as of the Loan closing date) acquires a 25% or greater interest in Borrower.

**Change of Control as Event of Default.** Any Change of Control that occurs without Lender\'s prior written consent shall constitute an immediate Event of Default under the Loan.

**Permitted Transfers.** The following transfers shall not require Lender\'s consent and shall not constitute a Change of Control:

> \(i\) Transfers among the existing members of Borrower (Pellegrini Family Trust, Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, and TransitWorks Equity Group LLC) that do not reduce Pellegrini Family Trust\'s ownership interest below \[50\]%;
>
> \(ii\) Transfers by Pellegrini for estate planning purposes to family trusts or entities controlled by Pellegrini, provided Pellegrini retains management control; and
>
> \(iii\) Transfers of non-managing limited partnership interests in Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP among its existing or admitted limited partners.

**Note on 50% Threshold.** Lender acknowledges Borrower\'s representation that the current ownership structure of Borrower is as follows: Pellegrini Family Trust --- 60%; Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP --- 25%; TransitWorks Equity Group LLC --- 15%. Borrower acknowledges that any dilution of Pellegrini Family Trust\'s interest below 50%, whether through additional equity raises, capital calls, anti-dilution adjustments, or admission of new members for Phases III and IV or otherwise, will require Lender\'s prior written consent and may trigger a Change of Control. Borrower represents that it is aware that the Development Agreement with the City of Lakemont may contain assignment restrictions keyed to Pellegrini\'s controlling interest, and Borrower covenants to ensure that the Development Agreement assignment provisions and the Loan Documents are not in conflict. The bracketed \[50\]% threshold is subject to further discussion and negotiation between Borrower and Lender during definitive documentation.

**8.2 Lender Transfer Rights**

Lender may sell, assign, or participate the Loan in whole or in part at any time without Borrower\'s consent. Lender may provide loan-related information and documentation to potential assignees, participants, or co-lenders, subject to customary confidentiality agreements. If the Loan is syndicated, a lead lender or agent bank shall be designated, and Lender shall retain servicing and administrative authority unless otherwise agreed.

**[9. EVENTS OF DEFAULT]{.underline}**

Each of the following shall constitute an \"Event of Default\" under the Loan:

1\. Failure to pay any principal, interest, fee, or other amount when due (subject to a five (5) business day grace period for non-willful monetary defaults).

2\. Any representation or warranty made by Borrower or any Guarantor proves to have been false or misleading in any material respect when made or deemed made.

3\. Breach of any covenant contained in the Loan Documents (subject to a thirty (30) day cure period for non-monetary defaults that are susceptible to cure, provided Borrower is diligently pursuing such cure).

4\. A Change of Control without Lender\'s prior written consent, as defined in Section 8.1.

5\. Any material default by Borrower under the Development Agreement that is not cured within applicable cure periods provided therein, or any termination of the Development Agreement by the City for any reason.

6\. The filing of a voluntary or involuntary petition in bankruptcy, assignment for the benefit of creditors, or appointment of a receiver for Borrower or any Guarantor.

7\. A material adverse change in the financial condition of Borrower or any Guarantor that, in Lender\'s reasonable judgment, impairs the ability to perform obligations under the Loan Documents.

8\. Condemnation or taking (including inverse condemnation) of more than 15% of the Project site by area or value.

9\. Failure to achieve Substantial Completion of Phase I by September 30, 2028 (subject to force majeure extensions of up to 180 days and any Lender-approved extension periods under Section 2.3).

10\. Loss, termination, revocation, or material adverse modification of any governmental entitlement, permit, or approval material to the Project, including the PD-TOD rezoning, TIF eligibility, or environmental approvals.

11\. The imposition, creation, or recording of any lien, encumbrance, or interest upon the Project site without Lender\'s prior written consent, including without limitation any reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, or defeasible condition in favor of the City of Lakemont or any other party.

12\. A default by Borrower under any other indebtedness exceeding \$1,000,000 that is not cured within any applicable grace period.

**[10. REMEDIES]{.underline}**

Upon the occurrence of any Event of Default, Lender may, at its sole option and without further notice (except as required by applicable law):

> \(a\) Declare all outstanding principal, accrued interest, fees, and other amounts immediately due and payable;
>
> \(b\) Cease making any further advances under the Construction Facility;
>
> \(c\) Exercise all rights and remedies under the mortgage, including foreclosure by judicial or other means available under Illinois law;
>
> \(d\) Exercise all rights and remedies under the assignment of leases and rents, including the right to collect rents directly;
>
> \(e\) Exercise all rights and remedies under the pledge of membership interests, including the right to sell or otherwise dispose of such interests;
>
> \(f\) Enforce the completion guaranty and partial repayment guaranty against the Guarantors;
>
> \(g\) Apply any performance bond or letter of credit proceeds to the outstanding Loan balance or to cure Project deficiencies; and
>
> \(h\) Exercise any and all other rights and remedies available under the Loan Documents, at law, or in equity.

Lender shall have the right, but not the obligation, to cure any default by Borrower under the Development Agreement on behalf of Borrower and to add all cure costs, including reasonable attorney\'s fees and expenses, to the outstanding Loan balance. All of Lender\'s rights and remedies under the Loan Documents are cumulative and not exclusive of any other rights or remedies available at law or in equity.

**[12. DEVELOPER ACKNOWLEDGMENTS AND LENDER REQUIREMENTS REGARDING DEVELOPMENT AGREEMENT]{.underline}**

The Development Agreement between Borrower and the City of Lakemont is a critical component of the Project\'s legal and financial framework. This section sets forth Lender\'s specific requirements and concerns regarding the Development Agreement. Borrower and its counsel (Thornfield & Pratt LLP, Attn: Catherine M. Albrecht) should carefully consider each requirement below in negotiating and drafting the Development Agreement.

**Lender Review Right.** The Development Agreement shall be delivered to Lender in draft form not less than fifteen (15) business days prior to its proposed execution date. The Development Agreement shall not be executed until Lender has confirmed in writing that it is in form and substance acceptable to Lender. Lender\'s review shall not be unreasonably delayed.

**Required Development Agreement Provisions (from Lender\'s Perspective):**

> \(a\) **No Reversionary Interest / Defeasible Fee.** The Development Agreement shall not grant the City of Lakemont a reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, or any other interest that would render Borrower\'s fee simple title defeasible. Lender acknowledges that City Council Resolution R-2025-017 (adopted February 18, 2025), at condition #8, contemplates a \"reversionary interest\" as a clawback mechanism in the event Phase I is not Substantially Complete within 36 months of land conveyance. Borrower must negotiate an alternative mechanism that satisfies the City\'s policy objective of ensuring timely Phase I completion without impairing Borrower\'s fee simple title or Lender\'s security interest. Acceptable alternatives are described below.
>
> \(b\) **Lender Cure Rights.** The Development Agreement shall include provisions granting Lender the right (but not the obligation) to receive prompt written notice of any Borrower default under the Development Agreement and a reasonable opportunity to cure such default. Lender\'s additional cure period shall be not less than thirty (30) days beyond Borrower\'s cure period for monetary defaults and not less than sixty (60) days beyond Borrower\'s cure period for non-monetary defaults, with such periods extended as necessary if Lender is diligently pursuing a cure that requires additional time (including time necessary to obtain possession of the Project site through foreclosure or appointment of a receiver).
>
> \(c\) **Lender Consent to Amendments.** No material amendment, modification, supplement, or termination of the Development Agreement shall be effective without Lender\'s prior written consent, which shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed.
>
> \(d\) **Subordination of City Interests.** Any lien, encumbrance, or security interest that the City may hold or acquire in the Project site (other than the City\'s reversionary or re-entry rights, which must be eliminated per subsection (a) above) shall be subordinated to Lender\'s mortgage pursuant to a subordination agreement in form and substance acceptable to Lender.
>
> \(e\) **Foreclosure / Deed-in-Lieu.** The Development Agreement shall include a provision permitting assignment of the Development Agreement to a Lender-designee or to a third-party purchaser at a foreclosure sale (or pursuant to a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure) without the City\'s prior consent, or if City consent is required, such consent shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed, and the City shall respond within thirty (30) days of request.
>
> \(f\) **Estoppel Certificates.** The Development Agreement shall require the City to deliver estoppel certificates to Lender upon reasonable request (not more than twice per calendar year), certifying the status of the Development Agreement, the existence or absence of any outstanding defaults, and such other customary matters as Lender may reasonably request.

**Specific Concern --- Resolution R-2025-017, Condition #8.**

Lender has reviewed City Council Resolution R-2025-017 and notes that Condition #8 requires Borrower to grant the City a \"reversionary interest\" in the Project site if Phase I is not Substantially Complete within 36 months of land conveyance. Lender cannot commit to a loan secured by a mortgage on property that is subject to a reversionary interest or any form of defeasible fee estate. A reversionary interest in favor of the City would constitute a superior possessory right that would: (i) be uninsurable under standard ALTA title insurance policies; (ii) effectively subordinate Lender\'s first-priority mortgage to the City\'s right to reclaim the property upon the occurrence of the stated condition; (iii) prevent Lender from exercising standard foreclosure remedies; and (iv) render the entire Construction Facility unsecurable.

This condition, if incorporated into the Development Agreement as stated in the Resolution, would make the Loan impossible to close.

Borrower must resolve this issue with the City prior to Loan closing. Lender suggests the following alternatives for Borrower to propose to the City as substitute clawback mechanisms that would satisfy the City\'s legitimate interest in ensuring timely completion while preserving Borrower\'s fee simple absolute title and Lender\'s first-priority security interest:

> \(i\) **Contractual Repurchase Option.** A contractual option in favor of the City to repurchase the Project site, exercisable only upon (A) Borrower\'s failure to achieve Substantial Completion of Phase I within the agreed timeframe, (B) expiration of all cure periods (including Lender\'s extended cure period), and (C) Lender\'s prior written consent or full satisfaction of the outstanding Loan balance. The repurchase price would be the lesser of the original purchase price paid by Borrower or the then-current fair market value, as determined by an independent MAI appraiser.
>
> \(ii\) **Performance Escrow / Additional Letter of Credit.** An additional escrow deposit or irrevocable standby letter of credit held by a mutually acceptable escrow agent, to be drawn upon by the City in the event of a completion failure. This mechanism converts the City\'s remedy from a real-property interest to a contractual/financial claim and preserves the integrity of Borrower\'s fee simple title.
>
> \(iii\) **Liquidated Damages.** A provision for liquidated damages payable to the City in a predetermined amount upon Borrower\'s failure to achieve timely completion, representing a reasonable pre-estimate of the City\'s actual damages. Such liquidated damages would constitute an unsecured contractual obligation of Borrower, not a lien on the property.
>
> \(iv\) **Enhanced Completion Guaranty.** An enhanced personal and/or trust guaranty from the Guarantors, guaranteeing completion of Phase I within the agreed timeframe and providing the City with a direct contractual remedy against the Guarantors (and their assets, other than the Project site) in the event of a completion failure.
>
> \(v\) **Right of First Refusal.** A right of first refusal in favor of the City with respect to any disposition of the Project site, subordinate to Lender\'s mortgage and exercisable only upon a default-related disposition (other than a foreclosure or deed-in-lieu).

In all cases, Lender\'s mortgage must remain a first-priority lien on the Project site and all improvements, and Borrower\'s fee simple title must remain absolute and indefeasible. Lender is prepared to work cooperatively with Borrower and its counsel to identify a structure that is acceptable to both Lender and the City, and Lender encourages Borrower to initiate discussions with the City on this issue at the earliest practicable date.
