**[6. Regulatory Pathway and NFR Letter Process]{.underline}**

**[6.1 IEPA Voluntary Site Remediation Program (VSRP)]{.underline}**

The IEPA Voluntary Site Remediation Program is administered by the IEPA Bureau of Land, Division of Remediation Management, under the authority of the Illinois Environmental Protection Act (415 ILCS 5/) and implementing regulations at 35 Illinois Administrative Code Part 740. The VSRP provides a structured, voluntary mechanism for investigation and remediation of contaminated properties under IEPA oversight. Enrollment in the VSRP is available to any party --- including current property owners, prospective purchasers, and other interested parties --- undertaking or planning to undertake remediation of contaminated real property in Illinois.

The VSRP process involves the following general steps: (1) submission of an application identifying the site, the known or suspected contamination, and the applicant; (2) IEPA review and acceptance of the application; (3) investigation (if additional investigation is warranted) and preparation of a Remedial Action Plan; (4) IEPA review and approval of the RAP; (5) implementation of the approved remedial actions; (6) post-remediation verification and monitoring; and (7) submission of a Remedial Action Completion Report and application for a No Further Remediation letter. IEPA charges review fees for its oversight services under the VSRP.

Upon satisfactory completion of all remediation activities and demonstration of compliance with applicable remediation objectives established under the TACO framework (Tier 1, 2, or 3), IEPA issues a No Further Remediation (\"NFR\") letter for the site. The NFR letter provides a release from further investigation or remediation obligations under the Environmental Protection Act for the specific contamination conditions addressed, subject to the terms and conditions set forth in the letter. These terms may include requirements for ongoing maintenance of institutional controls (such as ELUCs), maintenance of engineering controls (such as engineered barriers or vapor mitigation systems), and periodic monitoring or reporting.

The NFR letter is recorded against the property and runs with the land, providing protection to subsequent owners, operators, lenders, and other parties with an interest in the property. The NFR letter is a critical component of the Lakemont Station Commons project for several reasons: (a) it satisfies City Council Resolution R-2025-017, condition number two, which requires an environmental remediation plan approved by IEPA before land conveyance; (b) it provides the basis for title insurance companies to insure the property without broad environmental exceptions; and (c) it satisfies the standard requirements of institutional construction lenders for evidence of environmental regulatory closure as a condition of financing secured by the property.

**[6.2 Anticipated Remediation Objectives]{.underline}**

For **Parcel 016**, GEC recommends pursuing Tier 1 residential remediation objectives for soil, which would allow unrestricted residential use of the parcel surface following remediation. For groundwater, GEC recommends pursuing Tier 2 remediation objectives with a groundwater use restriction, given the availability of a municipal water supply in the Lakemont area and the absence of potable groundwater wells within 200 feet of the Site. The Tier 2 groundwater approach recognizes that achieving Class I groundwater quality standards for all parameters at every monitoring point may not be practicable within a reasonable timeframe, and provides for alternative groundwater remediation objectives when exposure to contaminated groundwater is effectively eliminated through institutional controls.

For **Parcel 017**, GEC recommends pursuing Tier 2 soil remediation objectives, with the engineered barrier (clean soil cap) serving as the engineering control and an ELUC serving as the institutional control. The Tier 2 approach for Parcel 017 allows the arsenic concentrations in the fill layer to remain in place, provided that the engineered barrier prevents direct contact exposure and the ELUC prevents future disturbance of the fill without proper environmental protocols.

The Tier 2 approach for both groundwater on Parcel 016 and soil on Parcel 017 requires the preparation and recording of an environmental land use control (\"ELUC\") against the property. The ELUC is a legal instrument, typically in the form of a restrictive covenant, recorded in the chain of title and enforceable by IEPA. The recording of an ELUC constitutes an encumbrance on the title to the affected property. The ELUC for Parcel 016 would restrict groundwater extraction and use on the property and would require notification to IEPA prior to any excavation or subsurface disturbance within the treated or monitored zone. The ELUC for Parcel 017 would restrict disturbance of the engineered barrier (clean soil cap) without prior notification to IEPA and implementation of an approved soil management plan.

**[7. Conceptual Site Model and Risk Assessment]{.underline}**

**[7.1 Sources, Pathways, and Receptors]{.underline}**

Based on the Phase I and Phase II ESA findings, GEC has developed the following conceptual site model identifying the contaminant sources, potential exposure pathways, and potential receptors at the Site:

**Sources of Contamination:**

> • *Parcel 016:* Former underground storage tanks (gasoline, diesel, and waste oil) and former metal finishing operations (degreasing with TCE and petroleum-based solvents). These historical operations resulted in releases of BTEX compounds and TCE to soil and groundwater.
>
> • *Parcel 017:* Imported fill material of varied and undocumented provenance, placed during grading operations in the 1970s through 1990s. The fill contains metals (primarily arsenic) at concentrations exceeding residential remediation objectives in localized areas.

**Potential Exposure Pathways:**

> • *Direct contact with soil:* Future residents, workers, visitors, and construction workers may come into direct contact with contaminated shallow soils during surface activities or subsurface excavation.
>
> • *Inhalation of vapors (vapor intrusion):* Volatile organic compounds (BTEX and TCE) in soil and shallow groundwater on Parcel 016 may volatilize and migrate through the vadose zone into occupied structures, creating an indoor air exposure pathway. This vapor intrusion pathway is a potentially significant concern for any structures constructed on or near the contaminated zone on Parcel 016.
>
> • *Leaching from soil to groundwater:* Continued leaching of contaminants from impacted soil to groundwater could sustain or expand the dissolved-phase plume if soils are not remediated.

**Potential Receptors:**

> • Future residents of the proposed mixed-use development
>
> • Future commercial tenants, employees, and patrons
>
> • Visitors to the proposed public spaces and transit facilities
>
> • Construction workers involved in site development activities, including excavation, foundation construction, and utility installation

Given the presence of BTEX and TCE in soil and shallow groundwater on Parcel 016, vapor intrusion into future occupied structures is a potential concern that warrants specific evaluation. GEC recommends that the Remedial Action Plan for Parcel 016 include a comprehensive vapor intrusion evaluation, including soil gas sampling within the proposed building footprint areas. If the vapor intrusion evaluation indicates that vapor intrusion may occur at concentrations posing a health risk, vapor mitigation measures --- such as sub-slab depressurization systems, vapor barriers, or passive venting systems --- should be incorporated into the building design for any structures constructed on or adjacent to the contaminated area on Parcel 016.

**[7.2 Risk Summary]{.underline}**

Without remediation, Parcel 016 poses unacceptable risk for residential use under IEPA TACO Tier 1 standards. Benzene and toluene concentrations in soil and benzene concentrations in groundwater substantially exceed residential remediation objectives and groundwater quality standards. The potential for vapor intrusion into future occupied structures presents an additional risk pathway that must be evaluated and, if warranted, mitigated. Parcel 017 poses a low but manageable risk associated with arsenic in shallow fill, which can be effectively addressed through the engineered barrier approach described herein. Parcel 015 presents no environmental risk above background levels and requires no remedial action.

**[8. Recommendations]{.underline}**

**[8.1 Immediate Actions]{.underline}**

GEC recommends the following immediate actions to advance the environmental remediation program for the Site:

1\. **Enroll in IEPA VSRP for Parcel 016** as soon as practicable. Early enrollment will establish the formal regulatory framework for remediation and allow Remedial Action Plan preparation to proceed in parallel with ongoing transactional due diligence and entitlement activities. The VSRP application should be submitted promptly to initiate the 60 to 90 day IEPA processing period.

2\. **Prepare a Remedial Action Plan (RAP) for Parcel 016** addressing both the soil and groundwater BTEX/TCE contamination. The RAP should include a detailed remedial work plan, implementation schedule, itemized cost estimate, contractor procurement approach, post-remediation monitoring plan, and the criteria for demonstrating achievement of remedial objectives. GEC recommends commencing RAP preparation immediately upon VSRP acceptance to minimize the overall project timeline.

3\. **Initiate engineered barrier design for Parcel 017.** The design should specify clean fill material requirements, placement and compaction standards, final grading plan, verification sampling requirements, and the ELUC language. The engineered barrier can be constructed concurrently with early site development activities, such as infrastructure installation on Parcels 015 and 017, to avoid separate mobilization costs.

4\. **Conduct a vapor intrusion screening evaluation** for Parcel 016, including the installation of soil gas sampling points in the areas of proposed building footprints on or near the BTEX/TCE plume. The vapor intrusion evaluation should be completed before building designs for Parcel 016 structures are finalized so that any necessary vapor mitigation systems can be incorporated into the design specifications.

**[8.2 Transactional Recommendations]{.underline}**

GEC recommends that the development agreement or related transactional documents between the Developer and the City of Lakemont clearly and comprehensively address the following environmental matters to avoid ambiguity or dispute during the remediation and development process:

**(a) Responsibility for Remediation Performance.** The transactional documents should clearly identify which party --- the Developer or the City of Lakemont --- will be responsible for performing or contracting for the remediation on Parcel 016 and the engineered barrier installation on Parcel 017. Given the pre-existing nature of the contamination and the City\'s ownership at the time of contamination awareness, the current property owner (the City) bears primary responsible-party status under the Illinois Environmental Protection Act. However, GEC notes that enrollment in the VSRP by a prospective purchaser is common practice in brownfield redevelopment transactions, and the parties may allocate performance responsibility through their contractual arrangements in a manner that differs from the statutory responsible-party framework. Regardless of which party performs the remediation, the transactional documents should clearly identify the party responsible for engaging and supervising the environmental consultant and remediation contractors, the party responsible for regulatory interaction with IEPA, and the party that will be the named applicant on the VSRP enrollment and NFR letter application.

**(b) Cost Allocation.** The transactional documents should clearly establish whether the Developer or the City of Lakemont bears the upfront cost of remediation --- \$2,800,000 for Parcel 016 and \$340,000 for Parcel 017, totaling \$3,140,000 based on current estimates. If the Developer is to advance remediation costs with an expectation of reimbursement from the City or from public funding sources, the reimbursement mechanism should be clearly identified and its reliability assessed. GEC understands that environmental remediation may qualify as an eligible expenditure under the Lakemont Central TIF District (established by City Ordinance No. 2019-42), but TIF reimbursement is typically structured on a pay-as-you-go basis from future tax increment revenues, meaning the Developer would need to fund remediation costs upfront and await reimbursement over time as tax increment is generated. The timing and amount of TIF reimbursement depend on the pace of development, assessed property values, and local tax rates, none of which can be guaranteed at the time of remediation expenditure.

**(c) Cost Overrun Provisions.** The transactional documents should establish a clear mechanism for allocating costs that exceed the current estimates. GEC\'s estimates include an internal contingency of approximately 25 percent, but actual costs could exceed the base estimate by an additional 25 to 35 percent in a worst-case scenario involving expanded excavation scope, extended groundwater treatment, or supplemental regulatory requirements. The total cost exposure (base estimate plus maximum contingency) could reach approximately \$3.8 to \$4.2 million for Parcel 016 remediation alone. The parties should agree in advance on how cost overruns above the budgeted amount will be funded and who bears the financial risk of such overruns.

**(d) Timeline Integration.** The 14 to 18 month remediation timeline for Parcel 016 must be carefully integrated with the project\'s development schedule and the land conveyance timeline. Delays in initiating remediation, unexpected expansion of the remedial scope, extended monitoring requirements, or delays in IEPA\'s processing of the NFR letter application could each individually or in combination delay the issuance of the NFR letter and, consequently, delay the conveyance of Parcel 016 and the commencement of construction on that parcel. The transactional documents should identify the consequences of remediation delays for the project\'s Phase I groundbreaking deadline of March 1, 2026, and should establish whether delay in NFR letter issuance constitutes a force majeure event, a basis for deadline extension, or a potential default by one or both parties.

GEC further recommends that the parties consider whether a phased conveyance approach --- conveying Parcels 015 and 017 to the Developer prior to completion of Parcel 016 remediation --- is feasible given the environmental conditions of the respective parcels. Parcel 015 requires no remediation and presents no environmental impediment to immediate conveyance. Parcel 017 requires only the relatively straightforward engineered barrier, which can be completed within approximately three to four months and does not involve complex regulatory processes comparable to the Parcel 016 VSRP program. A phased conveyance could allow Phase I construction to begin on the clean portions of the Site while Parcel 016 remediation proceeds concurrently, thereby reducing the risk of project-wide delay attributable to the Parcel 016 remediation timeline. However, a phased conveyance approach would require: (i) that the PD-TOD rezoning and parcel consolidation can accommodate phased delivery, or that the individual parcels can receive separate or interim land use approvals; (ii) resolution of any title issues specific to each parcel --- including any liens, encumbrances, or easements unique to the respective chains of title arising from the tax forfeiture (Parcel 015) and direct purchase (Parcels 016 and 017) acquisitions --- before conveyance; and (iii) coordination with the construction lender\'s requirements regarding the scope and legal description of the mortgaged premises.

**[8.3 Additional Investigation Recommendations]{.underline}**

GEC recommends the following additional investigative activities to refine the understanding of subsurface conditions and support remedial design:

**Pre-remediation plume delineation:** GEC recommends the advancement of 6 to 8 additional soil borings on the periphery of the Parcel 016 BTEX plume to refine the lateral and vertical boundaries of the excavation zone prior to finalization of the Remedial Action Plan. More precise delineation of the plume boundaries will reduce the risk of encountering unexpected contamination during excavation and will improve the reliability of the remediation cost estimate.

**Off-site groundwater investigation:** Although current monitoring data suggest that the dissolved-phase BTEX plume is contained within the boundaries of Parcel 016, GEC recommends the installation of at least two off-site groundwater monitoring points in the downgradient direction, on or adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra station property, to confirm the absence of off-site plume migration. This confirmation is important both for regulatory purposes (IEPA may require off-site delineation) and to mitigate potential third-party liability concerns. Coordination with Metra would be required to obtain access for monitoring well installation on Metra property.

**Vapor intrusion screening:** As noted in Section 8.1, GEC recommends conducting a vapor intrusion screening evaluation in accordance with IEPA vapor intrusion guidance prior to finalizing building designs for structures on Parcel 016. The screening should include the installation and sampling of soil gas probes at locations corresponding to proposed building footprints.

**[9. Limitations and Qualifications]{.underline}**

The findings, conclusions, and recommendations presented in this Phase II Environmental Site Assessment are subject to the following limitations and qualifications:

This report is based on data obtained during the specific investigation period of September 16 through October 11, 2024, and is subject to the inherent limitations of subsurface investigation techniques. Subsurface conditions between sampling locations, at depths not investigated, and at times other than the investigation period may differ from those described in this report. Environmental conditions are inherently variable across space and time, and no subsurface investigation, regardless of its scope and density, can fully characterize all conditions at a site.

The cost estimates presented in this report are opinions of probable cost based on GEC\'s professional experience and current market conditions. They are not guaranteed prices, guaranteed maximum prices, or bids for the described work. Actual costs may differ materially from the estimates provided. Cost estimates do not include costs associated with potential scope changes, regulatory changes, litigation support, or any other activities not specifically described in this report.

This report does not constitute a legal opinion regarding environmental liability, regulatory compliance, responsible-party status, cost allocation, or any other legal matter. GEC is an environmental consulting firm and does not provide legal advice. The Developer and other interested parties should consult their legal counsel regarding the legal implications of the findings presented herein.

GEC\'s findings are based on conditions as they existed at the time of the investigation. Future changes in regulatory standards, remediation objectives, site conditions, land use, or applicable law could affect the conclusions and recommendations presented in this report. GEC assumes no obligation to update this report for changes in conditions occurring after the date of the investigation.

This report was prepared in accordance with generally accepted environmental consulting practices for the type of investigation performed. No other warranty, expressed or implied, is made regarding the professional services described herein. The conclusions and recommendations in this report are based on GEC\'s professional judgment, exercised in good faith and in accordance with the standard of care applicable to environmental consulting professionals performing similar work in the same geographic area and at the same time.

**Reliance.** This report was prepared for the exclusive use and benefit of Granite City Development LLC and its authorized agents, including its legal counsel Thornfield & Pratt LLP. Any reliance on the findings or conclusions of this report by third parties --- including, without limitation, the City of Lakemont, Illinois, First Prairie National Bank, Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, or their respective counsel, agents, or representatives --- requires the prior execution of a reliance letter from Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC on terms acceptable to GEC. GEC assumes no duty, liability, or obligation to any third party that may obtain or review this report without execution of such a reliance letter.

**[10. Figures and Tables]{.underline}**

The following figures and tables are referenced throughout this report and are included in the Appendix to the full Phase II Environmental Site Assessment report. Figures and tables are summarized below for reference purposes:

**Figures:**

> • **Figure 1:** Site Location Map --- Depicts the three parcels (12-24-300-015, -016, and -017), surrounding streets, the Lakemont Central Metra station to the east, and Lakemont Station Boulevard to the north. *See Appendix.*
>
> • **Figure 2:** Soil Boring Location Map --- Depicts the locations of all 47 soil borings across the three parcels, with parcel boundaries and former structure footprints shown for reference. *See Appendix.*
>
> • **Figure 3:** Groundwater Monitoring Well Locations and Potentiometric Surface Map --- Depicts the locations of the 8 monitoring wells, measured groundwater elevations, and interpreted potentiometric surface showing southeasterly groundwater flow direction. *See Appendix.*
>
> • **Figure 4:** Parcel 016 --- BTEX Contamination Plume Extent (Soil) --- Depicts the horizontal extent of BTEX contamination in soil, showing the approximately 1.8-acre plume area. *See Appendix.*
>
> • **Figure 5:** Parcel 016 --- Groundwater BTEX Plume Extent --- Depicts the horizontal extent of dissolved-phase BTEX in groundwater, showing the approximately 1.5-acre plume area. *See Appendix.*
>
> • **Figure 6:** Parcel 017 --- Fill Area and Metals Exceedance Locations --- Depicts the approximately 3.2-acre fill area and the locations of arsenic exceedances. *See Appendix.*
>
> • **Figure 7:** Proposed Remediation Areas --- Parcel 016 --- Depicts the proposed excavation zone, in-situ treatment zone, and monitoring well network for the Parcel 016 remediation program. *See Appendix.*

**Tables:**

> • **Table 1:** Soil Analytical Results Summary --- All Parcels (with comparison to IEPA Tier 1 residential and industrial/commercial remediation objectives). *See Appendix.*
>
> • **Table 2:** Groundwater Analytical Results Summary --- All Parcels (with comparison to IEPA Class I groundwater quality standards). *See Appendix.*
>
> • **Table 3:** Remediation Cost Estimate --- Parcel 016 (detailed breakdown). *See Appendix.*
>
> • **Table 4:** Engineered Barrier Cost Estimate --- Parcel 017 (detailed breakdown). *See Appendix.*

**[11. Certification]{.underline}**

I hereby certify that this Phase II Environmental Site Assessment was conducted under my direct supervision and in accordance with ASTM Standard E1903-19, *Standard Practice for Environmental Site Assessments: Phase II Environmental Site Assessment Process*, and applicable Illinois Environmental Protection Agency guidance documents. The findings and recommendations set forth herein represent my professional opinion based on the data obtained during the investigation, analyzed and interpreted in accordance with generally accepted environmental consulting practices. The investigation was performed in a manner consistent with the standard of care exercised by environmental professionals conducting similar assessments in the State of Illinois.

 

GREENFIELD ENVIRONMENTAL CONSULTANTS LLC 450 North Michigan Avenue, Suite 1800 Chicago, Illinois 60611

**PRIVILEGED AND CONFIDENTIAL --- ATTORNEY-CLIENT COMMUNICATION / ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT**

**[MEMORANDUM]{.underline}**

**TO:** Gerald T. Fong, City Manager, City of Lakemont, Illinois

**FROM:** Rebecca A. Sinclair, Esq., Corporation Counsel, City of Lakemont

**DATE:** March 5, 2025

**RE:** Lakemont Central TIF District --- Summary of Parameters, Eligible Costs, Increment Projections, and Prevailing Wage Applicability in Connection with Proposed Lakemont Station Commons Development

**CC:** Paul R. Vasquez, Director, Community Development Department; Jonathan Ashford, Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP (outside municipal counsel)

**[I. Purpose and Background]{.underline}**

This memorandum has been prepared at the request of City Manager Gerald T. Fong in connection with the City of Lakemont\'s ongoing negotiation of a development agreement with Granite City Development LLC (\"Developer\") for the proposed Lakemont Station Commons project. On February 18, 2025, the City Council adopted Resolution R-2025-017, conditionally approving the selection of the Developer through a competitive Request for Proposals process. The City Manager and Marcus J. Pellegrini, on behalf of the Developer, executed a negotiated term sheet on January 22, 2025, outlining the preliminary parameters of the proposed public-private partnership.

The purpose of this memorandum is fourfold: (1) to provide a comprehensive summary of the Lakemont Central TIF District\'s legal parameters and remaining duration; (2) to identify eligible reimbursable project costs under the Illinois Tax Increment Allocation Redevelopment Act, 65 ILCS 5/11-74.4-3 (the \"TIF Act\"); (3) to project the tax increment available to support the \$48 million TIF reimbursement cap negotiated in the term sheet; and (4) to analyze prevailing wage obligations triggered by the various forms of public financial assistance associated with the project.

The Lakemont Station Commons project is a mixed-use, transit-oriented development on approximately 12.7 acres (Parcels 12-24-300-015, -016, and -017) located at 1400--1550 Lakemont Station Boulevard, Lakemont, Illinois 60048, immediately adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra station. The project carries a total estimated cost of approximately \$412 million, with total hard construction costs of approximately \$340 million to be delivered across four phases. The project will include residential towers, office space, retail, a hotel, structured parking, a public plaza, and a community center.

This memorandum is intended to inform the development agreement negotiations and should be reviewed in conjunction with the Phase II Environmental Site Assessment Summary prepared by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC (dated November 8, 2024) and the appraisal prepared by Crossland Appraisal Services (dated January 15, 2025, appraised value of \$18.4 million for the three subject parcels).

**[II. TIF District Establishment and Legal Framework]{.underline}**

**A. Establishing Ordinance and Statutory Authority**

The Lakemont Central TIF District was established by Ordinance No. 2019-42, adopted by the City Council on September 17, 2019, pursuant to the Illinois Tax Increment Allocation Redevelopment Act, 65 ILCS 5/11-74.4-1 *et seq.* The City of Lakemont is a home rule municipality with a population of approximately 87,000, exercising home rule powers under Article VII, Section 6 of the Illinois Constitution of 1970, in addition to the statutory TIF authority conferred by the TIF Act. The TIF district was designated on the basis of findings that the redevelopment project area qualified as a conservation area under the eligibility criteria then applicable under the TIF Act, including findings relating to obsolescence, deterioration, inadequate utilities, and environmental contamination. The Lakemont Central TIF District encompasses the project site and a number of surrounding parcels, including the area immediately adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra commuter rail station.

**B. District Boundaries and Included Parcels**

The three subject parcels --- 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017 --- are located within the Lakemont Central TIF District boundaries. These parcels are currently owned by the City, having been acquired through tax forfeiture proceedings in 2016 (Parcels -015 and -016) and by direct purchase in 2018 (Parcel -017). The parcels are presently zoned M-2 (General Manufacturing) and will require rezoning to PD-TOD (Planned Development--Transit Oriented District) as required by Condition No. 5 of Resolution R-2025-017. The three parcels have a combined acreage of approximately 12.7 acres (553,212 square feet) and constitute the former Lakemont Ironworks manufacturing campus, which ceased operations in 2009. The parcels are to be consolidated into a single legal parcel prior to or concurrent with the land conveyance to the Developer under the forthcoming development agreement.

**C. TIF District Duration and Expiration**

**The Lakemont Central TIF District expires on December 31, 2042.** Under the TIF Act, the maximum statutory life of a TIF district is twenty-three (23) years from the date of the ordinance designating the redevelopment project area. Ordinance No. 2019-42 was adopted in 2019, and the 23-year statutory limit extends through 2042, consistent with the December 31, 2042 expiration date set forth in the establishing ordinance.

Upon expiration, all tax increment ceases to be allocated to the Special Tax Allocation Fund, and all overlapping taxing districts --- including Lakemont Community Unit School District 204, Lake County, and the Lakemont Park District --- resume receiving their full share of property tax revenue from parcels within the former TIF district.

This expiration date is a fixed statutory constraint that cannot be extended unilaterally by the City. An extension would require either amending state legislation to authorize a longer duration for this specific TIF district or the adoption of a new TIF district subject to entirely new eligibility findings --- a process that would take a minimum of twelve to eighteen months and would require a new demonstration that the area qualifies as a blighted, conservation, or industrial park conservation area at the time of redesignation.

As of the date of this memorandum, the remaining life of the TIF district from March 2025 is approximately seventeen (17) years and nine (9) months. As discussed at length in Section IV below, this finite window for increment collection imposes a meaningful constraint on the City\'s ability to deliver the full \$48 million TIF reimbursement cap, particularly when considered in combination with the Phase I property tax abatement and the phased construction schedule.

**[III. Eligible Redevelopment Project Costs]{.underline}**

**A. Statutory Definition of Eligible Costs**

Under 65 ILCS 5/11-74.4-3(q), \"redevelopment project costs\" eligible for TIF reimbursement include the following categories relevant to the Lakemont Station Commons project:

> 1\. Costs of studies, surveys, development plans, and professional services, including planning, engineering, architectural, legal, and environmental assessment services;
>
> 2\. Costs of land acquisition within the TIF district, including any write-down of land value conveyed below appraised fair market value;
>
> 3\. Costs of site preparation, including demolition, grading, soil stabilization, and environmental remediation;
>
> 4\. Costs of installation, repair, and construction of public infrastructure improvements, including streets, utilities, sewers, water mains, traffic signals, sidewalks, and curbs;
>
> 5\. Costs of construction of public improvements and facilities, including parking structures serving a public purpose, public plazas, and community facilities;
>
> 6\. Costs of job training and retraining programs;
>
> 7\. Financing costs, including interest on TIF obligations and capitalized interest during construction of TIF-eligible improvements; and
>
> 8\. Relocation costs (not applicable to this project).

Critically, private development costs --- including market-rate or affordable residential construction, private office and retail space, and privately operated hotel components --- are *not* eligible for TIF reimbursement. Only expenditures that fall within the enumerated statutory categories and serve a public purpose qualify for reimbursement from the Special Tax Allocation Fund.

**B. Application to Lakemont Station Commons --- Eligible Cost Categories**

The following project costs have been identified as eligible for TIF reimbursement under the statutory framework:

**Environmental Remediation (\$3,140,000).** Parcel 12-24-300-016 requires remediation of petroleum hydrocarbon contamination (BTEX compounds) at an estimated cost of \$2,800,000. Parcel 12-24-300-017 requires installation of an engineered barrier to address metals contamination at an estimated cost of \$340,000. Both are eligible under the site preparation and environmental remediation category.

**Off-Site Public Infrastructure (\$12,500,000).** The City has committed to funding the widening of Lakemont Station Boulevard, a new traffic signal at the Oak Street intersection, water main extension, and sanitary sewer upgrades. All such costs are clearly eligible as public infrastructure improvements.

**On-Site Infrastructure (\$8,300,000).** The Developer\'s on-site infrastructure expenditures --- including internal public streets, utility connections to the municipal system, and stormwater management facilities --- are eligible to the extent they serve a documented public purpose. The development agreement should require the Developer to substantiate the public character of each on-site infrastructure component.

**Public Parking Structure, Public Plaza, and Community Center (Phase IV).** The 600-space public parking structure, the 2.5-acre public plaza with landscaping and public art, and the 28,000-square-foot community center to be conveyed to the City upon completion are all eligible as construction of public improvements and facilities. The portion of the \$71 million Phase IV hard construction costs allocable to these public components will require detailed cost segregation.

**Structured Parking in Phases I and II.** The 480-space garage (Phase I) and 350-space garage (Phase II) are potentially eligible to the extent they are publicly accessible or contractually committed to public use. Further analysis of access agreements and deed restrictions may be required to confirm eligibility.

**Land Write-Down (\$9,200,000).** The difference between the \$18,400,000 appraised value (Crossland Appraisal Services, January 15, 2025) and the \$9,200,000 sale price constitutes an eligible acquisition cost write-down under the TIF Act.

**Professional Services and Planning Costs.** Costs incurred by the City in connection with project planning, legal services, engineering, and environmental review are eligible.

Total estimated eligible costs for this project are likely to exceed the \$48 million cap by a significant margin. Accordingly, the cap itself --- not the universe of eligible costs --- will be the binding constraint on TIF reimbursement. As discussed in Section IV, the actual constraint is the availability of increment revenue over the remaining TIF district life.

**C. Costs NOT Eligible for TIF Reimbursement**

The following categories of project costs are not eligible for TIF reimbursement and must be excluded from any reimbursement claim:

> • Private residential construction, whether market-rate or affordable units (though certain affordable housing incentives may be separately eligible under specific TIF Act provisions);
>
> • Private office and commercial construction;
>
> • Private retail tenant improvements;
>
> • Hotel construction and fit-out for private hotel operations;
>
> • Developer\'s profit, general overhead, or contingency allocable to private components.

This distinction must be clearly delineated in the development agreement\'s reimbursement provisions. Only documented eligible costs, supported by certified cost documentation, should be submitted for TIF reimbursement.

**[IV. Increment Projections and Reimbursement Capacity]{.underline}**

**A. Methodology and Assumptions**

Tax increment is the increase in property tax revenue generated by the assessed value of new improvements within the TIF district above the \"frozen\" base equalized assessed value (EAV) established at the time of TIF district adoption. Because the three project parcels reflect a pre-development condition --- a vacant former manufacturing campus now owned by the City --- the base EAV is relatively low, and new improvements will generate substantial incremental assessed value.

The following assumptions underlie the projections set forth below:

> • Composite property tax rate (all overlapping taxing districts): approximately \$7.50 per \$100 of EAV;
>
> • Assessment ratio: 33.33% of fair market value (standard Illinois assessment level for non-farm, non-residential property);
>
> • Phase I estimated fair market value upon completion: approximately \$155 million (based on \$105 million hard construction cost plus land value and soft costs);
>
> • Phase II, III, and IV values estimated proportionally based on construction costs and market comparables;
>
> • Annual reassessment growth: 2% escalation per annum.

These projections are estimates only. Actual increment will depend on county assessor valuations, tax rates set annually by overlapping taxing districts, market conditions, and actual construction timelines.

**B. Increment Projection Table --- Phase-by-Phase Ramp-Up**

The following table presents estimated annual TIF increment from Phase I Substantial Completion through the TIF District expiration, assuming no abatement reduction:

  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
  **Tax Year**      **Phase(s) Generating Increment**      **Est. Annual Increment**   **Cumulative Increment**
  ----------------- -------------------------------------- --------------------------- --------------------------
  2029              Phase I (first full assessment year)   \$1,800,000                 \$1,800,000

  2030              Phase I                                \$2,100,000                 \$3,900,000

  2031              Phase I + Phase II (partial)           \$2,800,000                 \$6,700,000

  2032              Phase I + Phase II (full year)         \$3,400,000                 \$10,100,000

  2033              Phase I + II + Phase III (partial)     \$3,700,000                 \$13,800,000

  2034              Phase I--III + Phase IV (partial)      \$4,000,000                 \$17,800,000

  2035              All Phases (first full year)           \$4,200,000                 \$22,000,000

  2036              All Phases                             \$4,300,000                 \$26,300,000

  2037              All Phases                             \$4,400,000                 \$30,700,000

  2038              All Phases                             \$4,500,000                 \$35,200,000

  2039              All Phases                             \$4,600,000                 \$39,800,000

  2040              All Phases                             \$4,700,000                 \$44,500,000

  2041              All Phases                             \$4,800,000                 \$49,300,000

  2042              All Phases (final TIF year)            \$4,900,000                 \$54,200,000
  ---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Under this base-case projection, cumulative increment reaches approximately \$49.3 million by end of 2041 and \$54.2 million by the TIF district\'s expiration in 2042. This would exceed the \$48 million cap, suggesting that the cap is reachable under favorable conditions.

**However, this base case does not account for the Phase I property tax abatement.** The term sheet provides for a 12-year declining abatement on Phase I: 75% abatement in Years 1--4 (2029--2032), 50% in Years 5--8 (2033--2036), and 25% in Years 9--12 (2037--2040). Because the abatement reduces the assessed value of Phase I improvements for property tax purposes, it directly reduces the increment the TIF district captures from Phase I during those years. Applying the abatement to the Phase I contribution to annual increment yields substantially lower early-year figures. Under an adjusted scenario incorporating the abatement, cumulative increment through 2042 is estimated at approximately **\$38 million to \$42 million** --- potentially falling \$6 million to \$10 million short of the \$48 million cap.

Moreover, the pay-as-you-go structure means the Developer bears the timing risk. The Developer fronts all eligible costs and is reimbursed only as increment is actually generated and collected by the City. If cumulative increment falls short of the \$48 million cap by district expiration, the Developer absorbs the shortfall unless the development agreement provides an alternative mechanism.

**C. Sensitivity Analysis and Risk Factors**

Several scenarios could affect the increment projections described above:

**Upside scenarios** include: (a) higher-than-expected assessed values driven by a strong commercial and residential real estate market; (b) accelerated phasing resulting in earlier increment generation; and (c) structuring the Phase I abatement in a manner that minimizes its impact on TIF-capturable increment, if legally permissible.

**Downside scenarios** include: (a) construction delays extending Phase III and Phase IV completion beyond 2034, reducing the number of years of full-buildout increment collection before district expiration; (b) assessment appeals or challenges that reduce EAV; (c) declining market values affecting property assessments; (d) changes in the composite property tax rate; and (e) the Phase I tax abatement reducing capturable increment as described above.

Under a worst-case timing scenario, if all phases experience maximum permitted timeline extensions, including 18-month force majeure delays, Phase IV could be completed as late as 2036 or 2037, leaving only five to six years of full-buildout increment collection. At approximately \$4.2 million per year from full buildout, this would generate only \$21 million to \$25 million from the full-buildout period, plus partial increment from earlier phases --- dramatically reducing the likelihood of reaching the \$48 million cap.

It is my recommendation that the development agreement include: (a) a priority structure for TIF reimbursement, as set forth in Section VI.B below, ensuring environmental remediation and public infrastructure costs are reimbursed first; (b) an express acknowledgment that TIF reimbursement is not a guaranteed obligation of the City but is limited to actual increment generated and deposited in the Special Tax Allocation Fund; (c) a covenant requiring the City to use good-faith efforts to seek a TIF district extension if cumulative reimbursement is tracking materially below projections at a specified benchmark date (e.g., 2038); and (d) an express provision stating that a shortfall in TIF reimbursement does not constitute a default by the City under the development agreement. I recommend that Paul R. Vasquez coordinate with outside counsel at Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP on drafting these provisions.

**[V. Prevailing Wage Applicability]{.underline}**

**A. Statutory Framework --- Illinois Prevailing Wage Act**

The Illinois Prevailing Wage Act, 820 ILCS 130/0.01 *et seq.* (the \"PWA\"), requires the payment of prevailing wages to laborers, workers, and mechanics on all \"public works\" projects. Section 2 of the PWA defines \"public works\" as \"all fixed works constructed or demolished by any public body, or paid for wholly or in part out of public funds.\" The key operative phrase is **\"paid for wholly or in part out of public funds.\"** Illinois courts and the Illinois Department of Labor (\"IDOL\") have interpreted \"public funds\" broadly to encompass not only direct governmental expenditures but also TIF disbursements, land write-downs, tax abatements, fee waivers, and other forms of public financial assistance that reduce the cost of a private development project.

**B. Application to Lakemont Station Commons Components**

**Components clearly subject to the Prevailing Wage Act:**

> • All off-site public infrastructure improvements (\$12.5 million, City-funded) --- funded entirely by public funds and constructed for public use; unambiguously \"public works.\"
>
> • On-site infrastructure improvements (\$8.3 million, Developer-funded but partially TIF-reimbursable) --- to the extent reimbursed from TIF, these are \"paid for in part out of public funds.\"
>
> • Environmental remediation on Parcels -016 and -017 (\$3.14 million) --- if TIF-reimbursed, the remediation work is paid for in part out of public funds and triggers prevailing wage obligations.
>
> • Phase IV public parking structure, public plaza, and community center --- publicly funded improvements (TIF-reimbursable), with the community center to be conveyed to the City; clearly public works under any interpretation.

**Components where prevailing wage applicability is less clear:**

Phase I includes a residential tower and retail podium with \$105 million in hard construction costs. The Developer receives a land conveyance at a 50% discount (\$9.2 million write-down), a 12-year property tax abatement, and a permit fee waiver of \$920,000. The question is whether these incentives render Phase I construction \"paid for ... in part out of public funds.\"

IDOL has consistently taken the position that if a private project receives *any* form of public financial assistance --- including land write-downs, tax abatements, or fee waivers --- the entire project may be subject to prevailing wage requirements. While appellate case law is not entirely settled, the trend in recent decisions favors broad application of the PWA to projects receiving TIF assistance or other public subsidies. Phase II (which receives a permit fee waiver of \$480,000) presents a similar analysis. Phase III (residential and hotel) does not appear to receive direct public financial assistance beyond the overall TIF framework, though the fact that it will be constructed on land originally acquired by the Developer at a discounted price may extend prevailing wage exposure to that phase as well.

**Given the significant public financial assistance provided to this project** --- including a \$9.2 million land write-down, TIF reimbursement of up to \$48 million, a 12-year property tax abatement, and permit fee waivers totaling \$1.4 million --- **it is this office\'s recommendation that the Prevailing Wage Act likely applies to all construction activity on the project site**, not merely the TIF-reimbursed components.

An important distinction exists between (a) the statutory obligation imposed by the PWA and (b) a voluntary contractual commitment. The negotiated term sheet states that prevailing wages shall apply to \"all construction\" on the project site. If the development agreement incorporates this term, it converts a potentially debatable statutory question into an unambiguous contractual obligation. The City benefits from this clarity, ensuring prevailing wages for all workers on a City-supported project. However, the Developer should understand that this provision may increase construction costs for components --- such as market-rate residential units, private office space, and the hotel --- that might otherwise be argued to fall outside the statutory mandate.

I recommend that the development agreement specify the scope of prevailing wage applicability with precision and that outside counsel at Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP be prepared to address the Developer\'s likely arguments during negotiation regarding the scope of coverage, particularly for purely private components. The City\'s strongest position is the broad interpretation that any project receiving TIF assistance, land write-downs, or other public financial assistance triggers prevailing wage for the entire project.

**C. Prevailing Wage Monitoring and Enforcement**

The development agreement should include the following prevailing wage compliance provisions:

> • A Developer covenant requiring all general contractors and subcontractors to comply with the Prevailing Wage Act for all project construction;
>
> • Mandatory submission of certified payroll records to the City on a monthly basis;
>
> • A City right to audit contractor and subcontractor payroll records upon reasonable notice;
>
> • A Developer indemnification of the City for any claims, penalties, or liabilities arising from prevailing wage violations on the project;
>
> • Specified remedies for non-compliance, including withholding of TIF reimbursement payments.

**[VI. TIF Reimbursement Mechanics --- Pay-As-You-Go Structure]{.underline}**

**A. Reimbursement Process**

Under the negotiated terms, TIF reimbursement will be on a pay-as-you-go basis. The Developer will front all eligible costs and submit periodic reimbursement requests to the City, accompanied by certified cost documentation. The City will reimburse the Developer from the Special Tax Allocation Fund as increment is collected. Because property taxes are levied in one year and collected the following year, there is typically a twelve- to eighteen-month lag between property assessment and actual receipt of increment funds. The \$48 million cap is the maximum aggregate TIF reimbursement over the life of the TIF district. Reimbursement is not a general obligation of the City --- it is payable solely from available increment in the Special Tax Allocation Fund, and the City has no obligation to make payments from general revenue or any other source.

**B. Recommended Priority of Reimbursement**

Given the timing risk identified in Section IV, I recommend that the development agreement establish the following priority order for TIF reimbursement:

> 1\. **First Priority:** Environmental remediation costs for Parcels -016 and -017 (\$3,140,000). This prioritization aligns with the City\'s interest in ensuring contamination is addressed promptly and mitigates the City\'s indemnification exposure for pre-existing environmental conditions.
>
> 2\. **Second Priority:** Public infrastructure costs, including both the City\'s \$12.5 million commitment and the Developer\'s eligible on-site infrastructure expenditures.
>
> 3\. **Third Priority:** Public parking structure, public plaza, and community center costs associated with Phase IV.
>
> 4\. **Fourth Priority:** All other eligible costs, including professional services and financing costs.

This structure ensures that the most critical public-benefit costs are reimbursed first, even if total cumulative increment falls short of the \$48 million cap.

**[VII. Interaction with Other Public Incentives]{.underline}**

The TIF reimbursement must be understood in the context of the total public incentive package:

**Land Write-Down (\$9,200,000).** The write-down is itself an eligible TIF cost. If the City records it as a TIF expenditure from the Special Tax Allocation Fund, it counts against the \$48 million cap. I recommend that the development agreement clearly specify whether the land write-down is charged against the TIF cap or constitutes a separate incentive funded from another source.

**Property Tax Abatement (Phase I, 12-Year Declining).** As discussed in Section IV.B, the abatement reduces the assessed value of Phase I improvements, which in turn reduces the increment generated by Phase I. This creates an internal tension: the abatement benefits the Developer by reducing its property tax burden, but it simultaneously reduces the TIF increment available for Developer reimbursement. The development agreement must acknowledge this interaction explicitly.

**Permit Fee Waivers (\$1,400,000).** These are a separate incentive and should not be charged against the \$48 million TIF cap.

**City Infrastructure Commitment (\$12,500,000).** If funded from general revenue or capital improvement funds rather than TIF, this commitment does not reduce the TIF cap available for Developer reimbursement. If funded from TIF, it counts against the cap. The funding source should be specified in the development agreement.

The total incentive package --- \$9.2 million (land), up to \$48 million (TIF), \$12.5 million (infrastructure), \$1.4 million (fee waivers), plus the abatement value --- represents a substantial public investment. The development agreement should include robust clawback provisions in the event the Developer fails to deliver the project in accordance with the agreed-upon scope, timeline, and public benefit commitments.

**[VIII. Recommendations for Development Agreement Negotiations]{.underline}**

Based on the foregoing analysis, I offer the following recommendations for the City Manager and the Director of Community Development to carry into the development agreement negotiations:

**1. TIF Reimbursement Cap and Contingency.** The \$48 million cap may not be fully reachable given the December 31, 2042 district expiration and the impact of the Phase I tax abatement on increment generation. The development agreement should: (a) make TIF reimbursement expressly contingent on actual increment collected, not a guaranteed City obligation; (b) establish the reimbursement priority structure outlined in Section VI.B; (c) include a covenant requiring the City to use good-faith efforts to explore a TIF district extension if reimbursement is tracking materially below projections as of a specified benchmark date; and (d) expressly state that a TIF shortfall does not constitute a default by the City.

**2. Prevailing Wage Scope.** The development agreement should clearly delineate which construction components are subject to the Prevailing Wage Act by operation of statute and which are subject by contractual agreement pursuant to the term sheet\'s \"all construction\" language. The City should insist on prevailing wage coverage for all construction to eliminate ambiguity, but should be prepared for the Developer\'s counsel to resist the scope of coverage for purely private components. The City\'s strongest position is the broad interpretation that any project receiving TIF assistance, land write-downs, or other public funds triggers prevailing wage for the entire development.

**3. Environmental Remediation Priority.** Confirm that environmental costs (\$3.14 million) are documented as eligible TIF costs and prioritized for early reimbursement given the City\'s indemnification exposure for pre-existing contamination conditions.

**4. Abatement-TIF Interaction.** Consider whether the Phase I tax abatement can be structured to minimize its impact on TIF increment generation --- for example, by applying the abatement only to the non-TIF-captured portion of the tax bill, if legally permissible --- or, alternatively, include an express acknowledgment in the development agreement that the abatement reduces TIF reimbursement capacity and that the Developer accepts this trade-off.

**5. Outside Counsel Coordination.** Coordinate with Jonathan Ashford at Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP on drafting the TIF-related provisions of the development agreement, with particular attention to the pay-as-you-go reimbursement mechanics, eligible cost documentation requirements, and shortfall risk allocation.

**6. Certified Cost Documentation.** Require the Developer\'s auditing firm, Clearmont Accounting Group LLP, to provide certified cost documentation supporting all TIF reimbursement claims, subject to City audit rights.

**[IX. Conclusion]{.underline}**

The Lakemont Central TIF District provides a significant but finite funding mechanism for the Lakemont Station Commons project. The \$48 million reimbursement cap is supported by eligible costs well in excess of that amount, but actual reimbursement will depend on increment generation, which is constrained by the December 31, 2042 district expiration and reduced by the Phase I property tax abatement. Under a base-case scenario without abatement impact, the cap appears reachable; under an adjusted scenario incorporating the abatement, cumulative increment may fall \$6 million to \$10 million short. Prevailing wage requirements apply broadly to this project given the substantial level of public financial assistance. The development agreement must address these issues with precision and allocate risk appropriately between the City and the Developer.

I am available to discuss this memorandum in further detail and to participate in drafting sessions for the development agreement at your convenience. Please do not distribute this memorandum outside the City Manager\'s office without prior consultation with this office.

Rebecca A. Sinclair, Esq. Corporation Counsel City of Lakemont, Illinois

**[RESPONSE TO REQUEST FOR PROPOSALS]{.underline}**

**[Mixed-Use Transit-Oriented Development]{.underline}**

**[Lakemont Central Metra Station Site]{.underline}**

**[LAKEMONT STATION COMMONS]{.underline}**

 

**Submitted by:**

**GRANITE CITY DEVELOPMENT LLC** 900 West Monroe Street, Suite 2200 Chicago, Illinois 60607

 

**Submitted to:**

**City of Lakemont, Illinois** Community Development Department

Attention: Paul R. Vasquez, Director

**Primary Contact:** Marcus J. Pellegrini, Manager/CEO Telephone: (312) 555-4820
