## ARTICLE V. LAND CONVEYANCE AND COMPLETION ASSURANCE

**Section 5.1 Purchase Price.** The City shall convey the Property to the Developer for a purchase price of Nine Million Two Hundred Thousand Dollars ($9,200,000) (the "Purchase Price"), representing fifty percent (50%) of the Appraised Value. The difference between the Appraised Value and the Purchase Price (Nine Million Two Hundred Thousand Dollars ($9,200,000)) constitutes a land write-down incentive (the "Land Write-Down"). The Purchase Price shall be due and payable at closing. The Developer may fund the Purchase Price from equity, construction loan proceeds, or a combination thereof.

**Section 5.2 Closing.** Closing of the conveyance of the Property (the "Closing") shall occur within thirty (30) days following the satisfaction or waiver of all conditions precedent set forth in Article IX. The Parties' target Closing date is mid-2025, subject to the timing of rezoning, IEPA approvals, and the satisfaction of other conditions.

**Section 5.3 Permitted Exceptions.** The Permitted Exceptions shall be limited to: (i) real estate taxes not yet due and payable; (ii) standard utility and access easements that do not materially interfere with the development, use, or value of the Project; (iii) this Agreement; (iv) covenants and restrictions of the PD-TOD zoning ordinance; (v) the ELUC and institutional controls recorded in connection with the IEPA VSRP NFR Letter for Parcel 016 and the engineered barrier for Parcel 017, in each case as reviewed and approved by the Construction Lender's counsel; and (vi) such other exceptions as the Developer and the Construction Lender may approve in writing. No reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, defeasible condition, or other defeasible fee estate in favor of the City or any other party shall be a Permitted Exception.

**Section 5.4 Conditions Precedent to Closing.** The obligations of the Parties to close the conveyance of the Property are subject to the satisfaction (or waiver by the Party entitled to the benefit) of the conditions precedent set forth in Article IX.

**Section 5.5 Alternative Completion Assurance (in lieu of Reversionary Interest).**

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict (CRITICAL): Resolution R-2025-017, Condition No. (h), requires the Developer to grant the City a reversionary interest in the Property such that, if Phase I is not Substantially Complete within thirty-six (36) months of the date of conveyance, fee simple title to the Property (together with improvements) automatically reverts to the City, subject to the City refunding the Purchase Price (net of damages). The Construction Lender's Term Sheet (§4.2, §7.1(1), §12(a)) expressly prohibits any reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, or defeasible fee estate, stating that such an interest would (i) be uninsurable under standard ALTA title policies, (ii) subordinate the Construction Lender's first-priority mortgage to the City's right to reclaim the property, (iii) prevent foreclosure, and (iv) render the Construction Loan "impossible to close." The Lakeshore Side Letter (§7(d)) further provides that the Developer may not agree to any clawback, reversionary interest, right of re-entry, repurchase option, or similar mechanism without the Investor's consent, and requires terms compatible with the Construction Lender's requirements. The Developer's position—consistent with both the Construction Lender's and the Investor's requirements—is to substitute an alternative completion-assurance mechanism that satisfies the City's legitimate interest in ensuring timely Phase I completion while preserving the Developer's fee simple absolute title and the Construction Lender's first-priority security interest. The mechanism below is drafted as a contractual repurchase option plus liquidated damages, which the Construction Lender has identified as acceptable alternatives (Loan Term Sheet §12(a)(i), (iii)). This is the most significant open item and must be negotiated and confirmed with the City, the Construction Lender, and the Investor before execution.]*

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict (timing): The Resolution's thirty-six (36) month reversion clock runs from the date of conveyance. If Closing occurs in mid-2025 (as targeted), thirty-six months expires in mid-2028—before the Phase I Substantial Completion deadline of September 30, 2028. The reversion trigger and the Substantial Completion deadline are therefore internally inconsistent. The Developer's position is that any completion-assurance trigger should be tied to the Phase I Substantial Completion deadline of September 30, 2028 (as extended by Force Majeure and the cure periods in Article XIII), not to a separate thirty-six-month clock from conveyance. The City should confirm.]*

(a) **No Reversionary Interest.** The Parties acknowledge and agree that the City shall not hold, and the Developer shall not grant, any reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, fee simple determinable, fee simple subject to condition subsequent, or any other defeasible fee estate in the Property. The Developer's title to the Property shall be fee simple absolute and indefeasible, subject only to the Permitted Exceptions.

(b) **City Repurchase Option.** In lieu of any reversionary interest, and solely as a remedy for the Developer's uncured failure to achieve Substantial Completion of Phase I by September 30, 2028 (as such date may be extended pursuant to Article XIV (Force Majeure) and the cure periods in Article XIII), the City shall have a contractual option to repurchase the Property (the "Repurchase Option"), exercisable only upon all of the following: (i) the Developer's failure to achieve Phase I Substantial Completion by the deadline, as extended; (ii) the expiration of all applicable cure periods, including the Construction Lender's extended cure periods provided in Section 12.4; and (iii) the Construction Lender's prior written consent or the full satisfaction and release of the Construction Loan. The Repurchase Option shall be exercisable for a period of one hundred eighty (180) days after the satisfaction of clauses (i) through (iii). The repurchase price shall be the lesser of (A) the Purchase Price paid by the Developer, or (B) the then-current fair market value of the Property as determined by an independent MAI appraiser mutually selected by the Parties. The Repurchase Option shall be subordinate to the Construction Lender's mortgage and shall not be exercisable until the Construction Lender's lien is satisfied or the Construction Lender has consented.

(c) **Liquidated Damages Alternative.** As an alternative to the Repurchase Option, and at the Developer's election, the Developer may satisfy the City's completion-assurance objective by paying to the City liquidated damages in a predetermined amount representing a reasonable pre-estimate of the City's actual damages resulting from the failure to timely complete Phase I. The Parties acknowledge that the actual damages to the City from a completion delay would be difficult to ascertain and that the liquidated damages amount set forth in Exhibit D is a reasonable pre-estimate and not a penalty. Such liquidated damages shall constitute an unsecured contractual obligation of the Developer and shall not constitute a lien on the Property.

(d) **Performance Security.** The Developer's completion obligations are additionally secured by the Performance Security provided in Article XI. The Parties acknowledge that the Performance Security, the Repurchase Option, and the liquidated damages alternative, together with the Construction Lender's completion guaranty, collectively provide the City with substantial assurance of timely Phase I completion without impairing the Developer's fee simple title.

*[Drafting Note — Open Item: The specific liquidated damages amount (Exhibit D) and the precise mechanics of the Repurchase Option (including the appraisal procedure, the Construction Lender consent condition, and the interplay with the Construction Lender's foreclosure rights) must be finalized in consultation with the City, the Construction Lender, and the Investor. The Construction Lender has also suggested an enhanced completion guaranty and a right of first refusal as additional acceptable alternatives (Loan Term Sheet §12(a)(iv), (v)); the Developer may propose one or more of these in lieu of, or in addition to, the mechanisms above.]*

Private residential, office, retail, and hotel construction costs are not eligible.

(c) **Reimbursement Priority.** TIF Reimbursement shall be made in the following priority order: (1) environmental remediation costs for Parcels 016 and 017; (2) the Developer's eligible on-site infrastructure expenditures; (3) Phase IV public parking structure, public plaza, and community center costs; and (4) all other eligible costs, including professional services and financing costs.

The City does not guarantee that the full $48,000,000 cap will be reached. ## ARTICLE VII. ENVIRONMENTAL MATTERS

**Section 7.1 Phase II ESA; Reliance.** The Parties acknowledge the Phase II ESA prepared by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC, dated November 8, 2024, which identified: (a) Parcel 015—no contamination, no further action required; (b) Parcel 016—BTEX (benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, and xylene) contamination in soil and groundwater over approximately 1.8 acres, plus localized TCE contamination, requiring remediation under the IEPA VSRP, at an estimated cost of Two Million Eight Hundred Thousand Dollars ($2,800,000) and an estimated timeline of fourteen (14) to eighteen (18) months, culminating in an NFR Letter; and (c) Parcel 017—low-level metals contamination in shallow fill, suitable for residential use with an engineered barrier (clean fill cap) and ELUC, at an estimated cost of Three Hundred Forty Thousand Dollars ($340,000). The total estimated environmental remediation cost is Three Million One Hundred Forty Thousand Dollars ($3,140,000).

*[Drafting Note — Open Item: The Phase II ESA (§9, Reliance) was prepared for the exclusive use of the Developer and its counsel, and provides that any reliance by third parties (including the City and the Construction Lender) requires execution of a reliance letter from Greenfield on terms acceptable to Greenfield. The Developer should obtain reliance letters for the City and the Construction Lender, or the Agreement should address the City's and the Construction Lender's access to and reliance on the Phase II ESA.]*

**Section 7.2 City Indemnification for Pre-Existing Environmental Conditions.** The City shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the Developer, its members, officers, employees, agents, contractors, and successors from and against all claims, demands, actions, losses, costs, damages, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs) arising from or related to pre-existing environmental conditions on the Property (Parcels 015, 016, and 017), including but not limited to third-party claims, governmental enforcement actions, and contribution or cost-recovery actions under federal or state environmental laws. This indemnity applies to pre-existing conditions whether known or unknown, disclosed or undisclosed, and whether arising before or after conveyance, and shall include the cost of any remediation overruns attributable to pre-existing contamination. This indemnification obligation shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement and the conveyance of the Property.

*[Drafting Note — Developer's Position / Open Item: The Term Sheet (§16) provides that the City indemnifies the Developer for pre-existing environmental conditions. The Phase II ESA (§4.2.4, §5.3, §8.2) confirms that the contamination is pre-existing and attributable to the former Lakemont Ironworks operations (ceased 2009), that the City acquired the parcels with knowledge of the conditions (the City's 2018 purchase records reference a prior Phase I ESA identifying the UST farm and metal finishing operations), and that the City, as current owner, bears primary responsible-party status under the Illinois Environmental Protection Act. The ESA further warns that actual remediation costs may exceed the base estimate by 25% to 35% (total cost exposure for Parcel 016 alone could reach $3.8 to $4.2 million). The Developer's position is that the City, as the owner that created or acquired with knowledge of the pre-existing contamination, should bear all remediation costs and all cost overruns attributable to pre-existing contamination, with such costs reimbursable through TIF or direct City funding. The Lakeshore Side Letter (§4.3(d)) requires the Investor's consent to any environmental remediation obligation exceeding $3,140,000 without a corresponding increase in TIF Reimbursement or direct City funding sufficient to offset the excess. The scope of the City's indemnity (including whether it is capped, the survival period, and the treatment of overruns) is a critical open item and should be confirmed with the City and the Investor.]*

**Section 7.3 Remediation Performance and Cost Allocation.**

(a) **Performance.** The Developer shall, at the City's cost (reimbursable through TIF Reimbursement or direct City funding), perform or contract for the remediation of Parcel 016 under the IEPA VSRP and the installation of the engineered barrier on Parcel 017, using Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC or another qualified environmental consultant. The Developer shall be the named applicant on the VSRP enrollment and NFR Letter application, unless the Parties agree otherwise.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict / Open Item: Resolution R-2025-017, Condition (b), requires that the Developer submit, and the IEPA approve, a RAP for Parcel 016 prior to conveyance—indicating the Developer is responsible for the RAP. The Phase II ESA (§8.2(a)) notes that the City, as current owner, bears primary responsible-party status under the Illinois Environmental Protection Act, and that the allocation of remediation performance responsibility between the City and the Developer is a matter for the transactional documents. The Developer's position is that, while the Developer may perform the remediation work (for efficiency and schedule control), the City should bear the cost of all remediation and overruns attributable to pre-existing contamination, either through TIF Reimbursement or direct City funding. Whether the City or the Developer is the VSRP applicant, and the precise cost-allocation mechanism (including overruns), should be finalized.]*

(b) **Cost Overruns.** In the event that actual remediation costs exceed the estimated Three Million One Hundred Forty Thousand Dollars ($3,140,000), the City shall be responsible for funding the excess, either through additional TIF Reimbursement (to the extent eligible and within the cap) or direct City funding, and the Developer shall not bear the cost of overruns attributable to pre-existing contamination. The Developer shall promptly notify the City of any anticipated cost overrun and shall provide supporting documentation.

(c) **TIF Eligibility.** Environmental remediation costs are eligible TIF costs and are prioritized for early reimbursement pursuant to Section 6.2(c).

**Section 7.4 NFR Letter and Conveyance Condition.** IEPA approval of the RAP for Parcel 016 is a condition precedent to the conveyance of Parcel 016 to the Developer, as provided in Article IX. The Parties acknowledge that an NFR Letter is required prior to residential development on Parcel 016 and for insurable title and Construction Loan financing of improvements on Parcel 016. The Developer shall use commercially reasonable efforts to obtain the NFR Letter as promptly as practicable following RAP approval.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict / Open Item: Resolution R-2025-017, Condition (b), and the Term Sheet (§4) require IEPA approval of the RAP (not the NFR Letter) as a condition precedent to conveyance. The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §7.1(7)) requires, for Parcel 016, either (a) an NFR Letter, or (b) evidence of VSRP enrollment with an approved RAP plus an environmental escrow or insurance product satisfactory to the Lender sufficient to cover estimated remediation costs of $2,800,000 plus a 25% contingency ($3,500,000 total). The Phase II ESA (§5.2, §6.1) states that the NFR Letter is a prerequisite for residential development on Parcel 016 and for insurable title and lender financing. The Developer's position is that the conveyance condition should be RAP approval (consistent with the Resolution and Term Sheet), with the NFR Letter to be obtained thereafter, and that a phased conveyance (Section 2.1) should be available so that Phase I construction can commence on the clean portions of the Property while Parcel 016 remediation proceeds. The Construction Lender's alternative (VSRP enrollment + approved RAP + environmental escrow/insurance of $3.5 million) should be explored to allow earlier conveyance and financing of Parcel 016. The interplay among the conveyance condition, the NFR Letter timeline, the Construction Lender's requirements, and the Phase I groundbreaking deadline is a critical open item.]*

**Section 7.5 Environmental Insurance.** The Developer shall obtain pollution legal liability insurance in an amount not less than Ten Million Dollars ($10,000,000), naming the City and the Construction Lender as additional insureds, with a policy term covering the full construction period and a three-year extended reporting period, as required by the Construction Lender.

**Section 7.6 Vapor Intrusion.** The RAP for Parcel 016 shall include a vapor intrusion evaluation, including soil gas sampling within proposed building footprint areas on or near the BTEX/TCE plume. If the evaluation indicates a health risk, vapor mitigation measures (sub-slab depressurization, vapor barriers, or passive venting) shall be incorporated into the building design for structures on or adjacent to the contaminated area on Parcel 016. The cost of such evaluation and mitigation shall be a remediation cost borne by the City pursuant to Section 7.3.

**Section 7.7 Remediation Delays as Force Majeure.** Delays in IEPA review or approval of the VSRP application, the RAP, or the NFR Letter application, and delays in remediation attributable to the discovery of additional pre-existing contamination or to regulatory requirements beyond the Developer's reasonable control, shall constitute Force Majeure events under Article XIV to the extent they delay the Project schedule.

*[Drafting Note — Developer's Position / Open Item: The Phase II ESA (§5.2, §8.2(d)) recommends that the transactional documents address whether delay in NFR Letter issuance constitutes a Force Majeure event, a basis for deadline extension, or a potential default. The Term Sheet's Force Majeure definition (§14) includes "actions or inactions of governmental authorities (other than the City)"—which would encompass IEPA delays. The Developer's position is that IEPA/regulatory delays in RAP approval and NFR issuance, and delays from discovery of additional pre-existing contamination, should be Force Majeure events (or at minimum deadline extensions) and not Developer defaults, since the contamination is pre-existing and the regulatory process is outside the Developer's control. The City should confirm.]*

{"command": "cat >> $WORKSPACE_DIR/draft.md << 'ENDOFPART3'

## ARTICLE VIII. AFFORDABLE HOUSING

**Section 8.1 Affordable Units.** The Developer shall construct one hundred six (106) Affordable Units across the Project, representing twenty percent (20%) of the five hundred thirty (530) total residential units, distributed as follows: sixty-four (64) Affordable Units in Phase I (out of 320 total units) and forty-two (42) Affordable Units in Phase III (out of 210 total units). Affordable Units shall be restricted to households earning at or below sixty percent (60%) of AMI.

**Section 8.2 Integration.** Affordable Units shall be distributed proportionally across unit types (studio, one-bedroom, and two-bedroom) and shall not be segregated or concentrated on particular floors or in separate wings. Affordable Units shall be comparable in design, finishes, and amenity access to market-rate units within the same building, subject to reasonable variations consistent with industry practice.

**Section 8.3 Affordability Restriction.** The affordability restrictions shall remain in effect for a minimum period of thirty (30) years from the date of issuance of the certificate of occupancy for the applicable phase. The restrictions shall be memorialized by a restrictive covenant recorded against the applicable parcel and running with the land, in the form attached as Exhibit G.

**Section 8.4 Compliance.** The Developer shall comply with the City's Affordable Housing Compliance Program and shall provide annual income certification documentation to the City's Community Development Department for all Affordable Units throughout the affordability restriction period.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict: As flagged in Section 4.2, the Term Sheet (§12) refers to "at least 100 affordable units," while the Resolution (Condition (g)), the RFP Response Narrative, and the Term Sheet's own breakdown specify 106 Affordable Units (20% of 530). The Developer's position is to fix the number at 106, which satisfies the 20% minimum. The "at least 100" language should be removed to avoid ambiguity. The 30-year restriction period is consistent across the Term Sheet (§12) and the RFP Response Narrative (§6.1).]*

---

## ARTICLE IX. CONDITIONS PRECEDENT

**Section 9.1 Conditions Precedent to Execution of this Agreement.** The execution of this Agreement is subject to the satisfaction (or waiver by the Party entitled to the benefit) of the following conditions precedent:

(a) **Rezoning.** Completion of the rezoning of the Property from M-2 to PD-TOD, approved by the City Council and effective, with any applicable appeal period expired;

(b) **Parcel Consolidation.** Completion or authorization of the consolidation of the three parcels into a single parcel of record;

(c) **IEPA RAP Approval.** IEPA approval of the RAP for Parcel 016 under the VSRP;

(d) **Equity Demonstration.** The Developer's demonstration of committed equity of at least Eighty-Five Million Dollars ($85,000,000), in form and substance reasonably acceptable to the City;

(e) **City Council Approval.** Approval of this Agreement by the Lakemont City Council;

(f) **Construction Lender Confirmation.** Confirmation from the Construction Lender that this Agreement is in a form acceptable to the Construction Lender as a condition of the construction loan commitment; and

(g) **Investor Review.** Review of this Agreement by Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP in accordance with the Lakeshore Side Letter.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict / Open Item: The Resolution (Condition (a)) requires the Developer to demonstrate committed equity of at least $85,000,000 within sixty (60) days of the adoption of the Resolution (i.e., on or before April 19, 2025), verified by Clearmont Accounting Group LLP. The Term Sheet (§7) requires equity demonstration within sixty (60) days of City Council approval of the definitive Agreement. These are different triggers and deadlines. The Developer's committed equity ($51,000,000 from Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP and $34,000,000 from the Pellegrini Family Trust) is in place, so the demonstration should be satisfiable under either trigger; however, the Agreement should state a single, consistent equity-demonstration deadline and form of evidence. The Developer's interest is to align the deadline with the execution of this Agreement and to use the evidence already assembled (executed subscription/commitment letters and audited financials). The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §3, §7.1(8)) separately requires evidence of the $85,000,000 equity commitment (executed subscription agreements, fund commitment letters, and bank statements) as a condition to loan closing, on an "equity in first" basis. The timing and form of equity demonstration should be reconciled across the Resolution, the Term Sheet, and the Construction Lender's requirements.]*

*[Drafting Note — Open Item: The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §7.1) imposes additional conditions precedent to loan closing that are not conditions to execution of this Agreement but that the Developer must satisfy before the Construction Loan funds, including: an updated MAI appraisal demonstrating compliance with loan-to-value (65% max) and loan-to-cost (75% max) requirements; an ALTA lender's title policy; an ALTA/NSPS survey; environmental clearance (NFR Letter or VSRP enrollment + RAP + $3.5 million escrow/insurance for Parcel 016); the $15,000,000 performance bond/LC; all insurance policies; Phase I building permits; an executed GMP or stipulated-sum construction contract with a Lender-approved general contractor; an architect's certificate; legal opinions; organizational documents; guaranties; and a subordination/intercreditor agreement if the City holds any lien or security interest. The Developer should ensure that this Agreement's conditions precedent and the Construction Lender's conditions are harmonized and that the Developer is not required to satisfy the Construction Lender's closing conditions as conditions to executing this Agreement.]*

**Section 9.2 Conditions Precedent to Closing (Conveyance).** The obligations of the Parties to close the conveyance of the Property are subject to the satisfaction (or waiver) of the conditions in Section 9.1, together with: (a) execution of this Agreement by both Parties and approval by the City Council; (b) the Developer's posting of the Performance Security required by Article XI; and (c) delivery of the title insurance policy and survey described in Section 2.2.

**Section 9.3 Conditions Precedent to Later Phases.** The Developer's obligations to commence Phase II, Phase III, and Phase IV are subject to the Developer's satisfaction of phase-specific conditions precedent, including the availability of construction financing for the applicable phase. The Construction Lender's facility currently contemplates funding for Phase I and Phase II only; availability for Phases III and IV is subject to a separate supplemental commitment or amendment to be negotiated at the appropriate time. The Developer's failure to secure financing for a later phase shall not, in itself, constitute a default under this Agreement, provided that the Developer is pursuing financing in good faith and the applicable commencement deadline has not expired (as may be extended by Force Majeure).

*[Drafting Note — Open Item: Approximately $58,400,000 in additional debt and/or mezzanine financing is required for Phases III and IV (Term Sheet §7; Loan Term Sheet §3; Lakeshore Side Letter §1(E), §5.3, §7(a)). This financing has not yet been secured. The Construction Lender's facility covers Phase I and Phase II only (Loan Term Sheet §2.1). The Developer's interest is to ensure that the Agreement does not require later-phase financing to be in place at execution and that the Developer is not in default for failing to secure later-phase financing before the applicable commencement deadline (as extended). The Lakeshore Side Letter (§7(a)) restricts the Developer from incurring Project-level indebtedness in excess of $268,400,000 ($210,000,000 + $58,400,000) without the Investor's consent. The structure and terms of later-phase financing, and the conditions to each later phase, should be finalized.]*

---

## ARTICLE X. CONSTRUCTION STANDARDS AND REQUIREMENTS

**Section 10.1 Prevailing Wage.** All construction work performed on the Project that constitutes "public works" within the meaning of the Illinois Prevailing Wage Act, 820 ILCS 130/0.01 *et seq.* (the "Prevailing Wage Act"), including all work paid for in whole or in part out of public funds (including TIF Reimbursement, the Land Write-Down, the property tax abatement, and permit fee waivers), shall comply with the Prevailing Wage Act. The Developer shall include prevailing wage requirements in all contracts and subcontracts for such work, shall maintain certified payroll records and make them available to the City and the Illinois Department of Labor upon request, and shall post prevailing wage rate schedules at the construction site.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict / Open Item: The Term Sheet (§11.1) states that "all construction performed on the Project shall comply with the Illinois Prevailing Wage Act," which the TIF District Summary Memo (§V) notes converts a potentially debatable statutory question into an unambiguous contractual obligation covering the entire Project, including purely private components (market-rate residential, office, hotel) that might otherwise be argued to fall outside the statutory mandate. The Corporation Counsel's recommendation is that the City insist on prevailing wage coverage for "all construction" to eliminate ambiguity. The Developer's position, reflected above, is to comply with the Prevailing Wage Act to the extent it applies by operation of statute (which, given the substantial public financial assistance, the Corporation Counsel acknowledges likely extends to all construction activity on the Project site) and to the extent contractually required for TIF-reimbursed and public components, while preserving the Developer's ability to argue that purely private components not paid for with public funds are not statutorily subject to prevailing wage. The scope of prevailing wage coverage—statutory versus contractual "all construction"—is an open item that the Developer's counsel should negotiate with the City. The Developer should understand that broad prevailing wage coverage may increase construction costs for purely private components.]*

**Section 10.2 LEED Certification.** As provided in Section 4.5, all buildings shall achieve LEED Gold certification or an equivalent green building certification approved by the Director of the Community Development Department.

**Section 10.3 Construction Contracts.** The Developer shall enter into construction contracts with qualified general contractors and shall construct the Project in accordance with plans and specifications approved by the City under Section 4.4 and the Construction Lender's construction consultant. The Developer shall maintain a construction budget that includes a minimum five percent (5%) hard cost contingency for each phase.

**Section 10.4 Construction Monitoring.** The City shall have the right, upon reasonable notice, to inspect the Project site during construction to confirm compliance with this Agreement. The Construction Lender shall employ an independent construction monitor at the Developer's expense to verify work in place and compliance with the approved budget and schedule; the City may rely on the Construction Lender's construction monitoring reports to the extent made available to the City.

**Section 10.5 Compliance with Laws.** The Developer shall comply with all applicable laws, codes, and ordinances in the construction and operation of the Project, including the Prevailing Wage Act to the extent applicable, the requirements of this Agreement, and the PD-TOD zoning ordinance.

---

## ARTICLE XI. PERFORMANCE SECURITY

**Section 11.1 Phase I Performance Security.** Prior to the conveyance of the Property to the Developer, the Developer shall post a performance bond issued by Ridgepoint Surety Corporation (or another surety company reasonably acceptable to the City and the Construction Lender) or an irrevocable standby letter of credit issued by a nationally recognized financial institution acceptable to the City and the Construction Lender, in the amount of Fifteen Million Dollars ($15,000,000) (the "Phase I Performance Security"). The Phase I Performance Security shall secure the Developer's obligation to commence and substantially complete Phase I in accordance with this Agreement.

**Section 11.2 Release.** The Phase I Performance Security shall be maintained continuously from the date of conveyance until Phase I Substantial Completion, as evidenced by the issuance of a temporary or final certificate of occupancy by the City. Upon Phase I Substantial Completion, the Phase I Performance Security shall be released and returned to the Developer within thirty (30) days following written request and verification of Substantial Completion by the City.

**Section 11.3 Later-Phase Performance Security.** The amount, form, and conditions of performance security for Phase II, Phase III, and Phase IV shall be established by agreement of the Parties prior to the commencement of each such phase, in amounts reasonably related to the scope of the applicable phase and acceptable to the City, the Construction Lender, and the Investor.

*[Drafting Note — Open Item: The Term Sheet (§8) and the Lakeshore Side Letter (§4.3(e)) provide that performance security amounts for later phases are "to be established in the Development Agreement." The Developer's interest is to set later-phase security at reasonable amounts tied to phase scope, to be agreed before each phase commences, and to ensure release upon Substantial Completion of each phase. The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §4.1) requires an assignment of any performance bonds or letters of credit posted in connection with this Agreement, including the Phase I $15,000,000 performance bond. The form, draw mechanics, and assignment of the Performance Security should be finalized and attached as an exhibit.]*

---

## ARTICLE XII. ASSIGNMENT, CHANGE OF CONTROL, AND LENDER PROTECTIONS

**Section 12.1 Assignment by Developer.** The Developer may not assign this Agreement or any interest therein, in whole or in part, without the prior written consent of the City, which consent may be withheld in the City's reasonable discretion; provided, however, that the following transfers shall not require the City's prior written consent (each, a "Permitted Transfer"):

(a) **Internal Transfers.** Transfers of membership interests among the existing members of the Developer (the Pellegrini Family Trust, Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, and TransitWorks Equity Group LLC);

(b) **Estate Planning.** Transfers by Marcus J. Pellegrini for estate-planning purposes to family trusts or entities controlled by Pellegrini, provided Pellegrini retains management control;

(c) **Capital Raises.** Transfers or issuances of membership interests in connection with capital raises (including the approximately $58,400,000 in additional debt, mezzanine, or equity financing anticipated for later phases) that have been approved by Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP (pursuant to the Lakeshore Side Letter) and the City;

(d) **Controlling-Interest Affiliate.** Assignment to an entity in which Marcus J. Pellegrini (or the Pellegrini Family Trust) holds a controlling interest and which expressly assumes in writing all of the Developer's obligations under this Agreement; and

(e) **Lender Transfers.** Assignment or transfer to the Construction Lender or a Construction Lender-designee, or to a third-party purchaser at a foreclosure sale or pursuant to a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, as provided in Section 12.5.

The Developer shall provide the City with written notice of any Permitted Transfer, together with a copy of the assignment and assumption agreement, within fifteen (15) days of the effective date. Any purported assignment in violation of this Section 12.1 shall be void and of no force and effect.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict / Open Item: The Term Sheet (§10) permits assignment without City consent only to an entity in which Pellegrini (or the Pellegrini Family Trust) holds "at least a 50% controlling interest," and provides that City consent for other assignments "may be withheld in the City's reasonable discretion." The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §12(e)) requires that the Agreement permit assignment to a Lender-designee or foreclosure purchaser without the City's consent (or, if consent is required, consent not unreasonably withheld and the City responding within 30 days). The Lakeshore Side Letter (§8.3) requires the Developer to negotiate assignment and change-of-control provisions that include (i) carve-outs for internal transfers among existing members, (ii) carve-outs for capital raises approved by both the Investor and the City, (iii) a "Key Person" provision as an alternative or supplement to rigid percentage thresholds, and (iv) a reasonable cure period (not less than 60 days) before any assignment default is triggered. The Developer's position, reflected above, is to broaden the Permitted Transfers to accommodate internal transfers, estate planning, approved capital raises, and Lender transfers, and to add a Key Person provision (Section 12.3) as an alternative to rigid percentage thresholds. The City's consent standard for non-permitted assignments ("reasonable discretion" per the Term Sheet) must be reconciled with the Construction Lender's requirement that foreclosure transfers not be subject to City consent. This is a significant open item requiring coordination among the Developer, the City, the Construction Lender, and the Investor.]*

**Section 12.2 Change of Control.** A "Change of Control" shall be deemed to occur upon any of the following: (a) the Pellegrini Interest falls below fifty percent (50%) of the total membership interests in the Developer; (b) Marcus J. Pellegrini ceases to serve as Manager and Chief Executive Officer of the Developer and is not replaced by a person reasonably approved by the City (and, where required, the Investor and the Construction Lender) within sixty (60) days; (c) any single person or entity (other than the Pellegrini Family Trust, Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, or TransitWorks Equity Group LLC) acquires twenty-five percent (25%) or more of the membership interests in the Developer; or (d) the Developer merges with, consolidates into, or is acquired by another entity. A Change of Control without the City's prior written consent (and, where required, the Construction Lender's and the Investor's consent) shall constitute a material default under this Agreement, subject to the cure provisions in Article XIII.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict / Open Item: The Term Sheet (§10) and the Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §8.1) use a 50% Pellegrini Interest threshold for change-of-control/assignment restrictions (the Construction Lender's [50]% threshold is expressly "subject to further discussion and negotiation"). The Lakeshore Side Letter (§5.2) defines a Change of Control at a 51% Pellegrini Interest threshold, intended to provide the Investor with enhanced governance protections above the Term Sheet's 50% threshold. The Side Letter (§5.3) acknowledges that the approximately $58,400,000 in additional financing for later phases may require equity contributions or conversion features that could dilute the Pellegrini Interest below both the 51% and 50% thresholds, and commits the parties to work together to structure the Agreement's assignment and change-of-control provisions to accommodate internal transfers and approved capital raises. The Developer's position is to (i) use a single, consistent threshold (the Developer prefers 50% to align with the Term Sheet and the Construction Lender, while acknowledging the Investor's 51% governance threshold under the Side Letter operates at the entity level), (ii) add a Key Person provision (Section 12.3) as an alternative or supplement to the percentage threshold, and (iii) include broad Permitted Transfer carve-outs (Section 12.1) and a 60-day cure period (Section 13.3) so that dilution from approved capital raises does not automatically trigger a default. The reconciliation of the 50% and 51% thresholds, and the interaction with future capital raises, is a critical open item.]*

**Section 12.3 Key Person Provision.** The Parties acknowledge that the City's selection of the Developer was based, in substantial part, on the qualifications, experience, and financial capacity of Marcus J. Pellegrini and his affiliated entities. Accordingly, Marcus J. Pellegrini shall serve as Manager and Chief Executive Officer of the Developer and shall exercise day-to-day management authority over the Project. This Key Person provision is intended as an alternative and supplement to the percentage-of-interest thresholds in Section 12.2, such that the Developer's compliance with this Agreement is measured by Pellegrini's active and material involvement in the management of the Project, in addition to the ownership thresholds.

**Section 12.4 Construction Lender Cure Rights.** This Agreement shall provide the Construction Lender with the following protections, which the City acknowledges and agrees are necessary to enable the Construction Loan to close:

(a) **Notice of Default.** The City shall provide the Construction Lender with prompt written notice of any default by the Developer under this Agreement, concurrently with notice to the Developer.

(b) **Cure Periods.** The Construction Lender shall have the right (but not the obligation) to cure any default by the Developer under this Agreement. The Construction Lender's cure period shall be not less than thirty (30) days beyond the Developer's cure period for monetary defaults and not less than sixty (60) days beyond the Developer's cure period for non-monetary defaults, with such periods extended as necessary if the Construction Lender is diligently pursuing a cure that requires additional time (including time necessary to obtain possession of the Property through foreclosure or appointment of a receiver).

(c) **No Termination During Lender Cure.** The City shall not terminate this Agreement while the Construction Lender is diligently pursuing a cure of a Developer default within the Construction Lender's extended cure periods.

(d) **Lender Cure Costs.** The Construction Lender shall have the right to cure any default by the Developer under this Agreement on behalf of the Developer, and all cure costs (including reasonable attorneys' fees and expenses) may be added to the outstanding Construction Loan balance.

*[Drafting Note — Developer's Position / Lender Requirement: The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §12(b)) requires these cure rights as a condition to closing. The Term Sheet (§15) provides the Developer with 60-day monetary and 90-day non-monetary cure periods; the Construction Lender's extended cure periods would therefore be 90 days (monetary) and 150 days (non-monetary) beyond the Developer's cure periods. The Developer's interest is to incorporate these Lender cure rights into this Agreement so that the City cannot terminate the Agreement or exercise remedies while the Construction Lender is curing, preserving the Developer's (and the Construction Lender's) ability to protect the Project and the Construction Loan. The City should confirm its agreement to these Lender protections.]*

**Section 12.5 Foreclosure and Deed-in-Lieu Transfers.** This Agreement shall be freely assignable to the Construction Lender or a Construction Lender-designee, or to a third-party purchaser at a foreclosure sale or pursuant to a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, without the City's prior consent; provided, that if City consent is required for any such transfer, such consent shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed, and the City shall respond within thirty (30) days of request. The assignee shall assume in writing all of the Developer's obligations under this Agreement.

**Section 12.6 Lender Consent to Amendments.** No material amendment, modification, supplement, or termination of this Agreement shall be effective without the Construction Lender's prior written consent, which shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed. The Developer shall also obtain the consent of Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP for any Material Amendment (as defined in the Lakeshore Side Letter) in accordance with the Side Letter.

*[Drafting Note — Open Item: The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §12(c)) requires Lender consent to material amendments. The Lakeshore Side Letter (§4.2) requires the Investor's consent for any "Material Amendment" (defined in Side Letter §2 to include changes to the TIF cap, abatement schedule, phasing timelines, financial obligations, affordable housing requirements, assignment/change-of-control provisions, or clawback mechanisms). The Developer's interest is to ensure that the Agreement's amendment provisions accommodate both the Construction Lender's and the Investor's consent rights, while the City may resist third-party veto rights over a municipal agreement. The Developer covenants to obtain the Investor's consent for Material Amendments as a matter of entity-level obligation; the Agreement should provide that material amendments require Construction Lender consent (a Lender requirement the City must accept to enable the Construction Loan). The City's willingness to accept these third-party consent rights is an open item.]*

**Section 12.7 Subordination of City Interests.** Any lien, encumbrance, or security interest that the City may hold or acquire in the Property (other than the Permitted Exceptions, and expressly excluding any reversionary or re-entry right, which is prohibited by Section 5.5) shall be subordinated to the Construction Lender's first-priority mortgage pursuant to a subordination and intercreditor agreement in form and substance acceptable to the Construction Lender.

**Section 12.8 Collateral Assignment.** The Developer may grant to the Construction Lender a collateral assignment of this Agreement, the Developer's rights to receive TIF Reimbursement (up to $48,000,000), all governmental permits, approvals, entitlements, and development rights associated with the Project, and a pledge of the membership interests in the Developer, in each case as security for the Construction Loan. The City acknowledges and consents to such collateral assignments and pledges.

*[Drafting Note — Lender Requirement: The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §4.1) requires, as collateral, a first-priority mortgage, an assignment of leases and rents, a collateral assignment of construction contracts and development agreements (including this Agreement), a collateral assignment of TIF reimbursement rights, a collateral assignment of permits and entitlements, a pledge of 100% of the membership interests in the Developer, and an assignment of the performance bond/LC. The Developer's interest is to ensure that this Agreement expressly permits these collateral assignments to the Construction Lender, since they are conditions to the Construction Loan. The City's consent to the collateral assignment of this Agreement and the TIF reimbursement rights should be confirmed.]*

**Section 12.9 Estoppel Certificates.** The City shall deliver estoppel certificates to the Construction Lender upon reasonable request (not more than twice per calendar year), certifying the status of this Agreement, the existence or absence of any outstanding defaults by the Developer, and such other customary matters as the Construction Lender may reasonably request.

**Section 12.10 Lender Review of this Agreement.** This Agreement was delivered to the Construction Lender in draft form not less than fifteen (15) business days prior to its proposed execution date, and the Construction Lender has confirmed in writing that this Agreement is in a form acceptable to the Construction Lender.

---
ENDOFPART3

## ARTICLE VIII. AFFORDABLE HOUSING

**Section 8.1 Affordable Units.** The Developer shall construct one hundred six (106) Affordable Units across the Project, representing twenty percent (20%) of the five hundred thirty (530) total residential units, distributed as follows: sixty-four (64) Affordable Units in Phase I (out of 320 total units) and forty-two (42) Affordable Units in Phase III (out of 210 total units). Affordable Units shall be restricted to households earning at or below sixty percent (60%) of AMI.

**Section 8.2 Integration.** Affordable Units shall be distributed proportionally across unit types (studio, one-bedroom, and two-bedroom) and shall not be segregated or concentrated on particular floors or in separate wings. Affordable Units shall be comparable in design, finishes, and amenity access to market-rate units within the same building, subject to reasonable variations consistent with industry practice.

**Section 8.3 Affordability Restriction.** The affordability restrictions shall remain in effect for a minimum period of thirty (30) years from the date of issuance of the certificate of occupancy for the applicable phase. The restrictions shall be memorialized by a restrictive covenant recorded against the applicable parcel and running with the land, in the form attached as Exhibit G.

**Section 8.4 Compliance.** The Developer shall comply with the City's Affordable Housing Compliance Program and shall provide annual income certification documentation to the City's Community Development Department for all Affordable Units throughout the affordability restriction period.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict: As flagged in Section 4.2, the Term Sheet (§12) refers to "at least 100 affordable units," while the Resolution (Condition (g)), the RFP Response Narrative, and the Term Sheet's own breakdown specify 106 Affordable Units (20% of 530). The Developer's position is to fix the number at 106, which satisfies the 20% minimum. The "at least 100" language should be removed to avoid ambiguity. The 30-year restriction period is consistent across the Term Sheet (§12) and the RFP Response Narrative (§6.1).]*

---

## ARTICLE IX. CONDITIONS PRECEDENT

**Section 9.1 Conditions Precedent to Execution of this Agreement.** The execution of this Agreement is subject to the satisfaction (or waiver by the Party entitled to the benefit) of the following conditions precedent:

(a) **Rezoning.** Completion of the rezoning of the Property from M-2 to PD-TOD, approved by the City Council and effective, with any applicable appeal period expired;

(b) **Parcel Consolidation.** Completion or authorization of the consolidation of the three parcels into a single parcel of record;

(c) **IEPA RAP Approval.** IEPA approval of the RAP for Parcel 016 under the VSRP;

(d) **Equity Demonstration.** The Developer's demonstration of committed equity of at least Eighty-Five Million Dollars ($85,000,000), in form and substance reasonably acceptable to the City;

(e) **City Council Approval.** Approval of this Agreement by the Lakemont City Council;

(f) **Construction Lender Confirmation.** Confirmation from the Construction Lender that this Agreement is in a form acceptable to the Construction Lender as a condition of the construction loan commitment; and

(g) **Investor Review.** Review of this Agreement by Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP in accordance with the Lakeshore Side Letter.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict / Open Item: The Resolution (Condition (a)) requires the Developer to demonstrate committed equity of at least $85,000,000 within sixty (60) days of the adoption of the Resolution (i.e., on or before April 19, 2025), verified by Clearmont Accounting Group LLP. The Term Sheet (§7) requires equity demonstration within sixty (60) days of City Council approval of the definitive Agreement. These are different triggers and deadlines. The Developer's committed equity ($51,000,000 from Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP and $34,000,000 from the Pellegrini Family Trust) is in place, so the demonstration should be satisfiable under either trigger; however, the Agreement should state a single, consistent equity-demonstration deadline and form of evidence. The Developer's interest is to align the deadline with the execution of this Agreement and to use the evidence already assembled (executed subscription/commitment letters and audited financials). The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §3, §7.1(8)) separately requires evidence of the $85,000,000 equity commitment (executed subscription agreements, fund commitment letters, and bank statements) as a condition to loan closing, on an "equity in first" basis. The timing and form of equity demonstration should be reconciled across the Resolution, the Term Sheet, and the Construction Lender's requirements.]*

*[Drafting Note — Open Item: The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §7.1) imposes additional conditions precedent to loan closing that are not conditions to execution of this Agreement but that the Developer must satisfy before the Construction Loan funds, including: an updated MAI appraisal demonstrating compliance with loan-to-value (65% max) and loan-to-cost (75% max) requirements; an ALTA lender's title policy; an ALTA/NSPS survey; environmental clearance (NFR Letter or VSRP enrollment + RAP + $3.5 million escrow/insurance for Parcel 016); the $15,000,000 performance bond/LC; all insurance policies; Phase I building permits; an executed GMP or stipulated-sum construction contract with a Lender-approved general contractor; an architect's certificate; legal opinions; organizational documents; guaranties; and a subordination/intercreditor agreement if the City holds any lien or security interest. The Developer should ensure that this Agreement's conditions precedent and the Construction Lender's conditions are harmonized and that the Developer is not required to satisfy the Construction Lender's closing conditions as conditions to executing this Agreement.]*

**Section 9.2 Conditions Precedent to Closing (Conveyance).** The obligations of the Parties to close the conveyance of the Property are subject to the satisfaction (or waiver) of the conditions in Section 9.1, together with: (a) execution of this Agreement by both Parties and approval by the City Council; (b) the Developer's posting of the Performance Security required by Article XI; and (c) delivery of the title insurance policy and survey described in Section 2.2.

**Section 9.3 Conditions Precedent to Later Phases.** The Developer's obligations to commence Phase II, Phase III, and Phase IV are subject to the Developer's satisfaction of phase-specific conditions precedent, including the availability of construction financing for the applicable phase. The Construction Lender's facility currently contemplates funding for Phase I and Phase II only; availability for Phases III and IV is subject to a separate supplemental commitment or amendment to be negotiated at the appropriate time. The Developer's failure to secure financing for a later phase shall not, in itself, constitute a default under this Agreement, provided that the Developer is pursuing financing in good faith and the applicable commencement deadline has not expired (as may be extended by Force Majeure).

*[Drafting Note — Open Item: Approximately $58,400,000 in additional debt and/or mezzanine financing is required for Phases III and IV (Term Sheet §7; Loan Term Sheet §3; Lakeshore Side Letter §1(E), §5.3, §7(a)). This financing has not yet been secured. The Construction Lender's facility covers Phase I and Phase II only (Loan Term Sheet §2.1). The Developer's interest is to ensure that the Agreement does not require later-phase financing to be in place at execution and that the Developer is not in default for failing to secure later-phase financing before the applicable commencement deadline (as extended). The Lakeshore Side Letter (§7(a)) restricts the Developer from incurring Project-level indebtedness in excess of $268,400,000 ($210,000,000 + $58,400,000) without the Investor's consent. The structure and terms of later-phase financing, and the conditions to each later phase, should be finalized.]*

---

## ARTICLE X. CONSTRUCTION STANDARDS AND REQUIREMENTS

**Section 10.1 Prevailing Wage.** All construction work performed on the Project that constitutes "public works" within the meaning of the Illinois Prevailing Wage Act, 820 ILCS 130/0.01 *et seq.* (the "Prevailing Wage Act"), including all work paid for in whole or in part out of public funds (including TIF Reimbursement, the Land Write-Down, the property tax abatement, and permit fee waivers), shall comply with the Prevailing Wage Act. The Developer shall include prevailing wage requirements in all contracts and subcontracts for such work, shall maintain certified payroll records and make them available to the City and the Illinois Department of Labor upon request, and shall post prevailing wage rate schedules at the construction site.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict / Open Item: The Term Sheet (§11.1) states that "all construction performed on the Project shall comply with the Illinois Prevailing Wage Act," which the TIF District Summary Memo (§V) notes converts a potentially debatable statutory question into an unambiguous contractual obligation covering the entire Project, including purely private components (market-rate residential, office, hotel) that might otherwise be argued to fall outside the statutory mandate. The Corporation Counsel's recommendation is that the City insist on prevailing wage coverage for "all construction" to eliminate ambiguity. The Developer's position, reflected above, is to comply with the Prevailing Wage Act to the extent it applies by operation of statute (which, given the substantial public financial assistance, the Corporation Counsel acknowledges likely extends to all construction activity on the Project site) and to the extent contractually required for TIF-reimbursed and public components, while preserving the Developer's ability to argue that purely private components not paid for with public funds are not statutorily subject to prevailing wage. The scope of prevailing wage coverage—statutory versus contractual "all construction"—is an open item that the Developer's counsel should negotiate with the City. The Developer should understand that broad prevailing wage coverage may increase construction costs for purely private components.]*

**Section 10.2 LEED Certification.** As provided in Section 4.5, all buildings shall achieve LEED Gold certification or an equivalent green building certification approved by the Director of the Community Development Department.

**Section 10.3 Construction Contracts.** The Developer shall enter into construction contracts with qualified general contractors and shall construct the Project in accordance with plans and specifications approved by the City under Section 4.4 and the Construction Lender's construction consultant. The Developer shall maintain a construction budget that includes a minimum five percent (5%) hard cost contingency for each phase.

**Section 10.4 Construction Monitoring.** The City shall have the right, upon reasonable notice, to inspect the Project site during construction to confirm compliance with this Agreement. The Construction Lender shall employ an independent construction monitor at the Developer's expense to verify work in place and compliance with the approved budget and schedule; the City may rely on the Construction Lender's construction monitoring reports to the extent made available to the City.

**Section 10.5 Compliance with Laws.** The Developer shall comply with all applicable laws, codes, and ordinances in the construction and operation of the Project, including the Prevailing Wage Act to the extent applicable, the requirements of this Agreement, and the PD-TOD zoning ordinance.

---

## ARTICLE XI. PERFORMANCE SECURITY

**Section 11.1 Phase I Performance Security.** Prior to the conveyance of the Property to the Developer, the Developer shall post a performance bond issued by Ridgepoint Surety Corporation (or another surety company reasonably acceptable to the City and the Construction Lender) or an irrevocable standby letter of credit issued by a nationally recognized financial institution acceptable to the City and the Construction Lender, in the amount of Fifteen Million Dollars ($15,000,000) (the "Phase I Performance Security"). The Phase I Performance Security shall secure the Developer's obligation to commence and substantially complete Phase I in accordance with this Agreement.

**Section 11.2 Release.** The Phase I Performance Security shall be maintained continuously from the date of conveyance until Phase I Substantial Completion, as evidenced by the issuance of a temporary or final certificate of occupancy by the City. Upon Phase I Substantial Completion, the Phase I Performance Security shall be released and returned to the Developer within thirty (30) days following written request and verification of Substantial Completion by the City.

**Section 11.3 Later-Phase Performance Security.** The amount, form, and conditions of performance security for Phase II, Phase III, and Phase IV shall be established by agreement of the Parties prior to the commencement of each such phase, in amounts reasonably related to the scope of the applicable phase and acceptable to the City, the Construction Lender, and the Investor.

*[Drafting Note — Open Item: The Term Sheet (§8) and the Lakeshore Side Letter (§4.3(e)) provide that performance security amounts for later phases are "to be established in the Development Agreement." The Developer's interest is to set later-phase security at reasonable amounts tied to phase scope, to be agreed before each phase commences, and to ensure release upon Substantial Completion of each phase. The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §4.1) requires an assignment of any performance bonds or letters of credit posted in connection with this Agreement, including the Phase I $15,000,000 performance bond. The form, draw mechanics, and assignment of the Performance Security should be finalized and attached as an exhibit.]*

---

## ARTICLE XII. ASSIGNMENT, CHANGE OF CONTROL, AND LENDER PROTECTIONS

**Section 12.1 Assignment by Developer.** The Developer may not assign this Agreement or any interest therein, in whole or in part, without the prior written consent of the City, which consent may be withheld in the City's reasonable discretion; provided, however, that the following transfers shall not require the City's prior written consent (each, a "Permitted Transfer"):

(a) **Internal Transfers.** Transfers of membership interests among the existing members of the Developer (the Pellegrini Family Trust, Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, and TransitWorks Equity Group LLC);

(b) **Estate Planning.** Transfers by Marcus J. Pellegrini for estate-planning purposes to family trusts or entities controlled by Pellegrini, provided Pellegrini retains management control;

(c) **Capital Raises.** Transfers or issuances of membership interests in connection with capital raises (including the approximately $58,400,000 in additional debt, mezzanine, or equity financing anticipated for later phases) that have been approved by Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP (pursuant to the Lakeshore Side Letter) and the City;

(d) **Controlling-Interest Affiliate.** Assignment to an entity in which Marcus J. Pellegrini (or the Pellegrini Family Trust) holds a controlling interest and which expressly assumes in writing all of the Developer's obligations under this Agreement; and

(e) **Lender Transfers.** Assignment or transfer to the Construction Lender or a Construction Lender-designee, or to a third-party purchaser at a foreclosure sale or pursuant to a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, as provided in Section 12.5.

The Developer shall provide the City with written notice of any Permitted Transfer, together with a copy of the assignment and assumption agreement, within fifteen (15) days of the effective date. Any purported assignment in violation of this Section 12.1 shall be void and of no force and effect.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict / Open Item: The Term Sheet (§10) permits assignment without City consent only to an entity in which Pellegrini (or the Pellegrini Family Trust) holds "at least a 50% controlling interest," and provides that City consent for other assignments "may be withheld in the City's reasonable discretion." The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §12(e)) requires that the Agreement permit assignment to a Lender-designee or foreclosure purchaser without the City's consent (or, if consent is required, consent not unreasonably withheld and the City responding within 30 days). The Lakeshore Side Letter (§8.3) requires the Developer to negotiate assignment and change-of-control provisions that include (i) carve-outs for internal transfers among existing members, (ii) carve-outs for capital raises approved by both the Investor and the City, (iii) a "Key Person" provision as an alternative or supplement to rigid percentage thresholds, and (iv) a reasonable cure period (not less than 60 days) before any assignment default is triggered. The Developer's position, reflected above, is to broaden the Permitted Transfers to accommodate internal transfers, estate planning, approved capital raises, and Lender transfers, and to add a Key Person provision (Section 12.3) as an alternative to rigid percentage thresholds. The City's consent standard for non-permitted assignments ("reasonable discretion" per the Term Sheet) must be reconciled with the Construction Lender's requirement that foreclosure transfers not be subject to City consent. This is a significant open item requiring coordination among the Developer, the City, the Construction Lender, and the Investor.]*

**Section 12.2 Change of Control.** A "Change of Control" shall be deemed to occur upon any of the following: (a) the Pellegrini Interest falls below fifty percent (50%) of the total membership interests in the Developer; (b) Marcus J. Pellegrini ceases to serve as Manager and Chief Executive Officer of the Developer and is not replaced by a person reasonably approved by the City (and, where required, the Investor and the Construction Lender) within sixty (60) days; (c) any single person or entity (other than the Pellegrini Family Trust, Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP, or TransitWorks Equity Group LLC) acquires twenty-five percent (25%) or more of the membership interests in the Developer; or (d) the Developer merges with, consolidates into, or is acquired by another entity. A Change of Control without the City's prior written consent (and, where required, the Construction Lender's and the Investor's consent) shall constitute a material default under this Agreement, subject to the cure provisions in Article XIII.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict / Open Item: The Term Sheet (§10) and the Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §8.1) use a 50% Pellegrini Interest threshold for change-of-control/assignment restrictions (the Construction Lender's [50]% threshold is expressly "subject to further discussion and negotiation"). The Lakeshore Side Letter (§5.2) defines a Change of Control at a 51% Pellegrini Interest threshold, intended to provide the Investor with enhanced governance protections above the Term Sheet's 50% threshold. The Side Letter (§5.3) acknowledges that the approximately $58,400,000 in additional financing for later phases may require equity contributions or conversion features that could dilute the Pellegrini Interest below both the 51% and 50% thresholds, and commits the parties to work together to structure the Agreement's assignment and change-of-control provisions to accommodate internal transfers and approved capital raises. The Developer's position is to (i) use a single, consistent threshold (the Developer prefers 50% to align with the Term Sheet and the Construction Lender, while acknowledging the Investor's 51% governance threshold under the Side Letter operates at the entity level), (ii) add a Key Person provision (Section 12.3) as an alternative or supplement to the percentage threshold, and (iii) include broad Permitted Transfer carve-outs (Section 12.1) and a 60-day cure period (Section 13.3) so that dilution from approved capital raises does not automatically trigger a default. The reconciliation of the 50% and 51% thresholds, and the interaction with future capital raises, is a critical open item.]*

**Section 12.3 Key Person Provision.** The Parties acknowledge that the City's selection of the Developer was based, in substantial part, on the qualifications, experience, and financial capacity of Marcus J. Pellegrini and his affiliated entities. Accordingly, Marcus J. Pellegrini shall serve as Manager and Chief Executive Officer of the Developer and shall exercise day-to-day management authority over the Project. This Key Person provision is intended as an alternative and supplement to the percentage-of-interest thresholds in Section 12.2, such that the Developer's compliance with this Agreement is measured by Pellegrini's active and material involvement in the management of the Project, in addition to the ownership thresholds.

**Section 12.4 Construction Lender Cure Rights.** This Agreement shall provide the Construction Lender with the following protections, which the City acknowledges and agrees are necessary to enable the Construction Loan to close:

(a) **Notice of Default.** The City shall provide the Construction Lender with prompt written notice of any default by the Developer under this Agreement, concurrently with notice to the Developer.

(b) **Cure Periods.** The Construction Lender shall have the right (but not the obligation) to cure any default by the Developer under this Agreement. The Construction Lender's cure period shall be not less than thirty (30) days beyond the Developer's cure period for monetary defaults and not less than sixty (60) days beyond the Developer's cure period for non-monetary defaults, with such periods extended as necessary if the Construction Lender is diligently pursuing a cure that requires additional time (including time necessary to obtain possession of the Property through foreclosure or appointment of a receiver).

(c) **No Termination During Lender Cure.** The City shall not terminate this Agreement while the Construction Lender is diligently pursuing a cure of a Developer default within the Construction Lender's extended cure periods.

(d) **Lender Cure Costs.** The Construction Lender shall have the right to cure any default by the Developer under this Agreement on behalf of the Developer, and all cure costs (including reasonable attorneys' fees and expenses) may be added to the outstanding Construction Loan balance.

*[Drafting Note — Developer's Position / Lender Requirement: The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §12(b)) requires these cure rights as a condition to closing. The Term Sheet (§15) provides the Developer with 60-day monetary and 90-day non-monetary cure periods; the Construction Lender's extended cure periods would therefore be 90 days (monetary) and 150 days (non-monetary) beyond the Developer's cure periods. The Developer's interest is to incorporate these Lender cure rights into this Agreement so that the City cannot terminate the Agreement or exercise remedies while the Construction Lender is curing, preserving the Developer's (and the Construction Lender's) ability to protect the Project and the Construction Loan. The City should confirm its agreement to these Lender protections.]*

**Section 12.5 Foreclosure and Deed-in-Lieu Transfers.** This Agreement shall be freely assignable to the Construction Lender or a Construction Lender-designee, or to a third-party purchaser at a foreclosure sale or pursuant to a deed-in-lieu of foreclosure, without the City's prior consent; provided, that if City consent is required for any such transfer, such consent shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed, and the City shall respond within thirty (30) days of request. The assignee shall assume in writing all of the Developer's obligations under this Agreement.

**Section 12.6 Lender Consent to Amendments.** No material amendment, modification, supplement, or termination of this Agreement shall be effective without the Construction Lender's prior written consent, which shall not be unreasonably withheld, conditioned, or delayed. The Developer shall also obtain the consent of Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP for any Material Amendment (as defined in the Lakeshore Side Letter) in accordance with the Side Letter.

*[Drafting Note — Open Item: The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §12(c)) requires Lender consent to material amendments. The Lakeshore Side Letter (§4.2) requires the Investor's consent for any "Material Amendment" (defined in Side Letter §2 to include changes to the TIF cap, abatement schedule, phasing timelines, financial obligations, affordable housing requirements, assignment/change-of-control provisions, or clawback mechanisms). The Developer's interest is to ensure that the Agreement's amendment provisions accommodate both the Construction Lender's and the Investor's consent rights, while the City may resist third-party veto rights over a municipal agreement. The Developer covenants to obtain the Investor's consent for Material Amendments as a matter of entity-level obligation; the Agreement should provide that material amendments require Construction Lender consent (a Lender requirement the City must accept to enable the Construction Loan). The City's willingness to accept these third-party consent rights is an open item.]*

**Section 12.7 Subordination of City Interests.** Any lien, encumbrance, or security interest that the City may hold or acquire in the Property (other than the Permitted Exceptions, and expressly excluding any reversionary or re-entry right, which is prohibited by Section 5.5) shall be subordinated to the Construction Lender's first-priority mortgage pursuant to a subordination and intercreditor agreement in form and substance acceptable to the Construction Lender.

**Section 12.8 Collateral Assignment.** The Developer may grant to the Construction Lender a collateral assignment of this Agreement, the Developer's rights to receive TIF Reimbursement (up to $48,000,000), all governmental permits, approvals, entitlements, and development rights associated with the Project, and a pledge of the membership interests in the Developer, in each case as security for the Construction Loan. The City acknowledges and consents to such collateral assignments and pledges.

*[Drafting Note — Lender Requirement: The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §4.1) requires, as collateral, a first-priority mortgage, an assignment of leases and rents, a collateral assignment of construction contracts and development agreements (including this Agreement), a collateral assignment of TIF reimbursement rights, a collateral assignment of permits and entitlements, a pledge of 100% of the membership interests in the Developer, and an assignment of the performance bond/LC. The Developer's interest is to ensure that this Agreement expressly permits these collateral assignments to the Construction Lender, since they are conditions to the Construction Loan. The City's consent to the collateral assignment of this Agreement and the TIF reimbursement rights should be confirmed.]*

**Section 12.9 Estoppel Certificates.** The City shall deliver estoppel certificates to the Construction Lender upon reasonable request (not more than twice per calendar year), certifying the status of this Agreement, the existence or absence of any outstanding defaults by the Developer, and such other customary matters as the Construction Lender may reasonably request.

**Section 12.10 Lender Review of this Agreement.** This Agreement was delivered to the Construction Lender in draft form not less than fifteen (15) business days prior to its proposed execution date, and the Construction Lender has confirmed in writing that this Agreement is in a form acceptable to the Construction Lender.
