## ARTICLE XIII. DEFAULT, CURE, AND TERMINATION

**Section 13.1 Monetary Defaults.** In the event of a monetary default by either Party (including the Developer's failure to pay the Purchase Price or the City's failure to fund its committed infrastructure obligations), the non-defaulting Party shall provide written notice specifying the nature and amount of the default. The defaulting Party shall have sixty (60) days from receipt of such notice to cure the default.

**Section 13.2 Non-Monetary Defaults.** In the event of a non-monetary default by either Party (including failure to commence construction within applicable deadlines, failure to comply with affordable housing requirements, or failure to maintain required insurance), the non-defaulting Party shall provide written notice specifying the nature of the default in reasonable detail. The defaulting Party shall have ninety (90) days from receipt of such notice to cure the default; provided, that if the nature of the default is such that it cannot reasonably be cured within ninety (90) days, the defaulting Party shall have such additional time as is reasonably necessary to effect a cure, so long as the defaulting Party has commenced cure efforts within the initial ninety (90)-day period and is diligently and continuously pursuing cure to completion. The Construction Lender's extended cure periods under Section 12.4 shall apply in addition to the foregoing.

**Section 13.3 Termination by City.** The City shall have the right to terminate this Agreement upon: (a) a material breach by the Developer that remains uncured after the expiration of the applicable cure period (including the Construction Lender's extended cure periods under Section 12.4); (b) the Developer's abandonment of the Project, evidenced by cessation of all construction activity for a period of one hundred eighty (180) consecutive days (other than cessation caused by Force Majeure events); and (c) such other events of default as may be specified in this Agreement. The City's right to terminate is expressly subject to the Construction Lender's cure rights in Section 12.4, and the City shall not terminate this Agreement while the Construction Lender is diligently pursuing a cure.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict (CRITICAL): The Term Sheet (§15) provides that the City may terminate if the Developer "fails to commence Phase I construction within 6 months following land conveyance." If Closing occurs in mid-2025 (as targeted), six months from conveyance would be late 2025 or early 2026—before the Phase I groundbreaking deadline of March 1, 2026. The six-month-from-conveyance commencement trigger is therefore internally inconsistent with the March 1, 2026 groundbreaking deadline and could cause the City to have a termination right before the Developer's actual commencement deadline has arrived. The Developer's position, reflected above, is to remove the "six months following land conveyance" trigger and to tie the commencement obligation to the March 1, 2026 groundbreaking deadline (as extended by Force Majeure and cure periods). The City should confirm. The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §12) also requires that termination of this Agreement by the City be an event of default under the Construction Loan only after the Construction Lender's cure rights have been exhausted; the Developer's position is reflected in Section 12.4(c).]*

**Section 13.4 Termination by Developer.** The Developer shall have the right to terminate this Agreement upon: (a) the City's failure to deliver clean, marketable, and insurable fee simple absolute title to the Property at Closing in accordance with Article V; (b) the City's failure to fund its committed off-site infrastructure obligations ($12,500,000) within the timelines specified in this Agreement; (c) the City's failure to complete the rezoning or parcel consolidation in accordance with Article III; (d) a condemnation or taking by eminent domain of more than fifteen percent (15%) of the Property by any entity, including the City; and (e) such other events of default as may be specified in this Agreement.

*[Drafting Note — Developer's Position / Open Item: The Term Sheet (§15) limits the Developer's termination right for condemnation to a taking "by any entity other than the City." The Developer's position is that any condemnation or taking of more than 15% of the Property—whether by the City or any other entity—should give the Developer a termination right, since a material taking would impair the Project regardless of the condemning authority. The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §6.1, default #8) treats condemnation of more than 15% of the Project site by area or value as a Loan event of default. The Developer has also added a termination right for the City's failure to complete the rezoning or parcel consolidation, since those are City obligations under Article III. The City should confirm the expanded condemnation and rezoning termination rights.]*

**Section 13.5 Remedies.** Upon termination of this Agreement by a non-defaulting Party, the non-defaulting Party shall have all remedies available at law or in equity, including damages, specific performance, and injunctive relief, subject in all cases to the dispute resolution provisions of Article XVI. The Developer's Performance Security shall be the City's sole and exclusive monetary remedy for the Developer's failure to substantially complete Phase I, except for willful misconduct or fraud, and except as otherwise provided in Section 5.5.

*[Drafting Note — Developer's Position: The Developer's interest is to limit the City's monetary remedies for a Phase I completion failure to the Performance Security (and the alternative completion-assurance mechanisms in Section 5.5), consistent with the substitution of the reversionary interest with a liquidated-damages/repurchase-option mechanism. The City may seek broader remedies; the scope of remedies upon termination is an open item.]*

---

## ARTICLE XIV. FORCE MAJEURE

**Section 14.1 Definition.** "Force Majeure" means any event beyond the reasonable control of the affected Party that prevents or materially delays the performance of its obligations, including, without limitation: acts of God, fire, flood, earthquake, hurricane, tornado, severe weather events, pandemic, epidemic, quarantine, governmental health orders, war, terrorism, civil unrest, insurrection, strikes or labor disputes (other than those caused by the affected Party's labor practices or failure to comply with applicable labor laws), industry-wide supply chain disruptions beyond the affected Party's reasonable control, governmental moratoria on construction, actions or inactions of governmental authorities (including the IEPA, but excluding the City of Lakemont), and delays in the issuance of the NFR Letter or other regulatory approvals attributable to pre-existing environmental conditions or to regulatory review beyond the Developer's reasonable control.

**Section 14.2 Effect.** Force Majeure events shall extend the applicable deadlines for commencement and Substantial Completion on a day-for-day basis for the duration of the Force Majeure event.

**Section 14.3 Aggregate Cap.** The maximum extension of time available under this Article XIV shall not exceed eighteen (18) months in the aggregate across the Project.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict: The Term Sheet (§14) provides an 18-month aggregate Force Majeure cap across the Project. The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §6.1, default #9) provides Force Majeure extensions of "up to 180 days" (six months) for a Phase I Substantial Completion default. The Construction Lender's 180-day cap is more restrictive than the Term Sheet's 18-month aggregate cap: if Phase I Substantial Completion is delayed by Force Majeure for more than 180 days, the Construction Loan would be in default even though this Agreement might permit the extension. The Developer's interest is to align the Construction Lender's Force Majeure cap with this Agreement's 18-month aggregate cap (or at least to ensure consistency), and to confirm that the Construction Lender will grant extensions consistent with this Agreement. This is an open item to be resolved with the Construction Lender.]*

**Section 14.4 Notice.** The affected Party shall provide written notice to the other Party within fifteen (15) business days of the occurrence of a Force Majeure event, describing in reasonable detail the nature of the event, its anticipated duration, and its expected impact on the Project timeline, with updated notices as circumstances change.

**Section 14.5 Mitigation.** The affected Party shall use commercially reasonable efforts to mitigate the effects of any Force Majeure event, including pursuing alternative suppliers, adjusting construction sequencing, and engaging additional contractors as reasonably available.

**Section 14.6 Exclusions.** Force Majeure shall not include: financial inability to perform, changes in market conditions, increases in the cost of labor or materials (unless attributable to an industry-wide supply chain disruption), the Developer's failure to obtain financing, or any event caused by the affected Party's negligence or willful misconduct.

---

## ARTICLE XV. INDEMNIFICATION AND INSURANCE

**Section 15.1 Mutual Indemnification.** Each Party shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the other Party, and its officers, employees, agents, and representatives, from and against all claims, demands, actions, damages, losses, costs, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs) arising from or related to the indemnifying Party's negligent acts or omissions, reckless conduct, or willful misconduct in connection with the performance of its obligations under this Agreement.

**Section 15.2 Developer Indemnification of City.** The Developer shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the City from and against all claims, demands, actions, damages, losses, costs, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) arising from or related to construction activities on the Property, including without limitation claims for personal injury, death, property damage, and workers' compensation, regardless of whether such claims are brought by the Developer's employees, contractors, subcontractors, or third parties.

**Section 15.3 City Indemnification of Developer.** As provided in Section 7.2, the City shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the Developer, its members, officers, employees, agents, contractors, and successors from and against all claims, demands, actions, losses, costs, damages, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) arising from or related to pre-existing environmental conditions on the Property, including third-party claims, governmental enforcement actions, and contribution or cost-recovery actions under federal or state environmental laws, including the cost of remediation overruns attributable to pre-existing contamination.

**Section 15.4 Survival.** The indemnification obligations in this Article XV and in Section 7.2 shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement and the conveyance of the Property.

**Section 15.5 Insurance.** The Developer shall maintain, or cause its general contractor to maintain, throughout the construction period for each phase, the following minimum insurance coverages:

(a) **Commercial General Liability (CGL):** Ten Million Dollars ($10,000,000) per occurrence and Twenty-Five Million Dollars ($25,000,000) in the aggregate, including coverage for premises/operations, products/completed operations, contractual liability, and broad-form property damage;

(b) **Umbrella/Excess Liability:** Twenty-Five Million Dollars ($25,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate, in excess of the CGL policy;

(c) **Builder's Risk:** Full replacement value of each phase under construction (Phase I: $105,000,000; Phase II: $76,000,000; and subsequent phases at their respective hard construction costs), written on an all-risk basis including coverage for fire, windstorm, vandalism, theft, flood, and earthquake (with sub-limits as appropriate);

(d) **Workers' Compensation:** Statutory limits as required by Illinois law;

(e) **Automobile Liability:** Five Million Dollars ($5,000,000) combined single limit;

(f) **Professional Liability:** Five Million Dollars ($5,000,000) per claim and in the aggregate; and

(g) **Pollution Legal Liability:** Ten Million Dollars ($10,000,000), as provided in Section 7.5.

The City of Lakemont shall be named as an additional insured on the CGL and umbrella/excess liability policies, and the Construction Lender shall be named as an additional insured and loss payee as required by the Construction Loan Documents. All policies shall be issued by carriers rated A- VII or better by A.M. Best. Certificates of insurance shall be provided to the City prior to conveyance and updated annually and upon renewal. All policies shall provide for at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice to the City and the Construction Lender of cancellation, non-renewal, or material modification.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict: The Term Sheet (§11.3) requires CGL coverage of $10,000,000 per occurrence and $20,000,000 in the aggregate, and does not require Professional Liability or Pollution Legal Liability. The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §5) requires CGL of $10,000,000 per occurrence and $25,000,000 in the aggregate, Professional Liability of $5,000,000, and Pollution Legal Liability of $10,000,000, with carriers rated A- VII or better and the Lender as additional insured/loss payee. The Developer's position, reflected above, is to adopt the Construction Lender's more comprehensive requirements (CGL aggregate of $25,000,000, plus Professional Liability and Pollution Legal Liability) so that a single insurance program satisfies both the City's and the Construction Lender's requirements. The City should confirm its acceptance of the higher CGL aggregate and the additional coverages.]*

## ARTICLE XVI. DISPUTE RESOLUTION

**Section 16.1 Negotiation.** The Parties shall first attempt to resolve any dispute arising under or related to this Agreement through good-faith negotiation between the City Manager (or designee) and the Developer's CEO (or designee) for a period of thirty (30) days following written notice of the dispute by either Party.

**Section 16.2 Mediation.** If the dispute is not resolved through negotiation within the thirty (30)-day period, the dispute shall be submitted to non-binding mediation before the Lakemont Mediation Center, or such other neutral mediator as the Parties may agree upon. The costs of mediation, including mediator fees, shall be shared equally by the Parties, and each Party shall bear its own attorneys' fees.

**Section 16.3 Binding Arbitration.** If the dispute is not resolved through mediation within sixty (60) days following submission to the mediator, the dispute shall be submitted to final and binding arbitration administered under the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association, conducted by a single arbitrator with experience in real estate development matters.

**Section 16.4 Venue and Governing Law.** All mediation and arbitration proceedings shall be conducted in Cook County or Lake County, Illinois. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Illinois, without regard to conflict-of-law principles.

**Section 16.5 Provisional Remedies.** Nothing in this Article XVI shall prevent either Party from seeking temporary restraining orders, preliminary injunctions, or other provisional judicial relief where necessary to prevent irreparable harm, pending resolution of the dispute through the procedures set forth above.

**Section 17.3 City Representations.** The City represents and warrants that: (a) it has good and marketable fee simple absolute title to the Property, free and clear of all liens, encumbrances, and adverse claims (except the Permitted Exceptions), and has the legal authority to convey the Property to the Developer; (b) the Property is located within the TIF District and TIF funds are legally available for the purposes described in this Agreement, subject to annual appropriation and the availability of increment; (c) the rezoning and parcel consolidation are within the City's authority to accomplish; and (d) the City has disclosed to the Developer all known environmental conditions on the Property, as reflected in the Phase II ESA and the City's records.

## ARTICLE XIII. DEFAULT, CURE, AND TERMINATION

**Section 13.1 Monetary Defaults.** In the event of a monetary default by either Party (including the Developer's failure to pay the Purchase Price or the City's failure to fund its committed infrastructure obligations), the non-defaulting Party shall provide written notice specifying the nature and amount of the default. The defaulting Party shall have sixty (60) days from receipt of such notice to cure the default.

**Section 13.2 Non-Monetary Defaults.** In the event of a non-monetary default by either Party (including failure to commence construction within applicable deadlines, failure to comply with affordable housing requirements, or failure to maintain required insurance), the non-defaulting Party shall provide written notice specifying the nature of the default in reasonable detail. The defaulting Party shall have ninety (90) days from receipt of such notice to cure the default; provided, that if the nature of the default is such that it cannot reasonably be cured within ninety (90) days, the defaulting Party shall have such additional time as is reasonably necessary to effect a cure, so long as the defaulting Party has commenced cure efforts within the initial ninety (90)-day period and is diligently and continuously pursuing cure to completion. The Construction Lender's extended cure periods under Section 12.4 shall apply in addition to the foregoing.

**Section 13.3 Termination by City.** The City shall have the right to terminate this Agreement upon: (a) a material breach by the Developer that remains uncured after the expiration of the applicable cure period (including the Construction Lender's extended cure periods under Section 12.4); (b) the Developer's abandonment of the Project, evidenced by cessation of all construction activity for a period of one hundred eighty (180) consecutive days (other than cessation caused by Force Majeure events); and (c) such other events of default as may be specified in this Agreement. The City's right to terminate is expressly subject to the Construction Lender's cure rights in Section 12.4, and the City shall not terminate this Agreement while the Construction Lender is diligently pursuing a cure.

**Section 15.1 Mutual Indemnification.** Each Party shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the other Party, and its officers, employees, agents, and representatives, from and against all claims, demands, actions, damages, losses, costs, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs) arising from or related to the indemnifying Party's negligent acts or omissions, reckless conduct, or willful misconduct in connection with the performance of its obligations under this Agreement.

**Section 15.2 Developer Indemnification of City.** The Developer shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the City from and against all claims, demands, actions, damages, losses, costs, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) arising from or related to construction activities on the Property, including without limitation claims for personal injury, death, property damage, and workers' compensation, regardless of whether such claims are brought by the Developer's employees, contractors, subcontractors, or third parties.

**Section 15.3 City Indemnification of Developer.** As provided in Section 7.2, the City shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the Developer, its members, officers, employees, agents, contractors, and successors from and against all claims, demands, actions, losses, costs, damages, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees) arising from or related to pre-existing environmental conditions on the Property, including third-party claims, governmental enforcement actions, and contribution or cost-recovery actions under federal or state environmental laws, including the cost of remediation overruns attributable to pre-existing contamination.

**Section 15.4 Survival.** The indemnification obligations in this Article XV and in Section 7.2 shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement and the conveyance of the Property.

**Section 15.5 Insurance.** The Developer shall maintain, or cause its general contractor to maintain, throughout the construction period for each phase, the following minimum insurance coverages:

(a) **Commercial General Liability (CGL):** Ten Million Dollars ($10,000,000) per occurrence and Twenty-Five Million Dollars ($25,000,000) in the aggregate, including coverage for premises/operations, products/completed operations, contractual liability, and broad-form property damage;

(b) **Umbrella/Excess Liability:** Twenty-Five Million Dollars ($25,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate, in excess of the CGL policy;

(c) **Builder's Risk:** Full replacement value of each phase under construction (Phase I: $105,000,000; Phase II: $76,000,000; and subsequent phases at their respective hard construction costs), written on an all-risk basis including coverage for fire, windstorm, vandalism, theft, flood, and earthquake (with sub-limits as appropriate);

(d) **Workers' Compensation:** Statutory limits as required by Illinois law;

(e) **Automobile Liability:** Five Million Dollars ($5,000,000) combined single limit;

(f) **Professional Liability:** Five Million Dollars ($5,000,000) per claim and in the aggregate; and

(g) **Pollution Legal Liability:** Ten Million Dollars ($10,000,000), as provided in Section 7.5.

The City of Lakemont shall be named as an additional insured on the CGL and umbrella/excess liability policies, and the Construction Lender shall be named as an additional insured and loss payee as required by the Construction Loan Documents. All policies shall be issued by carriers rated A- VII or better by A.M. Best. Certificates of insurance shall be provided to the City prior to conveyance and updated annually and upon renewal. All policies shall provide for at least thirty (30) days' prior written notice to the City and the Construction Lender of cancellation, non-renewal, or material modification.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict: The Term Sheet (§11.3) requires CGL coverage of $10,000,000 per occurrence and $20,000,000 in the aggregate, and does not require Professional Liability or Pollution Legal Liability. The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §5) requires CGL of $10,000,000 per occurrence and $25,000,000 in the aggregate, Professional Liability of $5,000,000, and Pollution Legal Liability of $10,000,000, with carriers rated A- VII or better and the Lender as additional insured/loss payee. The Developer's position, reflected above, is to adopt the Construction Lender's more comprehensive requirements (CGL aggregate of $25,000,000, plus Professional Liability and Pollution Legal Liability) so that a single insurance program satisfies both the City's and the Construction Lender's requirements. The City should confirm its acceptance of the higher CGL aggregate and the additional coverages.]*

---

## ARTICLE XVI. DISPUTE RESOLUTION

**Section 16.1 Negotiation.** The Parties shall first attempt to resolve any dispute arising under or related to this Agreement through good-faith negotiation between the City Manager (or designee) and the Developer's CEO (or designee) for a period of thirty (30) days following written notice of the dispute by either Party.

**Section 16.2 Mediation.** If the dispute is not resolved through negotiation within the thirty (30)-day period, the dispute shall be submitted to non-binding mediation before the Lakemont Mediation Center, or such other neutral mediator as the Parties may agree upon. The costs of mediation, including mediator fees, shall be shared equally by the Parties, and each Party shall bear its own attorneys' fees.

**Section 16.3 Binding Arbitration.** If the dispute is not resolved through mediation within sixty (60) days following submission to the mediator, the dispute shall be submitted to final and binding arbitration administered under the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association, conducted by a single arbitrator with experience in real estate development matters.

**Section 16.4 Venue and Governing Law.** All mediation and arbitration proceedings shall be conducted in Cook County or Lake County, Illinois. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Illinois, without regard to conflict-of-law principles.

**Section 16.5 Provisional Remedies.** Nothing in this Article XVI shall prevent either Party from seeking temporary restraining orders, preliminary injunctions, or other provisional judicial relief where necessary to prevent irreparable harm, pending resolution of the dispute through the procedures set forth above.

---

## ARTICLE XVII. REPRESENTATIONS AND WARRANTIES

**Section 17.1 Mutual Representations.** Each Party represents and warrants that: (a) it has full power and authority to enter into this Agreement and to perform its obligations hereunder; (b) the execution and delivery of this Agreement has been duly authorized by all necessary action; and (c) this Agreement constitutes its legal, valid, and binding obligation, enforceable in accordance with its terms.

**Section 17.2 Developer Representations.** The Developer represents and warrants that: (a) it has the financial capacity, development experience, and organizational resources necessary to complete the Project in accordance with the terms of this Agreement; (b) it has committed equity of at least $85,000,000 available for the Project; and (c) it has obtained a preliminary construction loan commitment from the Construction Lender.

**Section 17.3 City Representations.** The City represents and warrants that: (a) it has good and marketable fee simple absolute title to the Property, free and clear of all liens, encumbrances, and adverse claims (except the Permitted Exceptions), and has the legal authority to convey the Property to the Developer; (b) the Property is located within the TIF District and TIF funds are legally available for the purposes described in this Agreement, subject to annual appropriation and the availability of increment; (c) the rezoning and parcel consolidation are within the City's authority to accomplish; and (d) the City has disclosed to the Developer all known environmental conditions on the Property, as reflected in the Phase II ESA and the City's records.

*[Drafting Note — Developer's Position: The Developer's interest is to obtain robust City representations regarding title, TIF availability, the City's authority to accomplish the rezoning and consolidation, and disclosure of environmental conditions. The Term Sheet (§18.1) provides for customary representations, including the City's representation that it has good and marketable title and that TIF funds are legally available. The Developer should seek an express City representation regarding disclosure of known environmental conditions, given the pre-existing contamination and the City's knowledge of those conditions at the time of acquisition (Phase II ESA §4.2.4).]*

---

## ARTICLE XVIII. MISCELLANEOUS

**Section 18.1 Notices.** All notices, demands, requests, consents, approvals, and other communications required or permitted under this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be deemed delivered upon personal delivery, upon the date of delivery by nationally recognized overnight courier, or three (3) business days following deposit in the United States mail, registered or certified, return receipt requested, postage prepaid, addressed as follows:

**If to the Developer:**
Granite City Development LLC

Attn: Marcus J. Pellegrini, Manager and CEO
*With a copy to:*

**If to the City:**
City of Lakemont
Office of the City Manager

Lakemont, IL 60048
Attn: Gerald T. Fong, City Manager
*With a copy to:*
Office of Corporation Counsel, City of Lakemont

Lakemont, IL 60048
Attn: Rebecca A. Sinclair, Esq.
*And to:*
Ashford, Keene & Mulvaney LLP
150 North Michigan Avenue, Suite 3200
Chicago, IL 60601
Attn: Jonathan Ashford

Either Party may change its notice address by providing written notice to the other Party in accordance with this Section 18.1.

**Section 18.2 Entire Agreement.** This Agreement, together with the Exhibits hereto, constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes the Negotiated Term Sheet and all prior negotiations, representations, and understandings, whether written or oral. In the event of any conflict between this Agreement and the Negotiated Term Sheet, this Agreement shall control.

*[Drafting Note — Open Item: The Lakeshore Side Letter (§10.5) provides that, in the event of any conflict between the Side Letter and the Operating Agreement with respect to Project-related matters, the Side Letter controls. The Side Letter is an agreement between the Developer and the Investor (Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP) and is not a Party to this Agreement. The Developer should ensure that this Agreement is consistent with the Developer's obligations under the Side Letter (including the Minimum IRR commitment, consent rights, anti-dilution protections, and the restrictions on agreeing to clawback/reversionary mechanisms without Investor consent), since the Developer has covenanted in the Side Letter not to agree to terms in this Agreement that would materially and adversely affect the Investor's ability to achieve the Minimum IRR (Side Letter §3.5) or to agree to clawback mechanisms without Investor consent (Side Letter §7(d)). The Developer's counsel should confirm consistency between this Agreement and the Side Letter.]*

**Section 18.3 Amendments.** This Agreement may be amended, modified, or supplemented only by a written instrument executed by both Parties, subject to the Construction Lender's and the Investor's consent rights as provided in Section 12.6.

**Section 18.4 Severability.** If any provision of this Agreement is held to be invalid or unenforceable, the remaining provisions shall remain in full force and effect, and the Parties shall negotiate in good faith a substitute provision that most nearly reflects the Parties' original intent.

**Section 18.5 No Waiver.** No waiver of any provision of this Agreement shall be effective unless in writing and signed by the Party against whom the waiver is asserted. No failure or delay in exercising any right shall operate as a waiver.

**Section 18.6 Assignment.** This Agreement shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of the Parties and their respective successors and permitted assigns, subject to Article XII.

**Section 18.7 Counterparts.** This Agreement may be executed in any number of counterparts, each of which shall be deemed an original and all of which together shall constitute one and the same instrument. Signatures transmitted by electronic means shall be deemed original signatures.

**Section 18.8 Costs.** Each Party shall bear its own costs and expenses incurred in connection with the negotiation and preparation of this Agreement, including legal fees, consultant fees, and other professional expenses.

**Section 18.9 Confidentiality.** The terms and contents of this Agreement are confidential and shall not be disclosed by either Party to any third party, except: (a) to each Party's legal counsel, financial advisors, accountants, lenders, and equity investors, as reasonably necessary; (b) as required by applicable law or governmental regulation, including the Illinois Freedom of Information Act (5 ILCS 140/) as applicable to the City; and (c) to the extent necessary for City Council proceedings and other governmental approvals related to the Project.

**Section 18.10 Further Assurances.** Each Party shall execute and deliver such further documents and instruments and take such further actions as may be reasonably necessary to carry out the purposes of this Agreement.

result:
---TABLE CONTEXT (lines 60-95)---
*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict / Open Item: The TIF District Summary Memo (§VII) recommends that the Development Agreement specify the funding source for the City's $12,500,000 off-site infrastructure commitment, because if it is funded from TIF it counts against the $48,000,000 cap (reducing the Developer's reimbursement), whereas if it is funded from general revenue or capital improvement funds it does not. The Developer's position, reflected above, is that the City's off-site infrastructure must be funded from non-TIF sources to preserve the full $48,000,000 cap for the Developer's eligible costs. The City should confirm the funding source. The TIF memo also notes that the Developer's on-site infrastructure is eligible only to the extent it serves a documented public purpose; the Developer should structure and document the on-site infrastructure to maximize eligibility.]*

**Section 6.4 Property Tax Abatement.** The City shall provide a twelve (12)-year declining property tax abatement on Phase I improvements only, measured from the pre-development base equalized assessed value, as follows: Years 1--4, seventy-five percent (75%) abatement; Years 5--8, fifty percent (50%) abatement; Years 9--12, twenty-five percent (25%) abatement; and Year 13 and thereafter, full assessment with no abatement. No property tax abatement is provided for Phase II, Phase III, or Phase IV.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict / Open Item: The TIF District Summary Memo (§IV.B, §VIII.4) identifies an internal tension: the Phase I abatement benefits the Developer by reducing its property tax burden, but simultaneously reduces the tax increment captured by the TIF District (because the abatement reduces the assessed value of Phase I improvements), thereby reducing the TIF Reimbursement available to the Developer. The Corporation Counsel recommends that the Development Agreement either (i) structure the abatement to apply only to the non-TIF-captured portion of the tax bill (if legally permissible) to minimize the impact on TIF increment, or (ii) include an express acknowledgment that the abatement reduces TIF reimbursement capacity and that the Developer accepts this trade-off. The Developer's interest is to preserve the full economic value of both the abatement and the TIF Reimbursement; the Developer should seek to structure the abatement to minimize its impact on TIF-capturable increment if legally permissible. This is an open item. Separately, the Term Sheet (§18.4) provides that the Parties' respective positions on incentives for later phases "shall be addressed during negotiation of the definitive Agreement"; the Developer may seek abatement or other incentives for Phases II--IV, which remain open.]*

**Section 6.5 Permit Fee Waiver.** The City shall waive building permit fees for Phase I and Phase II of the Project, with an estimated combined value of One Million Four Hundred Thousand Dollars ($1,400,000) (Nine Hundred Twenty Thousand Dollars ($920,000) for Phase I and Four Hundred Eighty Thousand Dollars ($480,000) for Phase II). The permit fee waiver is a separate incentive and shall not be charged against the TIF Reimbursement cap. No permit fee waiver is contemplated for Phase III or Phase IV.

*[Drafting Note — Open Item: Incentives for Phases III and IV (abatement, fee waivers, or other) are not provided under the Term Sheet and remain open for negotiation. The Developer's interest is to preserve the ability to seek additional incentives for later phases, particularly given that the Project's public amenities (plaza, community center, public parking) are concentrated in Phase IV.]*

**Section 6.6 Summary of Public Incentives.** The public incentives are summarized in Exhibit F and consist of: the Land Write-Down ($9,200,000); TIF Reimbursement (up to $48,000,000); City off-site infrastructure funding ($12,500,000); the Phase I property tax abatement; and the permit fee waivers ($1,400,000).

---

## ARTICLE VII. ENVIRONMENTAL MATTERS

**Section 7.1 Phase II ESA; Reliance.** The Parties acknowledge the Phase II ESA prepared by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC, dated November 8, 2024, which identified: (a) Parcel 015—no contamination, no further action required; (b) Parcel 016—BTEX (benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, and xylene) contamination in soil and groundwater over approximately 1.8 acres, plus localized TCE contamination, requiring remediation under the IEPA VSRP, at an estimated cost of Two Million Eight Hundred Thousand Dollars ($2,800,000) and an estimated timeline of fourteen (14) to eighteen (18) months, culminating in an NFR Letter; and (c) Parcel 017—low-level metals contamination in shallow fill, suitable for residential use with an engineered barrier (clean fill cap) and ELUC, at an estimated cost of Three Hundred Forty Thousand Dollars ($340,000). The total estimated environmental remediation cost is Three Million One Hundred Forty Thousand Dollars ($3,140,000).

*[Drafting Note — Open Item: The Phase II ESA (§9, Reliance) was prepared for the exclusive use of the Developer and its counsel, and provides that any reliance by third parties (including the City and the Construction Lender) requires execution of a reliance letter from Greenfield on terms acceptable to Greenfield. The Developer should obtain reliance letters for the City and the Construction Lender, or the Agreement should address the City's and the Construction Lender's access to and reliance on the Phase II ESA.]*

**Section 7.2 City Indemnification for Pre-Existing Environmental Conditions.** The City shall indemnify, defend, and hold harmless the Developer, its members, officers, employees, agents, contractors, and successors from and against all claims, demands, actions, losses, costs, damages, liabilities, and expenses (including reasonable attorneys' fees and court costs) arising from or related to pre-existing environmental conditions on the Property (Parcels 015, 016, and 017), including but not limited to third-party claims, governmental enforcement actions, and contribution or cost-recovery actions under federal or state environmental laws. This indemnity applies to pre-existing conditions whether known or unknown, disclosed or undisclosed, and whether arising before or after conveyance, and shall include the cost of any remediation overruns attributable to pre-existing contamination. This indemnification obligation shall survive the expiration or termination of this Agreement and the conveyance of the Property.

*[Drafting Note — Developer's Position / Open Item: The Term Sheet (§16) provides that the City indemnifies the Developer for pre-existing environmental conditions. The Phase II ESA (§4.2.4, §5.3, §8.2) confirms that the contamination is pre-existing and attributable to the former Lakemont Ironworks operations (ceased 2009), that the City acquired the parcels with knowledge of the conditions (the City's 2018 purchase records reference a prior Phase I ESA identifying the UST farm and metal finishing operations), and that the City, as current owner, bears primary responsible-party status under the Illinois Environmental Protection Act. The ESA further warns that actual remediation costs may exceed the base estimate by 25% to 35% (total cost exposure for Parcel 016 alone could reach $3.8 to $4.2 million). The Developer's position is that the City, as the owner that created or acquired with knowledge of the pre-existing contamination, should bear all remediation costs and all cost overruns attributable to pre-existing contamination, with such costs reimbursable through TIF or direct City funding. The Lakeshore Side Letter (§4.3(d)) requires the Investor's consent to any environmental remediation obligation exceeding $3,140,000 without a corresponding increase in TIF Reimbursement or direct City funding sufficient to offset the excess. The scope of the City's indemnity (including whether it is capped, the survival period, and the treatment of overruns) is a critical open item and should be confirmed with the City and the Investor.]*

**Section 7.3 Remediation Performance and Cost Allocation.**

(a) **Performance.** The Developer shall, at the City's cost (reimbursable through TIF Reimbursement or direct City funding), perform or contract for the remediation of Parcel 016 under the IEPA VSRP and the installation of the engineered barrier on Parcel 017, using Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC or another qualified environmental consultant. The Developer shall be the named applicant on the VSRP enrollment and NFR Letter application, unless the Parties agree otherwise.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict / Open Item: Resolution R-2025-017, Condition (b), requires that the Developer submit, and the IEPA approve, a RAP for Parcel 016 prior to conveyance—indicating the Developer is responsible for the RAP. The Phase II ESA (§8.2(a)) notes that the City, as current owner, bears primary responsible-party status under the Illinois Environmental Protection Act, and that the allocation of remediation performance responsibility between the City and the Developer is a matter for the transactional documents. The Developer's position is that, while the Developer may perform the remediation work (for efficiency and schedule control), the City should bear the cost of all remediation and overruns attributable to pre-existing contamination, either through TIF Reimbursement or direct City funding. Whether the City or the Developer is the VSRP applicant, and the precise cost-allocation mechanism (including overruns), should be finalized.]*

(b) **Cost Overruns.** In the event that actual remediation costs exceed the estimated Three Million One Hundred Forty Thousand Dollars ($3,140,000), the City shall be responsible for funding the excess, either through additional TIF Reimbursement (to the extent eligible and within the cap) or direct City funding, and the Developer shall not bear the cost of overruns attributable to pre-existing contamination. The Developer shall promptly notify the City of any anticipated cost overrun and shall provide supporting documentation.

(c) **TIF Eligibility.** Environmental remediation costs are eligible TIF costs and are prioritized for early reimbursement pursuant to Section 6.2(c).

**Section 7.4 NFR Letter and Conveyance Condition.** IEPA approval of the RAP for Parcel 016 is a condition precedent to the conveyance of Parcel 016 to the Developer, as provided in Article IX. The Parties acknowledge that an NFR Letter is required prior to residential development on Parcel 016 and for insurable title and Construction Loan financing of improvements on Parcel 016. The Developer shall use commercially reasonable efforts to obtain the NFR Letter as promptly as practicable following RAP approval.

*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict / Open Item: Resolution R-2025-017, Condition (b), and the Term Sheet (§4) require IEPA approval of the RAP (not the NFR Letter) as a condition precedent to conveyance. The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §7.1(7)) requires, for Parcel 016, either (a) an NFR Letter, or (b) evidence of VSRP enrollment with an approved RAP plus an environmental escrow or insurance product satisfactory to the Lender sufficient to cover estimated remediation costs of $2,800,000 plus a 25% contingency ($3,500,000 total). The Phase II ESA (§5.2, §6.1) states that the NFR Letter is a prerequisite for residential development on Parcel 016 and for insurable title and lender financing. The Developer's position is that the conveyance condition should be RAP approval (consistent with the Resolution and Term Sheet), with the NFR Letter to be obtained thereafter, and that a phased conveyance (Section 2.1) should be available so that Phase I construction can commence on the clean portions of the Property while Parcel 016 remediation proceeds. The Construction Lender's alternative (VSRP enrollment + approved RAP + environmental escrow/insurance of $3.5 million) should be explored to allow earlier conveyance and financing of Parcel 016. The interplay among the conveyance condition, the NFR Letter timeline, the Construction Lender's requirements, and the Phase I groundbreaking deadline is a critical open item.]*

**Section 7.5 Environmental Insurance.** The Developer shall obtain pollution legal liability insurance in an amount not less than Ten Million Dollars ($10,000,000), naming the City and the Construction Lender as additional insureds, with a policy term covering the full construction period and a three-year extended reporting period, as required by the Construction Lender.

result:
14:*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict (CRITICAL): Resolution R-2025-017, Condition No. (h), requires the Developer to grant the City a reversionary interest in the Property such that, if Phase I is not Substantially Complete within thirty-six (36) months of the date of conveyance, fee simple title to the Property (together with improvements) automatically reverts to the City, subject to the City refunding the Purchase Price (net of damages). The Construction Lender's Term Sheet (§4.2, §7.1(1), §12(a)) expressly prohibits any reversionary interest, right of re-entry, possibility of reverter, or defeasible fee estate, stating that such an interest would (i) be uninsurable under standard ALTA title policies, (ii) subordinate the Construction Lender's first-priority mortgage to the City's right to reclaim the property, (iii) prevent foreclosure, and (iv) render the Construction Loan "impossible to close." The Lakeshore Side Letter (§7(d)) further provides that the Developer may not agree to any clawback, reversionary interest, right of re-entry, repurchase option, or similar mechanism without the Investor's consent, and requires terms compatible with the Construction Lender's requirements. The Developer's position—consistent with both the Construction Lender's and the Investor's requirements—is to substitute an alternative completion-assurance mechanism that satisfies the City's legitimate interest in ensuring timely Phase I completion while preserving the Developer's fee simple absolute title and the Construction Lender's first-priority security interest. The mechanism below is drafted as a contractual repurchase option plus liquidated damages, which the Construction Lender has identified as acceptable alternatives (Loan Term Sheet §12(a)(i), (iii)). This is the most significant open item and must be negotiated and confirmed with the City, the Construction Lender, and the Investor before execution.]*
16:*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict (timing): The Resolution's thirty-six (36) month reversion clock runs from the date of conveyance. If Closing occurs in mid-2025 (as targeted), thirty-six months expires in mid-2028—before the Phase I Substantial Completion deadline of September 30, 2028. The reversion trigger and the Substantial Completion deadline are therefore internally inconsistent. The Developer's position is that any completion-assurance trigger should be tied to the Phase I Substantial Completion deadline of September 30, 2028 (as extended by Force Majeure and the cure periods in Article XIII), not to a separate thirty-six-month clock from conveyance. The City should confirm.]*
20:(b) **City Repurchase Option.** In lieu of any reversionary interest, and solely as a remedy for the Developer's uncured failure to achieve Substantial Completion of Phase I by September 30, 2028 (as such date may be extended pursuant to Article XIV (Force Majeure) and the cure periods in Article XIII), the City shall have a contractual option to repurchase the Property (the "Repurchase Option"), exercisable only upon all of the following: (i) the Developer's failure to achieve Phase I Substantial Completion by the deadline, as extended; (ii) the expiration of all applicable cure periods, including the Construction Lender's extended cure periods provided in Section 12.4; and (iii) the Construction Lender's prior written consent or the full satisfaction and release of the Construction Loan. The Repurchase Option shall be exercisable for a period of one hundred eighty (180) days after the satisfaction of clauses (i) through (iii). The repurchase price shall be the lesser of (A) the Purchase Price paid by the Developer, or (B) the then-current fair market value of the Property as determined by an independent MAI appraiser mutually selected by the Parties. The Repurchase Option shall be subordinate to the Construction Lender's mortgage and shall not be exercisable until the Construction Lender's lien is satisfied or the Construction Lender has consented.
22:(c) **Liquidated Damages Alternative.** As an alternative to the Repurchase Option, and at the Developer's election, the Developer may satisfy the City's completion-assurance objective by paying to the City liquidated damages in a predetermined amount representing a reasonable pre-estimate of the City's actual damages resulting from the failure to timely complete Phase I. The Parties acknowledge that the actual damages to the City from a completion delay would be difficult to ascertain and that the liquidated damages amount set forth in Exhibit D is a reasonable pre-estimate and not a penalty. Such liquidated damages shall constitute an unsecured contractual obligation of the Developer and shall not constitute a lien on the Property.
24:(d) **Performance Security.** The Developer's completion obligations are additionally secured by the Performance Security provided in Article XI. The Parties acknowledge that the Performance Security, the Repurchase Option, and the liquidated damages alternative, together with the Construction Lender's completion guaranty, collectively provide the City with substantial assurance of timely Phase I completion without impairing the Developer's fee simple title.
42:(c) **Reimbursement Priority.** TIF Reimbursement shall be made in the following priority order: (1) environmental remediation costs for Parcels 016 and 017; (2) the Developer's eligible on-site infrastructure expenditures; (3) Phase IV public parking structure, public plaza, and community center costs; and (4) all other eligible costs, including professional services and financing costs.
44:*[Drafting Note — Developer's Position: The TIF District Summary Memo (§VI.B) recommends a reimbursement priority that places environmental remediation first (which the Developer supports, as the Developer fronts remediation costs), followed by public infrastructure. The Developer's position is that the Developer's own fronted eligible costs (remediation, on-site infrastructure, and the Phase IV public components) should be prioritized ahead of the City's off-site infrastructure commitment to the extent the City's off-site infrastructure is funded from the Special Tax Allocation Fund, so that the Developer's risk-bearing expenditures are reimbursed first. The City's $12,500,000 off-site infrastructure commitment should, in the Developer's view, be funded from the City's general or capital improvement funds rather than from TIF, so that it does not consume the $48,000,000 cap (see Section 6.3 and the open item flagged there).]*
48:*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict / Open Item: The TIF District Summary Memo (§IV.B, §IV.C, §VIII.1) projects that, under a base case without the Phase I abatement, cumulative increment reaches approximately $54.2 million by the TIF District's December 31, 2042 expiration (exceeding the $48 million cap); but under an adjusted scenario incorporating the Phase I property tax abatement, cumulative increment may be only approximately $38 million to $42 million—potentially $6 million to $10 million short of the cap. The Developer bears the timing and shortfall risk under the pay-as-you-go structure. The Corporation Counsel's recommendation is that a TIF shortfall not constitute a City default and that the City covenant only to use "good-faith efforts" to explore an extension. The Developer's position, reflected above, accepts that TIF is not a guaranteed obligation but seeks (i) a City good-faith covenant to pursue a TIF extension if reimbursement is tracking below projections, (ii) the reimbursement priority in subsection (c) to protect the Developer's most critical fronted costs, and (iii) confirmation that the Land Write-Down and the City's off-site infrastructure are not charged against the cap. The benchmark date, the definition of "materially below projections," and the consequences (if any) of the City's failure to pursue an extension in good faith should be finalized.]*
54:(a) **City Off-Site Infrastructure.** The City shall fund Twelve Million Five Hundred Thousand Dollars ($12,500,000) for off-site infrastructure improvements, including the widening of Lakemont Station Boulevard, installation of a new traffic signal at the Oak Street intersection, extension of the water main, and upgrade of sanitary sewer capacity. The City's off-site infrastructure funding shall be appropriated from the City's general fund or capital improvement funds and shall not be charged against the TIF Reimbursement cap in Section 6.2. The Construction Lender's alternative (VSRP enrollment + approved RAP + environmental escrow/insurance of $3.5 million) should be explored to allow earlier conveyance and financing of Parcel 016. The interplay among the conveyance condition, the NFR Letter timeline, the Construction Lender's requirements, and the Phase I groundbreaking deadline is a critical open item.]*
102:*[Drafting Note — Developer's Position / Open Item: The Phase II ESA (§5.2, §8.2(d)) recommends that the transactional documents address whether delay in NFR Letter issuance constitutes a Force Majeure event, a basis for deadline extension, or a potential default. The Term Sheet's Force Majeure definition (§14) includes "actions or inactions of governmental authorities (other than the City)"—which would encompass IEPA delays. The Developer's position is that IEPA/regulatory delays in RAP approval and NFR issuance, and delays from discovery of additional pre-existing contamination, should be Force Majeure events (or at minimum deadline extensions) and not Developer defaults, since the contamination is pre-existing and the regulatory process is outside the Developer's control. The City should confirm.]*
108:**Section 8.1 Affordable Units.** The Developer shall construct one hundred six (106) Affordable Units across the Project, representing twenty percent (20%) of the five hundred thirty (530) total residential units, distributed as follows: sixty-four (64) Affordable Units in Phase I (out of 320 total units) and forty-two (42) Affordable Units in Phase III (out of 210 total units). Affordable Units shall be restricted to households earning at or below sixty percent (60%) of AMI.
120:## ARTICLE IX. CONDITIONS PRECEDENT
140:*[Drafting Note — Open Item: The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §7.1) imposes additional conditions precedent to loan closing that are not conditions to execution of this Agreement but that the Developer must satisfy before the Construction Loan funds, including: an updated MAI appraisal demonstrating compliance with loan-to-value (65% max) and loan-to-cost (75% max) requirements; an ALTA lender's title policy; an ALTA/NSPS survey; environmental clearance (NFR Letter or VSRP enrollment + RAP + $3.5 million escrow/insurance for Parcel 016); the $15,000,000 performance bond/LC; all insurance policies; Phase I building permits; an executed GMP or stipulated-sum construction contract with a Lender-approved general contractor; an architect's certificate; legal opinions; organizational documents; guaranties; and a subordination/intercreditor agreement if the City holds any lien or security interest. The Developer should ensure that this Agreement's conditions precedent and the Construction Lender's conditions are harmonized and that the Developer is not required to satisfy the Construction Lender's closing conditions as conditions to executing this Agreement.]*
144:**Section 9.3 Conditions Precedent to Later Phases.** The Developer's obligations to commence Phase II, Phase III, and Phase IV are subject to the Developer's satisfaction of phase-specific conditions precedent, including the availability of construction financing for the applicable phase. The Construction Lender's facility currently contemplates funding for Phase I and Phase II only; availability for Phases III and IV is subject to a separate supplemental commitment or amendment to be negotiated at the appropriate time. The Developer's failure to secure financing for a later phase shall not, in itself, constitute a default under this Agreement, provided that the Developer is pursuing financing in good faith and the applicable commencement deadline has not expired (as may be extended by Force Majeure).
146:*[Drafting Note — Open Item: Approximately $58,400,000 in additional debt and/or mezzanine financing is required for Phases III and IV (Term Sheet §7; Loan Term Sheet §3; Lakeshore Side Letter §1(E), §5.3, §7(a)). This financing has not yet been secured. The Construction Lender's facility covers Phase I and Phase II only (Loan Term Sheet §2.1). The Developer's interest is to ensure that the Agreement does not require later-phase financing to be in place at execution and that the Developer is not in default for failing to secure later-phase financing before the applicable commencement deadline (as extended). The Lakeshore Side Letter (§7(a)) restricts the Developer from incurring Project-level indebtedness in excess of $268,400,000 ($210,000,000 + $58,400,000) without the Investor's consent. The structure and terms of later-phase financing, and the conditions to each later phase, should be finalized.]*
168:**Section 11.1 Phase I Performance Security.** Prior to the conveyance of the Property to the Developer, the Developer shall post a performance bond issued by Ridgepoint Surety Corporation (or another surety company reasonably acceptable to the City and the Construction Lender) or an irrevocable standby letter of credit issued by a nationally recognized financial institution acceptable to the City and the Construction Lender, in the amount of Fifteen Million Dollars ($15,000,000) (the "Phase I Performance Security"). The Phase I Performance Security shall secure the Developer's obligation to commence and substantially complete Phase I in accordance with this Agreement.
170:**Section 11.2 Release.** The Phase I Performance Security shall be maintained continuously from the date of conveyance until Phase I Substantial Completion, as evidenced by the issuance of a temporary or final certificate of occupancy by the City. Upon Phase I Substantial Completion, the Phase I Performance Security shall be released and returned to the Developer within thirty (30) days following written request and verification of Substantial Completion by the City.
172:**Section 11.3 Later-Phase Performance Security.** The amount, form, and conditions of performance security for Phase II, Phase III, and Phase IV shall be established by agreement of the Parties prior to the commencement of each such phase, in amounts reasonably related to the scope of the applicable phase and acceptable to the City, the Construction Lender, and the Investor.
174:*[Drafting Note — Open Item: The Term Sheet (§8) and the Lakeshore Side Letter (§4.3(e)) provide that performance security amounts for later phases are "to be established in the Development Agreement." The Developer's interest is to set later-phase security at reasonable amounts tied to phase scope, to be agreed before each phase commences, and to ensure release upon Substantial Completion of each phase. The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §4.1) requires an assignment of any performance bonds or letters of credit posted in connection with this Agreement, including the Phase I $15,000,000 performance bond. The form, draw mechanics, and assignment of the Performance Security should be finalized and attached as an exhibit.]*
240:*[Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict (CRITICAL): The Term Sheet (§15) provides that the City may terminate if the Developer "fails to commence Phase I construction within 6 months following land conveyance." If Closing occurs in mid-2025 (as targeted), six months from conveyance would be late 2025 or early 2026—before the Phase I groundbreaking deadline of March 1, 2026. The six-month-from-conveyance commencement trigger is therefore internally inconsistent with the March 1, 2026 groundbreaking deadline and could cause the City to have a termination right before the Developer's actual commencement deadline has arrived. The Developer's position, reflected above, is to remove the "six months following land conveyance" trigger and to tie the commencement obligation to the March 1, 2026 groundbreaking deadline (as extended by Force Majeure and cure periods). The City should confirm. The Construction Lender (Loan Term Sheet §12) also requires that termination of this Agreement by the City be an event of default under the Construction Loan only after the Construction Lender's cure rights have been exhausted; the Developer's position is reflected in Section 12.4(c).]*

text: Found it: at the Article IV/V boundary, the `---` is immediately followed by `## ARTICLE V` with no blank line (the `write`-created file lacked a trailing newline). Pandoc then misread the whole Article V block as a 1-column table. Let me normalize every `---` separator to have blank lines on both sides, then regenerate.

tool_calls.name: bash
tool_calls.arguments:
{"command": "cd $WORKSPACE_DIR && python3 << 'PYEOF'
# Normalize: ensure every standalone '---' line has a blank line before and after.
with open('full_draft.md') as f:
    lines = f.read().split('\n')

out = []
for i, ln in enumerate(lines):
    if ln.strip() == '---':
        # ensure blank line before
        if out and out[-1].strip() != '':
            out.append('')
        out.append('---')
        # ensure blank line after (look ahead)
        # we'll handle by checking next non-added; simpler: add blank after unless next is already blank
        # We'll just add a blank after and let duplicates collapse
        # peek next original line
        nxt = lines[i+1] if i+1 < len(lines) else ''
        if nxt.strip() != '':
            out.append('')
    else:
        # collapse multiple consecutive blanks into max one
        if ln.strip() == '' and out and out[-1].strip() == '':
            continue
        out.append(ln)

with open('full_draft.md','w') as f:
    f.write('\n'.join(out))

# Verify: no '---' immediately followed by non-blank
import re
bad = 0
for i in range(len(out)-1):
    if out[i].strip()=='---' and out[i+1].strip()!='':
        bad += 1
        print('BAD at', i, repr(out[i+1][:50]))
print('Remaining bad boundaries:', bad)
print('Total --- lines:', sum(1 for l in out if l.strip()=='---'))
PYEOF
echo "=== Re-check Article IV/V boundary ===" && grep -n "^## ARTICLE V\." full_draft.md && sed -n '174,178p' full_draft.md | cat -n"}

result:
# DEVELOPMENT AGREEMENT

## LAKEMONT STATION COMMONS MIXED-USE TRANSIT-ORIENTED DEVELOPMENT

**by and between**

**GRANITE CITY DEVELOPMENT LLC** (an Illinois limited liability company)

**and**

**THE CITY OF LAKEMONT, ILLINOIS** (an Illinois home rule municipality)

Dated as of \[•\], 2025

*\[Drafting Note --- Open Item: The target execution date reflected across the source documents is June 2025 (Negotiated Term Sheet §19; Lakeshore Side Letter §1(E)). Execution is contingent on satisfaction of the conditions precedent set forth in Article IX, including completion of the PD-TOD rezoning, IEPA approval of the Remedial Action Plan for Parcel 016, equity demonstration, and Lender/Investor review of this Agreement. The date should be confirmed once those conditions are satisfied.\]*

## RECITALS

**A. The Property.** The City of Lakemont, Illinois (the "City") is the owner in fee simple of certain real property commonly known as 1400--1550 Lakemont Station Boulevard, Lakemont, Illinois 60048, consisting of three (3) separate parcels of record identified by Parcel Identification Numbers 12-24-300-015, 12-24-300-016, and 12-24-300-017, totaling approximately 12.7 acres (553,212 square feet) (collectively, the "Property"). The Property constitutes the former site of the Lakemont Ironworks manufacturing campus, which ceased industrial operations in 2009, and is situated immediately adjacent to the Lakemont Central Metra commuter rail station on the Metra Milwaukee District/North Line.

*\[Drafting Note --- Cross-Document Conflict: The source documents conflict regarding (i) the individual acreage of the parcels and (ii) the City's acquisition history for Parcel 016. As to acreage, the Phase II ESA (Greenfield, Nov. 8, 2024) reports Parcel 015 at \~4.2 acres and Parcel 017 at \~4.4 acres, while the RFP Response Narrative (Dec. 16, 2024) reports Parcel 015 at \~3.8 acres and Parcel 017 at \~4.8 acres; the total of \~12.7 acres is consistent. As to acquisition, the City Council Resolution R-2025-017, the Negotiated Term Sheet, and the TIF District Summary Memo all state that Parcels 015 and 016 were acquired by tax forfeiture in 2016 and Parcel 017 by direct purchase in 2018, whereas the Phase II ESA states that Parcel 015 was acquired by tax forfeiture in 2016 and Parcels 016 and 017 by direct purchase in 2018. The acquisition history affects each parcel's chain of title and any liens or encumbrances arising from the tax-forfeiture versus direct-purchase mechanisms. The City should be required to deliver, as a condition precedent, a current title commitment for each individual parcel and to certify the acquisition history, and the legal descriptions in Exhibit A should be confirmed by survey.\]*

**B. The RFP and Selection of Developer.** The City, through its Community Development Department, conducted a competitive Request for Proposals process for the redevelopment of the Property as a mixed-use transit-oriented development. In response, Granite City Development LLC, an Illinois limited liability company (the "Developer"), submitted a proposal and was selected as the preferred developer based on its qualifications, experience, project proposal, and demonstrated financial capacity.

*\[Drafting Note --- Cross-Document Conflict: The RFP Response Narrative identifies the solicitation as "RFP No. 2024-CD-07," while the Negotiated Term Sheet refers to "RFP No. 2024-CD-018." The correct RFP number should be confirmed with the City's Community Development Department and stated consistently throughout the Agreement and the authorizing resolution.\]*

**C. The Negotiated Term Sheet.** On January 22, 2025, the Developer, by its Manager, Marcus J. Pellegrini, and the City, by its City Manager, Gerald T. Fong, executed a non-binding Negotiated Term Sheet setting forth the principal economic and legal terms proposed to govern this Agreement.

**D. City Council Conditional Approval.** On February 18, 2025, the City Council adopted Resolution No. R-2025-017 (the "Resolution"), granting conditional approval for the Project and authorizing the City Manager to negotiate and execute this Agreement, subject to the conditions set forth in Section 3 of the Resolution.

**E. The Project.** The Developer proposes to develop the Property as a four-phase mixed-use transit-oriented development to be known as "Lakemont Station Commons" (the "Project"), with a total gross building area of approximately 1,620,000 square feet and a total estimated Project cost of approximately Four Hundred Twelve Million Dollars (\$412,000,000), as more particularly described in Article IV.

**F. Public Incentives.** In consideration of the Developer's undertaking to redevelop a vacant, environmentally contaminated former industrial site and to deliver substantial public benefits, the City has agreed to provide certain public incentives, including a land write-down, tax increment financing reimbursement, infrastructure cost-sharing, property tax abatement, and permit fee waivers, as more particularly described in Article VI.

**G. Environmental Conditions.** A Phase II Environmental Site Assessment was completed by Greenfield Environmental Consultants LLC in a report dated November 8, 2024 (the "Phase II ESA"), identifying petroleum hydrocarbon (BTEX) contamination on Parcel 016 and low-level metals contamination in fill material on Parcel 017, as more particularly described in Article VII.

**H. Financing.** The Developer has obtained a preliminary (non-binding) commitment from First Prairie National Bank (the "Construction Lender") for a construction loan facility of up to Two Hundred Ten Million Dollars (\$210,000,000), and has committed equity of Eighty-Five Million Dollars (\$85,000,000) from Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP and the Pellegrini Family Trust, in each case subject to execution of this Agreement in form and substance acceptable to the Construction Lender and the equity investors.

**I. Authority.** The City is a home rule municipality organized under the Constitution and laws of the State of Illinois, including Article VII, Section 6 of the Illinois Constitution of 1970, and is authorized to enter into this Agreement pursuant to its home rule powers and the Illinois Tax Increment Allocation Redevelopment Act, 65 ILCS 5/11-74.4-1 *et seq.* (the "TIF Act").

**NOW, THEREFORE,** in consideration of the mutual covenants and agreements set forth herein, and other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, the City and the Developer (each a "Party" and collectively the "Parties") agree as follows:

## ARTICLE I. DEFINITIONS AND INTERPRETATION

**Section 1.1 Definitions.** In addition to terms defined elsewhere in this Agreement, the following capitalized terms shall have the meanings set forth below:

(a) **"Affordable Unit"** means a residential dwelling unit restricted to occupancy by households earning at or below sixty percent (60%) of Area Median Income, as more particularly described in Article VIII.

(b) **"AMI"** means Area Median Income as published annually by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development for the Chicago-Naperville-Elgin, IL-IN-WI Metropolitan Statistical Area, or any successor designation.

(c) **"Appraised Value"** means the as-is fair market value of the Property of Eighteen Million Four Hundred Thousand Dollars (\$18,400,000), as established in the appraisal report of Crossland Appraisal Services dated January 15, 2025.

(d) **"Change of Control"** has the meaning set forth in Section 12.2.

(e) **"Construction Lender"** means First Prairie National Bank, and any successor construction lender or assignee approved in accordance with the loan documents and this Agreement.

(f) **"Construction Loan Documents"** means the definitive loan agreements, mortgages, assignments, guaranties, and other instruments evidencing and securing the construction loan facility.

(g) **"ELUC"** means an environmental land use control, in the form of a restrictive covenant recorded against the Property in accordance with the IEPA Voluntary Site Remediation Program and 35 Illinois Administrative Code Part 742.

## Deliverable
`output/development-agreement-draft.docx` — a developer-side draft of the **Lakemont Station Commons** Development Agreement between **Granite City Development LLC** (Developer) and the **City of Lakemont, Illinois**, with bracketed drafting notes flagging cross-document conflicts and open items.

## Source documents reviewed
1. City Council Resolution R-2025-017 (conditional approval)
2. Negotiated Term Sheet (Jan. 22, 2025)
3. Construction Loan Term Sheet (First Prairie National Bank)
4. Lakeshore Side Letter (Investor governance)
5. Phase II ESA Summary (Greenfield, Nov. 8, 2024)
6. TIF District Summary Memo (Corporation Counsel)
7. RFP Response Narrative (Dec. 16, 2024)

## Approach
- Drafted from the **Developer's perspective**, with developer-favorable positions on the principal open items, consistent with the Developer's obligations to the Construction Lender and the Investor under the loan term sheet and the Lakeshore Side Letter.
- Embedded **46 bracketed drafting notes** throughout, each labeled "Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict" or "Drafting Note — Open Item," citing the specific source documents and provisions in conflict.
- Concluded with a **consolidated 37-item index** of all conflicts and open items for negotiation reference.

## Structure (18 Articles + Exhibits)
Recitals → I. Definitions → II. Property → III. Rezoning/Consolidation → IV. Project (phasing tables) → V. Land Conveyance & Completion Assurance → VI. Public Incentives (TIF) → VII. Environmental → VIII. Affordable Housing → IX. Conditions Precedent → X. Construction Standards → XI. Performance Security → XII. Assignment/Change of Control/Lender Protections → XIII. Default/Cure/Termination → XIV. Force Majeure → XV. Indemnification/Insurance → XVI. Dispute Resolution → XVII. Representations → XVIII. Miscellaneous → Exhibits A–K.

## Most critical conflicts flagged
1. **Reversionary interest vs. Lender prohibition vs. Investor consent** (Resolution cond. (h) vs. Loan TS §12 vs. Lakeshore §7(d)) — substituted with a contractual repurchase option + liquidated damages + performance security that preserves fee simple absolute title (Art. V, §5.5).
2. **36-month reversion clock vs. Sept. 30, 2028 Substantial Completion deadline** (internally inconsistent).
3. **"Six months from conveyance" commencement trigger vs. March 1, 2026 groundbreaking** (Art. XIII).
4. **Force Majeure cap: 18 months (Term Sheet) vs. 180 days (Loan TS)** (Art. XIV).
5. **Change-of-control threshold: 50% (Term Sheet/Loan) vs. 51% (Lakeshore)** plus future capital-raise dilution risk (Art. XII).
6. **Environmental cost-overrun allocation, NFR vs. RAP conveyance condition, and remediation timeline vs. groundbreaking** (Art. VII).
7. **TIF shortfall risk, land write-down/cap interaction, and City infrastructure funding source** (Art. VI).

## Validation
- Generated via Pandoc (markdown → docx); validated with `skills/docx/scripts/validate.py` — **exit code 0 (valid)**.
- Confirmed: 18 articles as headings, 2 data tables (Project Program, Phasing Schedule), 46 drafting-note paragraphs, all key developer-favorable provisions present."}

text:
The task is complete. The deliverable `development-agreement-draft.docx` has been created and validated.

## Summary

I drafted a **developer-side Development Agreement** for the Lakemont Station Commons project, output to `output/development-agreement-draft.docx` (validated, ~16,600 words, 18 articles + 11 exhibits).

**What I did:**
1. **Read all 7 source documents** — the City Council Resolution, Negotiated Term Sheet, Construction Loan Term Sheet, Lakeshore Side Letter, Phase II ESA, TIF District Memo, and RFP Response Narrative.
2. **Identified 37 cross-document conflicts and open items**, then drafted the agreement from the Developer's (Granite City Development LLC's) perspective, with developer-favorable positions consistent with the Developer's obligations to its Construction Lender (First Prairie National Bank) and Investor (Lakeshore Capital Partners Fund III LP).
3. **Embedded 46 bracketed drafting notes** throughout — each labeled "Drafting Note — Cross-Document Conflict" or "Drafting Note — Open Item," citing the specific source documents and provisions in tension — plus a consolidated 37-item index at the end.

**Most critical conflicts flagged and addressed:**
- **Reversionary interest** (Resolution) vs. **Lender's prohibition** (Loan TS) vs. **Investor consent** (Lakeshore) — substituted with a contractual repurchase option + liquidated damages + performance security that preserves fee simple absolute title (Art. V).
- **36-month reversion clock vs. Sept. 30, 2028 completion deadline**; **"six months from conveyance" trigger vs. March 1, 2026 groundbreaking**; **18-month vs. 180-day force majeure cap**; **50% vs. 51% change-of-control thresholds**; **environmental cost-overrun allocation and NFR-vs-RAP conveyance condition**; **TIF shortfall risk and incentive/cap interactions**.

**Technical note:** I resolved two pandoc conversion issues during generation — a YAML-metadata misparse of `---` separators (disabled the extension) and a missing blank line at the Article IV/V boundary that had turned Article V into a spurious table (normalized separator spacing). The final document passed `validate.py` (exit 0) with all 18 articles, 2 data tables, and all drafting notes intact.
